EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms produced large hail stones west of Granite Falls, near Olivia, and northeast toward the northwest suburbs of the Twin Cities. Most of these storms were elevated but had a steep lapse rate to generate large hail, up to 2 inches in diameter at times. ||These late morning, and early afternoon storms moved northeast out of Minnesota. A warm front lifted northward across southern Minnesota during the afternoon with a potent shortwave moving northeast out of Nebraska. This shortwave generated a large complex of storms and quickly moved northeast across southern Minnesota and produced numerous damaging wind gusts, tornadoes and hail. There were even some flooding concerns as three to five inches of rain fell across portions of west central and central Minnesota by the late evening as the storms diminished and moved off to the northeast.||Three tornadoes occurred within the large bow echo that surged from southwest Minnesota, northeast toward east central Minnesota. One occurred in Brown County, another in Sibley and Carver County, and a third in Anoka County. All of the tornadoes were rated EF0 and produced mainly tree and roof damage.||According to Xcel Energy, more than 75,000 customers were without power across southern Minnesota during the peak of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several trees and power lines blown down in Chanhassen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant hail event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Southeast-Central Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A multicellular convective mode advanced in two waves with the first wave occurring through the morning hours in Southern MN, while the second wave produced a long-track supercell over the Twin Cities metro and Central Wisconsin. Both waves produced significant hail and damaging wind gusts. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon produced countless hailstones across the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. Beyond the typical 1 inch diameter hailstones, there were several hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter that fell over Washington County. A few other large hailstones fell over Le Sueur, Dakota and Goodhue Counties of southern Minnesota.

Two rounds of severe storms moved across southeast Minnesota on April 12th. The first round occurred in the afternoon as storms producing large hail moved across the area. Quarter sized hail fell from north of Racine (Mower County) east to near Winona (Winona County). A second round of severe storms occurred in the evening. An EF2 tornado moved through Taopi (Mower County), where homes were destroyed or severely damaged, grain bins were destroyed, cars were tossed or rolled, and trees and power lines were downed. Two people sustained injuries while sheltering from the storm when they became trapped under debris. An EF1 tornado occurred just southwest of Spring Valley (Fillmore County), damaging several farm outbuildings and trees. Two EF0 tornadoes occurred southwest of Lewiston (Winona County), where the damage was mainly to trees and one farm. The Mower County Board of Commissioners ratified an emergency declaration for Taopi and a state of emergency was declared for Fillmore County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A half dozen campers were tipped over at a campground northeast of Preston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into western Minnesota. This thunderstorm complex generated 2 confirmed tornadoes across Big Stone and Traverse Counties, and produced a broad area of widespread and destructive straight-line winds in excess of 80 mph. Trees, buildings, power lines, grain bins, semi trucks, and other property was damaged or destroyed across west central Minnesota, and power outages lasted for multiple days in some cases.

A deep area of low pressure that was centered across southern North Dakota late Wednesday night, slowly moved eastward across northern Minnesota on Thursday. On the south side of this area of low pressure, a strong pressure gradient created winds in excess of 40-45 mph sustained across a wide area of western and southern Minnesota. Several gusts in the 50 to 55 mph range also occurred with the strongest wind gust of 59 mph at the Redwood Falls Airport Thursday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind sensors across Yellow Medicine County reported sustained winds in excess of 45 to 50 mph, with gusts near 55 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low-pressure system impacted the region during the afternoon and evening hours of May 30th. A larger scale Derecho tracked from far southeastern South Dakota, through much of western through northcentral Minnesota. Widespread damage was reported from damaging winds, and tornadoes. Hail up to 1.5 inches was reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large pine and poplar trees were snapped or uprooted along Highway 72, between Shooks and Kelliher. Time estimated based on radar and nearby wind sensor reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed in far northern Todd County and hit two farmsteads before exiting into Wadena County (see corresponding entry from NWS Grand Forks). At the first farmstead, a garage door was blown in and part of the garage roof came off. Maximum winds for the Todd County portion were estimated to be 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the early morning hours of April 23, 2022 within an area of elevated instability mainly along and east of Interstate 29. A couple thunderstorms produced marginally severe hail and damaging wind gusts.

The evening of Sunday, February 20th, a large Arctic ridge of high pressure built southward across the Upper Midwest. Brisk north/northeasterly surface winds were across the Upper Midwest with an increasing area of (850-700 MB) low-level forcing preceding an upper trough over the northern Rockies. These two elements aided in the development of a narrow band of moderate to heavy snowfall that was first centered along the North Dakota, South Dakota border Sunday evening. Regional radar that evening showed an enhanced area of higher reflectivity that slowly spread eastward across central Minnesota late Sunday night, and into Monday morning before lifting northeast over the arrowhead of Minnesota. ||Snowfall rates of 1  to 2 inches per hour were noted over central Minnesota early Monday morning as modest forcing within the dendritic growth layer supported these type of rates.  By Monday afternoon, most of the snowfall tapered off to flurries or light snow will little additional accumulation until Monday night. ||A secondary wave developed across the central plains late Monday night. It was much |stronger than the previous wave and had more moisture surging northward into the system. Another area of light snow that affected central Minnesota Tuesday morning was broader but less intense. Although additional snow fell during this time period, the heaviest occurred during the first wave late Sunday night, and into Monday morning. ||Total snowfall amounts by Tuesday evening, led to a broad area of 6 to 14 inches of snow that fell from Morris, east-northeast to Sauk Centre, Rice, Princeton, and Rush City northward. Some of the highest totals occurred over Todd County where locally 14.3 inches fell near Bertha.  ||Some of the higher totals include:||Bertha in Todd County 14.3 inches.|South of Mora in Kanabec County 14.0 inches. |Clarissa in Todd County 14.0 inches.|Little Falls in Morrison County 13.0 inches.|North of Flensburg in Morrison County 13.0 inches.|Morris in Stevens County 12.4 inches. |Donnelly in Stevens County 10.5 inches.|West of Mora in Kanabec County 12.1 inches. |East of Motley in Morrison County 11.9 inches. |Long Prairie in Todd County 10.4 inches. |Isle in Mille Lacs County 9.1 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began across Todd County during the early morning of Monday, February 21st. Heavy snow fell during the morning, with up to 7 inches prior to 8 AM, Monday morning. There was a lull in the precipitation Monday afternoon and evening before a second wave of light to moderate snow fell across the county. Total snowfall amounts by time it ended ranged from 8 to 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front. The airmass featured at least 1000 J/kg DCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear parallel to the frontal boundary, which supported upscale growth and transition toward damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of at least 70 mph caused damage to tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across south-central South Dakota in the late afternoon of August 2nd in response to an eastward advancing short-wave aloft. Weak deep-layer shear and skinny CAPE profiles limited the overall severe threat, but a very dry sub-cloud layer allowed for stronger wind gusts aloft to reach the surface across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the evening hours. Observed wind gusts ranged from 60 to 80 mph with occasional reports of trees down and isolated structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 74 mph was measured by Automated Weather Observing System site KMML 1 mile west of Marshall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 65 mph was measured by a hand-held anemometer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-level jet gradually weakened early morning, but sustained a back-building cluster of thunderstorms across southwest Minnesota within an environment of 1500 J/kg MUCAPE and 40 knots of effective shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 66 mph was measured by Automated Weather Observing System site KMWM 2 miles north-northeast of Windom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beneath the anvil area of stronger thunderstorms well to the south, a convective heat burst occurred at Tracy.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Automated Weather Observing System site KTKC at Tracy, late-night temperatures vaulted a quick 10 degrees into the lower 90s, while winds increased briefly to a gusty 52 mph. Large trees were downed with power outages reported in Tracy, and a semi-trailer was also blown over.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple two-inch tree branches were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure across the Northern Plains lifted a warm front across the Northland on Memorial Day morning. Showers and storms moved through during the morning hours with the atmosphere recovering during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms were severe during the morning and produced small hail and tree damage. An arc of thunderstorms developed across southwestern Minnesota and adjacent areas of Nebraska, South Dakota and Iowa by the mid-afternoon hours and moved to the northeast and across the Northland for the late afternoon and evening hours. These storms were much stronger and contained very strong winds that produced widespread damage across the region. Additionally, there were a few embedded tornadoes with the storms with the strongest tornadoes producing EF-1 damage in the Deer River and Hinckley areas.

Showers and storms moved through during the morning hours with the atmosphere recovering during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms were severe during the morning and produced small hail and tree damage. An arc of thunderstorms developed across southwestern Minnesota and adjacent areas of Nebraska, South Dakota and Iowa by the mid-afternoon hours and moved to the northeast and across the Northland for the late afternoon and evening hours. These storms were much stronger and contained very strong winds that produced widespread damage across the region. Additionally, there were a few embedded tornadoes with the storms with the strongest tornadoes producing EF-1 damage in the Deer River and Hinckley areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe D2 Drought conditions began in west central Minnesota during the second half of October. The area saw a prolonged period of below-average rainfall, as much as 5 to 7 inches since August, along with average temperatures one to three degrees above normal. The National Agricultural Statistics Service showed topsoil moisture rated at 80 percent short or very short. The limited soil moisture, high winds, and harvest season sometimes created poor air quality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line advanced across South Central Minnesota during the late evening of Sunday, October 23rd. An isolated strong wind gust of 54 knots was recorded at the Redwood Falls Airport ASOS station just after 0200 UTC. As the line continued northeastward, storms continued to develop, however they remained sub-severe with gusty winds and small hail.

Scattered storms developed across central Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening. Most reports of hail were for 1/2 inch or smaller, but one storm in eastern Morrison County had a report of 3/4 inch hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One storm in eastern Morrison County had a report of 3/4 inch hail as relayed via mPing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic high pressure brought bitterly cold temperatures from the morning of January 5th into the morning hours of January 7th. High temperatures dropped from the single digits and teens above zero on the 5th, the single digits on either side of zero on the 6th. The coldest wind chills of 35 below to 40 degrees below zero occurred during the morning hours on January 7th.

A strong pressure gradient set up along the North Shore of Lake Superior on January 21st between departing high pressure to the southeast and a warm front approaching from the west. This gradient generated strong southwesterly winds that gusted to 50 to 60 mph during the late afternoon hours. A peak gust of 62 mph was recorded at the Bay of Grand Marais just before 5 PM. Despite the strong winds, no reports of damage or power outages were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KGNA at the Bay of Grand Marais recorded a peak gust of 62 mph at 4:41 PM CST. Winds gusted to 50 to 60 mph for about 3 hours during the late afternoon and early evening hours.

A strong Arctic high pressure tracked south-southeast across the area the evening of the 19th into the morning hours of the 20th, bringing bitterly cold temperatures to west central Minnesota. Low temperatures in the single digits below to the teens below zero combined with a northerly wind to produce wind chill values of 35 below to 40 degrees below zero.

Cold high pressure moved into the Northland to begin 2022. Overnight lows the morning of the 1st settled into the 20s to 30s below zero across much of northeastern Minnesota. Light winds through the night led to wind chills falling into the 30s and 40s below zero with a few locations dropping into the 50s below zero. Two stations recorded minimum wind chills of -52F near Cass Lake and Virginia.

The cold air to begin the new year lingered over the Northland for a second night. The area of high pressure was more overhead this night, so temperatures were a bit colder with widespread -30F readings. Winds were lighter than the previous night which helped limit the area of extreme wind chills, but a few readings in the 40s and 50s below zero were recorded with a minimum wind chill report of -52F near Cass Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from the Central Plains to northern Lower Michigan on the 4th into the 5th. This storm track brought an area of heavy snowfall to parts of northeastern Minnesota starting the evening of the 4th before lingering into the afternoon hours of the 5th. Most of the region received under 6 inches of accumulation, but lake-effect snow along the North Shore of Lake Superior at the beginning of the storm contributed to higher snowfall totals in that area of 6 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals were generally in the 2 to 5 inch range across southern Lake County, but a few reports to around 6 inches were received from the Two Harbors area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold frontal system dropped across eastern ND and northwest MN through the afternoon hours of January 18th. Very strong winds speed developed rapidly behind the front, and with abundant fresh and blowable snow, ground blizzard conditions became widespread across the largely exposed terrain of eastern ND and adjacent portions of northwest through westcentral MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 40 to 58 mph produced widespread ground blizzard condition over open country. Fresh and very blowable snow helped to sustain visibilities below 1/4 mile for several hours, with whiteout conditions at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a rapidly moving arctic cold front, an intensely cold arctic airmass settled into the area. As winds of from 10 to 20 mph persisted into the overnight hours of January 18th into the early morning of the 19th, and temperatures dropped from minus 15 to minus 25F, wind chills dropped from minus 35 to minus 50 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight low temperatures dropped from minus 15 to minus 25 F, with winds of 10 to 20 mph producing wind chills from minus 35 to minus 50 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of cold Canadian high pressure settled across the Northland the night of the 9th into the 10th. Morning lows dipped into the 20s and 30s below zero and wind chills in the 30s and 40s below zero were recorded in the hours around sunrise as temperatures bottomed out and winds began to increase.

A strong area of low pressure across the Northern Plains lifted a warm front across the Northland on Memorial Day morning. Showers and storms moved through during the morning hours with the atmosphere recovering during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms were severe during the morning and produced small hail and tree damage. An arc of thunderstorms developed across southwestern Minnesota and adjacent areas of Nebraska, South Dakota and Iowa by the mid-afternoon hours and moved to the northeast and across the Northland for the late afternoon and evening hours. These storms were much stronger and contained very strong winds that produced widespread damage across the region. Additionally, there were a few embedded tornadoes with the storms with the strongest tornadoes producing EF-1 damage in the Deer River and Hinckley areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A boat lift and a dock were flipped on Lower Whitefish Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure moved into the Northland to begin 2022. Overnight lows the morning of the 1st settled into the 20s to 30s below zero across much of northeastern Minnesota. Light winds through the night led to wind chills falling into the 30s and 40s below zero with a few locations dropping into the 50s below zero. Two stations recorded minimum wind chills of -52F near Cass Lake and Virginia.

An area of low pressure moved from the Central Plains to northern Lower Michigan on the 4th into the 5th. This storm track brought an area of heavy snowfall to parts of northeastern Minnesota starting the evening of the 4th before lingering into the afternoon hours of the 5th. Most of the region received under 6 inches of accumulation, but lake-effect snow along the North Shore of Lake Superior at the beginning of the storm contributed to higher snowfall totals in that area of 6 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the snowfall totals in the area were under 6 inches, but a localized area of heavy snowfall northwest of Duluth near Payne led to a few reports just over 6 inches in that area.

The cold high pressure that brought widespread wind chills in the 40s below zero the night of the 25th moved to the east of the region through the day and into the night of the 26th. While temperatures were a bit warmer, a tighter pressure gradient was in place ahead of an area of low pressure moving into Manitoba. The winds led to wind chill readings again in the 40s below zero, but were not as widespread as the night before. The coldest reading was -48F near Cotton.

A strong area of low pressure over the Upper Midwest Tuesday evening, January 4th, continued to deepen and move off to the east. The strong pressure gradient in the wake of this system, produced sustained winds of 20 to 35 mph, with gusts of 40 to 45 mph across portions of west central Minnesota Tuesday evening, through Wednesday afternoon. Although the worst conditions occurred when during the overnight hours, gusty winds that continued on Wednesday caused occasional white-out conditions over the region. Slowly, winds diminished and visibilities improved in the afternoon as the area of low pressure moved off to the east. The worst conditions were across Stevens and western Swift Counties where deeper snow cover caused more blowing snow and reduced visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surface observations across Stevens County indicated wind speeds around 30 mph sustained, with gusts near 45 mph during the early morning of Wednesday, January 5th. These conditions led to whiteout conditions for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across much of northeastern Minnesota during the month of January with overall little change observed. The worst areas were across parts of Koochiching and northwestern Itasca counties, along with portions of Lake and Cook counties where areas of D2 Severe Drought persisted. Precipitation overall was generally near to below normal, but slight improvement was observed over southern Lake County where deep snowpack was in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the county was in the D2 Severe Drought category with a sliver of D1 Moderate Drought across the far eastern portion of the county. Generally near normal precipitation was received over the course of the month and no change in the drought status was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across much of northeastern Minnesota during the month of January with overall little change observed. The worst areas were across parts of Koochiching and northwestern Itasca counties, along with portions of Lake and Cook counties where areas of D2 Severe Drought persisted. Precipitation overall was generally near to below normal, but slight improvement was observed over southern Lake County where deep snowpack was in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much of northern Lake and Cook counties remained under D2 Severe Drought throughout January with just far western Lake County being in D1 Moderate Drought. Precipitation for the month was within a few tenths of an inch of normal with portions of eastern Cook County receiving up to a half of an inch of precipitation above normal. Precipitation at the end of December led to the trimming of the area of D2 Severe Drought across southeastern Lake County with the update on the 11th, but no further changes were observed over the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pine trees were uprooted between Maple Lake and Monticello.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado entered Douglas County from Pope County, just east of Lake Reno. It immediately demolished a garage and broke many hundreds of trees before hitting an area of homes along the east side of Maple Lake in the city of Forada. Dozens of homes had roofs torn off, garages and sheds destroyed. It threw a car several hundred feet. The tornado tore part of the roof off from the fire station. It continued beyond Forada and hit numerous farmsteads before hitting the west side of the city of Nelson, where a farm lost most outbuildings and a home lost its roof. The tornado continued moving northeast, hitting more farms, before dissipating several miles east of Carlos. Maximum winds were estimated to be 120 mph.

Most of the hail fell across the central third of Minnesota, with some hail stones measured at 2 inches in diameter. In addition, a few mesonet wind sensors and the St. Cloud regional airport all reported severe wind gusts. There was also wind damage near Cold Spring and in central Morrison County where power outages occurred.

A warm front lifted into the region during the morning hours of the 11th bringing unseasonably warm temperatures into the Northland for the afternoon hours. The associated cold front arrived for the evening and overnight hours and led to the development of showers and thunderstorms. While most remained below severe levels, one storm that moved across northern St. Louis County did produce hail to the size of golf balls.

Drought conditions across northeastern Minnesota during the month of October saw an expansion of D0 Abnormally Dry at the beginning of the month across a large swath of the region. Additionally, a small area of D2 Severe Drought was introduced with the update on the 4th into far southeastern Carlton County. Overall, precipitation was only about 25-50% of normal for the month, but no significant expansion of D2 Severe Drought was observed across the region with little change overall in drought conditions over the remainder of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of D1 Moderate Drought was in place at the beginning of the month in the southeastern corner of Carlton County. With the update on the 4th, a small area of D2 Severe Drought was introduced in the far southeastern corner of the county. Little change in drought status was observed over the remainder of the month despite the area receiving only about 25 percent of normal rainfall for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions led to D2 drought in portions of 3 Minnesota counties throughout the month of November.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Wilkin county in D2 drought November 1st - November 30th, 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across most of northeastern Minnesota through the month of February. Precipitation was near to above normal over most of the region for the month as numerous large systems and clippers impacted the region. Some minor improvement in the drought status was observed during the middle of the month, but overall little change was seen. The worst spots for drought conditions continued to be across portions of Lake and Cook counties along with much of Koochiching County into adjacent portions of Itasca County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the county was in the D2 Severe Drought category with a sliver of D1 Moderate Drought across the far eastern portion of the county. Generally near normal to slightly above normal precipitation was received over the course of the month, but no change in the drought status was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of February 18th. Behind this front, northwest winds with gusts of 50 to 60 mph buffeted the area into the early evening. The automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester (Olmsted County) airport measured a gust of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph buffeted Dodge County during the late afternoon and early evening. The highest recorded gust was 55 mph by a mesonet station near Kasson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snowfall event occurred across northeastern Minnesota beginning the morning of the 21st and lasting into the late evening hours of the 22nd and early morning hours of the 23rd. A strong area of low pressure moved from the Central Plains into the Mid-Mississippi Valley during this time period leading to multiple waves of snow that affected the Northland. Most of the region received at least 6 inches of accumulation by the time the storm ended with many areas receiving upwards of 1 foot of accumulation by the time snow ended. The highest total came from near Isle where 19.8 inches piled up in a little over 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 to 11 inches were received from Pine County with a high report of 11.2 inches near Sturgeon Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong cold air advection brought gusty winds and very cold temperatures to the region from late Feb 1st into much of Feb 2nd. Wind chill values fell into the range of 30 below zero to 50 below zero across much of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures fell as low as 20 below zero, with winds ranging from 10 to 20 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extreme cold event occurred in conjunction with a series of system that produced heavy snow and persistent winds. Temperatures failed to break the zero degree mark by the evening of the February 22nd and fell to between 15 and 20 degrees below zero by the morning of February 23rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph buffeted Winona County during the late afternoon and evening. The highest recorded wind gust was 51 mph by a mesonet station near Ridgeway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mixture of freezing rain, sleet and snow fell across portions of southeast Minnesota from the evening of February 21st into the evening of the 22nd. This left roads across the region snow and ice covered. A two vehicle accident in Winona (Winona County) was fatal when the driver lost control and slammed into the rear of a parked semi.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a two vehicle accident in Winona. The driver lost control on snow and ice covered roads and slammed into the rear of a parked semi. A mixture of freezing rain, sleet and snow occurred from the evening of February 21st into the evening of the 22nd and was responsible for creating the hazard driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of moderate to heavy snow developed within a very cold airmass, migrating into western Minnesota during the evening of February 20th and persisting into the morning of February 21st. This was part of a broader arctic blast that also featured dangerous cold and wind chills as well as an additional round of snow with strong winds and intermittent blizzard conditions. Peak snow amounts with this initial band ranged between 7 inches in Browns Valley and 6 inches in Beardsley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a band of moderate to heavy snow moved through on February 20th into the 21st, periodic snowfall persisted across the area for much of February 21st and 22nd. Winds increased with peak gusts of 35 mph to 45 mph across western Minnesota. Two to three day snow totals topped out at 12 inches in Wheaton and 14.5 inches in Browns Valley. The fresh snow and persistent winds result in widespread near blizzard to blizzard conditions for western Minnesota.

Significant impacts to travel were observed in response to a blizzard that impacted much of west-central and northwest Minnesota. Strong winds lofted snow, creating very low visibility across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions prevailed through the late morning and into much of the afternoon, with visibility down to 1/4 mile and winds frequently gusting over 35 mph.

Low temperatures dropped into the 20s and 30s below zero in parts of northeastern Minnesota with wind chills falling into the 40s and 50s below zero. The coldest wind chill recorded was -52F near Birchdale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system began to strengthen late Monday night across the central plains in advance of a powerful southern jet stream that intensified over the central part of the country on Tuesday. The center of this storm moved from northern Missouri, northeast across the Midwest and Great Lakes region by late Tuesday night. ||A large area of light to moderate precipitation developed across southern and central Minnesota early Tuesday morning and moved northeast while heavier precipitation started to develop across south central, and into east central Minnesota, and adjacent west-central Wisconsin Tuesday morning. This secondary area of higher reflectivity was in response to the deepening southern jet and associated upper level forcing. This led to moderate, to occasional heavy snowfall across parts of south central, southeast, and east-central Minnesota. A band of 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the southeastern Twin Cities metro area.||Some of the higher snowfall totals across the east-southeastern Twin Cities metro area include:||8.8 inches near Afton. |7.0 inches in Hastings. |6.5 inches in Rosemount.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began early Tuesday morning, February 22nd. However, the bulk of the heavier snowfall, higher snowfall rates, and possibly mixed with sleet at times, occurred during the late morning and early afternoon hours. By the time the light snow tapered off, locally 5 to 9 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The evening of Sunday, February 20th, a large Arctic ridge of high pressure built southward across the Upper Midwest. Brisk north/northeasterly surface winds were across the Upper Midwest with an increasing area of (850-700 MB) low-level forcing preceding an upper trough over the northern Rockies. These two elements aided in the development of a narrow band of moderate to heavy snowfall that was first centered along the North Dakota, South Dakota border Sunday evening. Regional radar that evening showed an enhanced area of higher reflectivity that slowly spread eastward across central Minnesota late Sunday night, and into Monday morning before lifting northeast over the arrowhead of Minnesota. ||Snowfall rates of 1  to 2 inches per hour were noted over central Minnesota early Monday morning as modest forcing within the dendritic growth layer supported these type of rates.  By Monday afternoon, most of the snowfall tapered off to flurries or light snow will little additional accumulation until Monday night. ||A secondary wave developed across the central plains late Monday night. It was much |stronger than the previous wave and had more moisture surging northward into the system. Another area of light snow that affected central Minnesota Tuesday morning was broader but less intense. Although additional snow fell during this time period, the heaviest occurred during the first wave late Sunday night, and into Monday morning. ||Total snowfall amounts by Tuesday evening, led to a broad area of 6 to 14 inches of snow that fell from Morris, east-northeast to Sauk Centre, Rice, Princeton, and Rush City northward. Some of the highest totals occurred over Todd County where locally 14.3 inches fell near Bertha.  ||Some of the higher totals include:||Bertha in Todd County 14.3 inches.|South of Mora in Kanabec County 14.0 inches. |Clarissa in Todd County 14.0 inches.|Little Falls in Morrison County 13.0 inches.|North of Flensburg in Morrison County 13.0 inches.|Morris in Stevens County 12.4 inches. |Donnelly in Stevens County 10.5 inches.|West of Mora in Kanabec County 12.1 inches. |East of Motley in Morrison County 11.9 inches. |Long Prairie in Todd County 10.4 inches. |Isle in Mille Lacs County 9.1 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began across Stevens County shortly after midnight, Monday, February 21st. However, the bulk of the snowfall occur along the Stevens, Grant County line where locally 2-4 inches fell before noon Monday. Additional snow fell early Tuesday, and snowfall totals by the time the snow tapered off Tuesday evening ranged from 6 to 12 inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southward pushing cold air near the surface interacted with a post-frontal wave in southwest flow aloft to produce a prolonged period of light snowfall, which began as a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle across far southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and early evening of February 21. In addition to snowfall which accumulated from as much as 1 to 2 inches, strong northerly winds produced dangerous wind chills from 25 below to 35 below zero from later on February 21 through the afternoon of February 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While snowfall was not significant by amount with 2.0 inches 8 miles southeast of Hendricks, when combined with winds which gusted to 40 mph near Lake Benton and 39 mph 5 miles south of Hendricks, visibility was one mile or less and frequently one-half mile or less between 0700-1600CST February 22. In addition, wind chill was as low as -30F to -35F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A re-intensifying surface pressure gradient produced winds gusting at 35 to 42 mph and widespread ground blizzard conditions across far southeastern ND and adjacent portions of westcentral MN, through the morning and afternoon of February 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 35 to 42 mph, along with blowing and drifting snow, produced widespread ground blizzard conditions.. with visibilities frequently below one quarter mile as reported by MN DOT RWIS wind sensors and observed by area law enforcement and DOT personnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold Arctic Air overspread the Northern Plains Area, accompanied by periods of moderate to strong northerly winds.  Wind chill temperatures ranged from minus 30F to minus 50F from late on the 22nd into the morning of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 15 to 30 mph, in combination to air temperatures from zero to minus 25F, resulted in prolonged periods of wind chills from minus 30F to minus 50F.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of freezing drizzle produced a thin glaze of ice on surfaces during the early evening of February 21. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of freezing drizzle produced a thin glaze of ice on surfaces during the late afternoon and early evening of February 21. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front moved across central Minnesota during the late morning of Friday, February 18th. Some snow fell behind the frontal passage leading to some accumulation. However, the combination of residual snowpack, light snowfall accumulation, strong cold air advection, and deep mixing in the boundary layer, led to surface winds of 25 to 40 mph sustained across central Minnesota. These wind speeds, and occasional gusts over 50 mph, caused whiteout conditions across portions of central Minnesota. These conditions were worst where the snowpack was greater and able to lift any snow aloft. Farther to the south and southeast, snowpack was limited and visibilities were not as low but wind speeds kept blowing snow for hours.||In addition to the blizzard conditions, strong winds were measured between 50 and 56 mph across a large area of central and southern Minnesota. Some of the winds were measured at or above 58 mph. These stronger winds included; Hanley Falls (MNDOT RWIS), KMSP (ASOS), and KSTP (ASOS).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather observations across Douglas County indicated blizzard conditions for several hours Friday afternoon as winds gusted over 45 mph. Visibilities were less than one-quarter of a mile at the Alexandria airport, especially during times where winds were sustained in the 30 to 35 mph range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow fell across southeast Minnesota from the afternoon of February 24th into the morning of the 25th. This left roads snow covered across the region. A multi-vehicle accident with several injuries occurred during the morning of the 25th in Rochester (Olmsted County). A vehicle lost control, slid into the path of school bus and then collided with another vehicle. Seven children on the bus sustain injuries along with three people in the vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of ten people were injured in a three vehicle accident in Rochester. The first vehicle lost control on a snow covered road, slid into the path of a school bus and then collided with another vehicle. Seven children on the bus were injured in the accident. A light snow event from the afternoon of February 24th into the morning of the 25th caused the roads to be snow covered and slippery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep area of low pressure that was centered across southern North Dakota late Wednesday night, slowly moved eastward across northern Minnesota on Thursday. On the south side of this area of low pressure, a strong pressure gradient created winds in excess of 40-45 mph sustained across a wide area of western and southern Minnesota. Several gusts in the 50 to 55 mph range also occurred with the strongest wind gust of 59 mph at the Redwood Falls Airport Thursday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind sensors across Douglas County reported sustained winds in excess of 40 to 45 mph, with gusts near 50 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southward pushing cold air near the surface interacted with a post-frontal wave in southwest flow aloft to produce a prolonged period of light snowfall, which began as a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle across far southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and early evening of February 21. In addition to snowfall which accumulated from as much as 1 to 2 inches, strong northerly winds produced dangerous wind chills from 25 below to 35 below zero from later on February 21 through the afternoon of February 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of freezing drizzle produced a thin glaze of ice on surfaces during the early evening of February 21. Precipitation changed to light snow, and while amounts were not significant with 2.5 inches near Lakefield, when combined with winds which gusted as high as 40 mph at Jackson and 35 mph near Lakefield, visibility was around one mile and occasionally one-half mile or less between 0600-2100CST February 22. In addition, wind chill was as low as -25F to -30F.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall totals were reported across the area, with amounts ranging from 7 inches to 14 inches. Blowing snow reduced visibility at times.

A strong clipper system moved across the Northland the night of the 4th into the 5th spreading snow from west to east across the area. Robust southerly flow out ahead of the approaching low helped to enhance snowfall totals along the North Shore. Most locations received 2-5 inches of snow but a few locally higher reports of 6 inches were observed with a high report of 7.8 inches near Silver Bay along the North Shore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 1 to 3 inches were common across the county with locally higher reports of 6 inches near Lutsen.

A favorable jet stream set up with anomalously high moisture combined to boost snow totals into the 2 to 5 inch range across northern Minneosta. Locally higher amounts to around 6 inches were received along the North Shore in southern Cook County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 3 to 5 inches were common across the county with locally higher reports of 6 inches near Hovland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snowfall event occurred across northeastern Minnesota beginning the morning of the 21st and lasting into the late evening hours of the 22nd and early morning hours of the 23rd. A strong area of low pressure moved from the Central Plains into the Mid-Mississippi Valley during this time period leading to multiple waves of snow that affected the Northland. Most of the region received at least 6 inches of accumulation by the time the storm ended with many areas receiving upwards of 1 foot of accumulation by the time snow ended. The highest total came from near Isle where 19.8 inches piled up in a little over 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 3 to 6 inches were common across the central portion of St. Louis County with a high report of 7.8 inches near Payne.

A strong cold front pushed across the Northland during the midday and early afternoon hours of the 18th. Heavy snow showers developed along the front and produced wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph leading to snow squalls in spots that reduced visibilities to under a quarter of a mile at times. The snow squalls led a wreck involving 23 cars north of Brainerd that resulted in multiple injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph along with fresh snow and falling snow led to a snow squall along Minnesota Highway 371 in western Crow Wing County around noon. The squall moved off Gull Lake and over the roadway leading to near zero visibility. This sudden change in conditions resulted in a chain reaction wreck that involved 23 vehicles, including three semi-trailers. Three people were taken to a local hospital due to injuries sustained in the wreck. The road remained closed for much of the afternoon while cleanup was taking place.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged snowfall event occurred across northeastern Minnesota beginning the morning of the 21st and lasting into the late evening hours of the 22nd and early morning hours of the 23rd. A strong area of low pressure moved from the Central Plains into the Mid-Mississippi Valley during this time period leading to multiple waves of snow that affected the Northland. Most of the region received at least 6 inches of accumulation by the time the storm ended with many areas receiving upwards of 1 foot of accumulation by the time snow ended. The highest total came from near Isle where 19.8 inches piled up in a little over 24 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 6 to 9 inches were received across the southern part of Itasca County with a high report of 9.1 inches near Coleraine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska.  With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds.  With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours.  These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 72 mph was measured by Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN005 1 mile west-southwest Beaver Creek. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed across the Northland during the midday and early afternoon hours of the 18th. Heavy snow showers developed along the front and produced wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph leading to snow squalls in spots that reduced visibilities to under a quarter of a mile at times. The snow squalls led a wreck involving 23 cars north of Brainerd that resulted in multiple injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph along with heavy snowfall moved from west to east across the southern reaches of southern St. Louis County and northern Carlton County during the early afternoon hours. Visibilities rapidly dropped to a quarter of a mile or less with rapid snow accumulations on roadways per webcams in the area. Travel was especially difficult along I-35 from Cloquet to Duluth as the snow squall passed through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system began to strengthen late Monday night across the central plains in advance of a powerful southern jet stream that intensified over the central part of the country on Tuesday. The center of this storm moved from northern Missouri, northeast across the Midwest and Great Lakes region by late Tuesday night. ||A large area of light to moderate precipitation developed across southern and central Minnesota early Tuesday morning and moved northeast while heavier precipitation started to develop across south central, and into east central Minnesota, and adjacent west-central Wisconsin Tuesday morning. This secondary area of higher reflectivity was in response to the deepening southern jet and associated upper level forcing. This led to moderate, to occasional heavy snowfall across parts of south central, southeast, and east-central Minnesota. A band of 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the southeastern Twin Cities metro area.||Some of the higher snowfall totals across the east-southeastern Twin Cities metro area include:||8.8 inches near Afton. |7.0 inches in Hastings. |6.5 inches in Rosemount.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began early Tuesday morning, February 22nd. However, the bulk of the heavier snowfall, higher snowfall rates, and possibly mixed with sleet at times, occurred during the late morning and early afternoon hours. By the time the light snow tapered off, locally 5 to 7 inches fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass moved overhead across the Upper Midwest during the early morning of Friday, January 7th. The actual temperature dropped into the teens below to near -30F by morning. Although the surface ridge moved overhead, initially before winds became nearly calm, wind chill values dropped into the 35 to 45 below zero range. During the morning, winds shifted to the south as the surface ridge moved off to the east and allowed for wind chill values to once again drop below -35F before noon. ||Some of the lowest wind chill values during this time frame:||-46F at Glenwood. |-45F at Long Prairie. |-43F at Paynesville.|-43F at Morris.|-41F at Alexandria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surface observations, and in combination of airport ASOS/AWOS sites, indicated wind chill values dropped below -35F during the afternoon of Thursday, January 6th. Wind chill values didn't recover above -35F until noon the next day. However, there was a period during the overnight where winds dropped to nearly calm but temperatures fell to -30F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of Arctic high pressure built across the Upper Midwest from Monday, January 24, through Tuesday, January 25th. The center of the high moved across the region Tuesday, with return flow developing Tuesday night and Wednesday the 26th. There were two periods where the wind chill values fell below -35F for several hours across portions of central, west central, southwest, and south central Minnesota. The first occurred with the initial ridge building across the region Monday, and Monday night. The second occurred with winds strengthen on the return flow Tuesday night, and into Wednesday. The worst and longest time frame of wind chill values between -35F to -42F were felt Tuesday night, and early Wednesday as return flow strengthen and temperatures were still near -20F. ||A few of the coldest wind chills during this time frame:||-42F at Morris. |-41F at Fairmont. |-40F at Benson. |-40F at Sauk Centre.|-40F at Mankato.|-40F at New Ulm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the Arctic airmass shifted to the east, return flow developed and dropped wind chill values once again below -35F for several hours from late Tuesday evening, through Wednesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass persisted across the Northern Plains from the morning of the 1st through the morning of the 2nd, having started in late December the previous calendar year (see the December 2021 Storm Data entry for additional details). Under dominant high pressure, very cold wind chills persisted during this period across west central Minnesota, with only slight improvements in conditions during the daytime on the 1st. Some of the coldest recorded wind chill readings include -40 degrees F 2 miles NNW of Big Stone Colony and -38 F 3 miles WSW of Wheaton.

With cold air entrenched across the area, a mid-level wave dug southward through the Plains. A swath of snowfall and some light freezing rain developed in the leading warm advection zone northeast of the track of low pressure from South Dakota to Missouri. The highest snowfall of 5 to 11 inches was northeast of a line from De Smet, SD to Ida Grove, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was moderate to heavy at times between 1000-1300CST, and produced dangerous travel conditions. Snowfall totaled 5.5 inches at Pipestone and 5.3 inches at Edgerton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a blizzard producing storm system moved out of the area through the morning of January 5th, an Arctic front and associated Arctic airmass pushed in behind. The coldest air spread across portions of central and eastern ND for the first evening into the morning of January 6th, and then spread eastward across all of eastern ND and much pf northwest through westcentral MN on the evening of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures plummeted well below zero, ranging from minus 25 to minus 35 degrees Fahrenheit, with wind chills of from minus 35 to minus 50F, throughout the overnight hours of the 5th through the morning of the 6th, and again from the late afternoon of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage was noted near Holloway. Although winds were estimated at 60 mph, the Benson airport which is 10 miles east of this site had a measured wind gust of 54 mph at 6:56 PM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms that developed over South Dakota early Thursday morning, moved across west-central, and central Minnesota during the morning. Most of the hail fell across the central third of Minnesota, with some hail stones measured at 2 inches in diameter. In addition, a few mesonet wind sensors and the St. Cloud regional airport all reported severe wind gusts. There was also wind damage near Cold Spring and in central Morrison County where power outages occurred.

Following a rapidly moving arctic cold front, an intensely cold arctic airmass settled into the area. As winds of from 10 to 20 mph persisted into the overnight hours of January 18th into the early morning of the 19th, and temperatures dropped from minus 15 to minus 25F, wind chills dropped from minus 35 to minus 50 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight low temperatures dropped from minus 15 to minus 25 F, with winds of 10 to 20 mph producing wind chills from minus 35 to minus 50 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass moved overhead across the Upper Midwest during the early morning of Friday, January 7th. The actual temperature dropped into the teens below to near -30F by morning. Although the surface ridge moved overhead, initially before winds became nearly calm, wind chill values dropped into the 35 to 45 below zero range. During the morning, winds shifted to the south as the surface ridge moved off to the east and allowed for wind chill values to once again drop below -35F before noon. ||Some of the lowest wind chill values during this time frame:||-46F at Glenwood. |-45F at Long Prairie. |-43F at Paynesville.|-43F at Morris.|-41F at Alexandria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surface observations, in combination of ASOS/AWOS sites, indicated that wind chill values dropped to -35F during the afternoon of Thursday, January 6th. Wind speeds diminished significantly by late evening and become calm overnight. Thus, wind chill values were non-existent after midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of Arctic high pressure built across the Upper Midwest from Monday, January 24, through Tuesday, January 25th. The center of the high moved across the region Tuesday, with return flow developing Tuesday night and Wednesday the 26th. There were two periods where the wind chill values fell below -35F for several hours across portions of central, west central, southwest, and south central Minnesota. The first occurred with the initial ridge building across the region Monday, and Monday night. The second occurred with winds strengthen on the return flow Tuesday night, and into Wednesday. The worst and longest time frame of wind chill values between -35F to -42F were felt Tuesday night, and early Wednesday as return flow strengthen and temperatures were still near -20F. ||A few of the coldest wind chills during this time frame:||-42F at Morris. |-41F at Fairmont. |-40F at Benson. |-40F at Sauk Centre.|-40F at Mankato.|-40F at New Ulm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the Arctic airmass shifted to the east, return flow developed and dropped wind chill values once again below -35F for several hours from late Tuesday evening, through Wednesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a blizzard producing storm system moved out of the area through the morning of January 5th, an Arctic front and associated Arctic airmass pushed in behind. The coldest air spread across portions of central and eastern ND for the first evening into the morning of January 6th, and then spread eastward across all of eastern ND and much pf northwest through westcentral MN on the evening of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures plummeted well below zero, ranging from minus 25 to minus 35 degrees Fahrenheit, with wind chills of from minus 35 to minus 50F, throughout the overnight hours of the 5th through the morning of the 6th, and again from the late afternoon of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of Thursday, May 12th, was a classic bow echo that surged north and east across western Minnesota. ||Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dew points, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area.||Winds were measured by wind sensors at over 100 mph across portions of South Dakota, with a measured wind gust at Madison, Minnesota of 94 mph. There were also numerous measured wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph across west central and southwest Minnesota with embedded surges that produced a few tornadoes. The most concentrated area of damage based on reports of farm equipment, trailers, semis, trees, power lines, and other debris, occurred near the upper Minnesota River Valley, northward to Alexandria and Long Prairie.||Over Stevens County alone, structural damage was considerable as the majority of the damage was to farm outbuildings, sheds and grain bins. There were a few locations in the county where the main inhabited house or home structure did suffer loss of roof and siding and windows but those were more isolated in nature. Many locations had debris tossed a mile northeast from its parent location. Nearly 100% of the damage witnessed in Stevens county was consistent with a straight line wind event, with just one tornado crossing the southeast corner of the county.  ||Ten tornadoes were confirmed this day, with two in Lac Qui Parle County, including a house in Bellingham that had much of its roof removed.  In northwestern Swift County, a tornado tipped over trailers and various farm equipment was heavily damaged.  Two tornadoes were in Todd County, including one that moved through Clarissa.  Two more were in Morrison County, and one was on the east side of Lake Alexander, where many trees came down on vehicles, sheds, and cabins. A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed in far northwest Todd County and moved into Wadena County (see separate entry from NWS Grand Forks). On the Todd County side of the county line, the tornado hit a farmstead and destroyed two tin sheds, leading to estimated maximum winds in Todd County of 90 mph. The debris was strewn in a narrow path. The tornado also broke or uprooted a number of trees. Damage assessment was aided by photographs (not included here) provided by the Todd County Sheriff's Office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic surface ridge moved southward behind the light snowfall and freezing rain on February 21-22. Northerly winds which gusted at times to 35 to 50 mph gradually diminished as temperatures dropped to zero to 10 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -34F near Lakefield (Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN007) and -33F at Jackson (site KMJQ).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow with over 1 inch per hour rates exceeded 6 to 12 inches in total over portions of southeast North Dakota into southern portions of northwest through west-central Minnesota, heaviest snow nearest the South Dakota border.  Very storm winds and cold temperatures also produced two separate but concurrent (to the snowfall) periods of blizzard conditions and extremely cold windchills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 8 to 14 inches of snow was reported across most of the county, along with periods of blizzard conditions as winds frequently gusted to 40 mph. Bitter cold wind chills of from minus 30F to minus 50F through the morning hours of the 21st and 22nd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 8 to 14 inches of snow was reported across most of the county, along with periods of significantly reduced visibilities in snow and blowing snow as winds frequently gusted to 40 mph. Bitter cold wind chills of from minus 30F to minus 50F through the morning hours of the 21st and 22nd.

Bitter cold wind chills of from minus 30F to minus 50F through the morning hours of the 21st and 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong clipper impacted the region on February 11th, leading to widespread blizzard conditions including gusty winds, blowing snow, and near-zero visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility well below 0.25 mile with winds sustained at or above 35 mph. Numerous highways were shut down in MN.

Several rounds of severe thunderstorms traversed the region starting on the evening of April 22nd. Initially, large hail was the primary concern, with an increasingly tornadic environment heading into April 23rd. Both hail (up to 1.5 inches) and tornadoes were reported with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: We received a video of a tornado tracking for less than one-half mile across wet and open fields. The tornado formed south of MN Hwy 102, about 8 miles southeast of Crookston, MN. The tornado lifted after less than one minute; however, the funnel cloud remained visible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The evening of Sunday, February 20th, a large Arctic ridge of high pressure built southward across the Upper Midwest. Brisk north/northeasterly surface winds were across the Upper Midwest with an increasing area of (850-700 MB) low-level forcing preceding an upper trough over the northern Rockies. These two elements aided in the development of a narrow band of moderate to heavy snowfall that was first centered along the North Dakota, South Dakota border Sunday evening. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass moved overhead across the Upper Midwest during the early morning of Friday, January 7th. The actual temperature dropped into the teens below to near -30F by morning. Although the surface ridge moved overhead, initially before winds became nearly calm, wind chill values dropped into the 35 to 45 below zero range. During the morning, winds shifted to the south as the surface ridge moved off to the east and allowed for wind chill values to once again drop below -35F before noon. ||Some of the lowest wind chill values during this time frame:||-46F at Glenwood. |-45F at Long Prairie. |-43F at Paynesville.|-43F at Morris.|-41F at Alexandria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surface observations, in combination of AWOS/ASOS sites, indicated that wind chill values dropped to -35F for several hours between the late afternoon, through mid evening of January 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm was associated with a mid-level trough, along with a surface inverted trough centered across the eastern Dakotas. Most of the energy with this system was mid-level warm air advection across west central, southwest and south central Minnesota between Friday morning, through early Friday evening, January 14th. Light snow started Friday morning across western Minnesota and quickly moved southeast. Bands of moderate snowfall produced locally 1 inch per hour snowfall rates. The storm prediction center (SPC) highlighted west-central, southwest and south central Minnesota 3 times Friday with snowfall rates increasing. A broad area of light snow slowly expanded in areal coverage during the morning and spread as far east as east-central and southeast Minnesota. The heaviest snowfall occurred in a band from Madison, Minnesota, southeast toward Fairmont. Within this band, snowfall amounts ranged from 6-10 inches, with the heaviest along the Buffalo Ridge. The snowfall tapered off during the afternoon and evening from northwest to southeast as the storm moved out of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources across the county indicated that between 7 and 9 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of Arctic high pressure built across the Upper Midwest from Monday, January 24, through Tuesday, January 25th. The center of the high moved across the region Tuesday, with return flow developing Tuesday night and Wednesday the 26th. There were two periods where the wind chill values fell below -35F for several hours across portions of central, west central, southwest, and south central Minnesota. The first occurred with the initial ridge building across the region Monday, and Monday night. The second occurred with winds strengthen on the return flow Tuesday night, and into Wednesday. The worst and longest time frame of wind chill values between -35F to -42F were felt Tuesday night, and early Wednesday as return flow strengthen and temperatures were still near -20F. ||A few of the coldest wind chills during this time frame:||-42F at Morris. |-41F at Fairmont. |-40F at Benson. |-40F at Sauk Centre.|-40F at Mankato.|-40F at New Ulm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the Arctic airmass shifted to the east, return flow developed and dropped wind chill values below -35F for several hours Wednesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a blizzard producing storm system moved out of the area through the morning of January 5th, an Arctic front and associated Arctic airmass pushed in behind. The coldest air spread across portions of central and eastern ND for the first evening into the morning of January 6th, and then spread eastward across all of eastern ND and much pf northwest through westcentral MN on the evening of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures plummeted well below zero, ranging from minus 25 to minus 35 degrees Fahrenheit, with wind chills of from minus 35 to minus 50F, throughout the overnight hours of the 5th through the morning of the 6th, and again from the late afternoon of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow with over 1 inch per hour rates exceeded 6 to 12 inches in total over portions of southeast North Dakota into southern portions of northwest through west-central Minnesota, heaviest snow nearest the South Dakota border.  Very storm winds and cold temperatures also produced two separate but concurrent (to the snowfall) periods of blizzard conditions and extremely cold windchills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From 6 to 10 inches of snow was reported across most of the county, along with periods of significantly reduced visibilities in snow and blowing snow as winds frequently gusted to 35 mph. Bitter cold wind chills of from minus 30F to minus 50F through the morning hours of the 21st and 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of Arctic high pressure built across the Upper Midwest from Monday, January 24, through Tuesday, January 25th. The center of the high moved across the region Tuesday, with return flow developing Tuesday night and Wednesday the 26th. There were two periods where the wind chill values fell below -35F for several hours across portions of central, west central, southwest, and south central Minnesota. The first occurred with the initial ridge building across the region Monday, and Monday night. The second occurred with winds strengthen on the return flow Tuesday night, and into Wednesday. The worst and longest time frame of wind chill values between -35F to -42F were felt Tuesday night, and early Wednesday as return flow strengthen and temperatures were still near -20F. ||A few of the coldest wind chills during this time frame:||-42F at Morris. |-41F at Fairmont. |-40F at Benson. |-40F at Sauk Centre.|-40F at Mankato.|-40F at New Ulm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the Arctic airmass shifted to the east, return flow developed and dropped wind chill values below -35F for several hours Wednesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms that developed over South Dakota early Thursday morning, moved across west-central, and central Minnesota during the morning. Most of the hail fell across the central third of Minnesota, with some hail stones measured at 2 inches in diameter. In addition, a few mesonet wind sensors and the St. Cloud regional airport all reported severe wind gusts. There was also wind damage near Cold Spring and in central Morrison County where power outages occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm was associated with a mid-level trough, along with a surface inverted trough centered across the eastern Dakotas. Most of the energy with this system was mid-level warm air advection across west central, southwest and south central Minnesota between Friday morning, through early Friday evening, January 14th. Light snow started Friday morning across western Minnesota and quickly moved southeast. Bands of moderate snowfall produced locally 1 inch per hour snowfall rates. The storm prediction center (SPC) highlighted west-central, southwest and south central Minnesota 3 times Friday with snowfall rates increasing. A broad area of light snow slowly expanded in areal coverage during the morning and spread as far east as east-central and southeast Minnesota. The heaviest snowfall occurred in a band from Madison, Minnesota, southeast toward Fairmont. Within this band, snowfall amounts ranged from 6-10 inches, with the heaviest along the Buffalo Ridge. The snowfall tapered off during the afternoon and evening from northwest to southeast as the storm moved out of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources across the county indicated that between 4 and 7 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a departing winter system, a strong arctic high settled into the region. Winds gusted to 45 to 60 mph as temperatures plummeted on the evening of January 4 into January 5. Northerly winds stayed steady and brisk into January 6th, as air temperatures dropped into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills colder than 20 below zero lasted for up to three days at many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -41F at Road Weather Information System site MN007 3 miles southeast of Lakefield, -40F at KMJQ in Jackson, and -35F at a personal weather station in Lakefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms traversed the region starting on the evening of April 22nd. Initially, large hail was the primary concern, with an increasingly tornadic environment heading into April 23rd. Both hail (up to 1.5 inches) and tornadoes were reported with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief funnel was reported to the Wilkin County S.O. as being overhead the spotters location. Local storm chasers confirmed that it was short lived and did not touch down.

A blizzard developed in the afternoon hours on January 4th across eastern ND towards the Red River Valley, and adjacent counties in northwest through westcentral MN. Impacts where felt across the area, with blowing and drifting snow, strong gusting winds, and visibilities at or below 1/4 mile, often at whiteout conditions. This prompted the Travel not Advised across most of eastern ND and within the Red River Valley corridor of MN. Highest wind gusts were observed around 45-52mph across the RRV and towards the west. Snow totals ranged from 3-5 inches around the Highway 2 Corridor, and 1-3 further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted at 35 to 52 mph, with visibilities at or below 1/4 mile due to falling and blowing snow for several hours. From 2 to 4 inches of new snow occurred along with existing blowable snowpack.
