EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of Thursday, May 12th, was a classic bow echo that surged north and east across western Minnesota. ||Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dew points, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area.||Winds were measured by wind sensors at over 100 mph across portions of South Dakota, with a measured wind gust at Madison, Minnesota of 94 mph. There were also numerous measured wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph across west central and southwest Minnesota with embedded surges that produced a few tornadoes. The most concentrated area of damage based on reports of farm equipment, trailers, semis, trees, power lines, and other debris, occurred near the upper Minnesota River Valley, northward to Alexandria and Long Prairie.||Over Stevens County alone, structural damage was considerable as the majority of the damage was to farm outbuildings, sheds and grain bins. There were a few locations in the county where the main inhabited house or home structure did suffer loss of roof and siding and windows but those were more isolated in nature. Many locations had debris tossed a mile northeast from its parent location. Nearly 100% of the damage witnessed in Stevens county was consistent with a straight line wind event, with just one tornado crossing the southeast corner of the county.  ||Ten tornadoes were confirmed this day, with two in Lac Qui Parle County, including a house in Bellingham that had much of its roof removed.  In northwestern Swift County, a tornado tipped over trailers and various farm equipment was heavily damaged.  Two tornadoes were in Todd County, including one that moved through Clarissa.  Two more were in Morrison County, and one was on the east side of Lake Alexander, where many trees came down on vehicles, sheds, and cabins. A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hundreds of trees were uprooted or broken. Several outbuildings lost roofs. The damage pattern in this part of Todd County was very complicated, but ground and aerial surveys revealed a tornado moved through part of Clarissa. Maximum winds were estimated at 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.

A reinforcing shot of Arctic air dropped temperatures from minus 15 to minus 35F across all of eastern ND and northwest through westcentral MN, and with winds from 10 to 20 mph, produced wind chills from minus 35 to minus 55 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight low temperatures dropped from minus 15 to minus 25 F, with winds of 10 to 20 mph producing wind chills from minus 35 to minus 55 F.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility was reduced to 1/4 mile or less through much of the day, with winds gusting frequently at 35 to 50 mph, as reported by various airport and mesonet sensors.

An Arctic airmass remained in place during the first two days of 2022. Temperatures managed to rise slowly, but gusty winds continue to produce wind chill values in the -35F degree range for several hours in the morning, and into the early afternoon for a few counties across central and west central Minnesota. Although wind speeds decreased during the afternoon, temperatures held in the upper single digits below zero, to around 15 degrees below zero. By early evening, as winds shifted to the southwest, temperatures dropped into the 20 to 25 below zero range and wind chill values once again fell to -35 to -40 degrees below zero for a few counties across west central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few observations across the county indicated temperatures fell into the 20s below zero by Saturday morning. These temperatures combined with winds around 5-10 mph, held wind chill values between 35 and 45 degrees below zero through mid morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm was associated with a mid-level trough, along with a surface inverted trough centered across the eastern Dakotas. Most of the energy with this system was mid-level warm air advection across west central, southwest and south central Minnesota between Friday morning, through early Friday evening, January 14th. Light snow started Friday morning across western Minnesota and quickly moved southeast. Bands of moderate snowfall produced locally 1 inch per hour snowfall rates. The storm prediction center (SPC) highlighted west-central, southwest and south central Minnesota 3 times Friday with snowfall rates increasing. A broad area of light snow slowly expanded in areal coverage during the morning and spread as far east as east-central and southeast Minnesota. The heaviest snowfall occurred in a band from Madison, Minnesota, southeast toward Fairmont. Within this band, snowfall amounts ranged from 6-10 inches, with the heaviest along the Buffalo Ridge. The snowfall tapered off during the afternoon and evening from northwest to southeast as the storm moved out of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources across the county indicated that between 6 and 9 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of Arctic high pressure built across the Upper Midwest from Monday, January 24, through Tuesday, January 25th. The center of the high moved across the region Tuesday, with return flow developing Tuesday night and Wednesday the 26th. There were two periods where the wind chill values fell below -35F for several hours across portions of central, west central, southwest, and south central Minnesota. The first occurred with the initial ridge building across the region Monday, and Monday night. The second occurred with winds strengthen on the return flow Tuesday night, and into Wednesday. The worst and longest time frame of wind chill values between -35F to -42F were felt Tuesday night, and early Wednesday as return flow strengthen and temperatures were still near -20F. ||A few of the coldest wind chills during this time frame:||-42F at Morris. |-41F at Fairmont. |-40F at Benson. |-40F at Sauk Centre.|-40F at Mankato.|-40F at New Ulm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the Arctic airmass shifted to the east, return flow developed and dropped wind chill values below -35F for several hours Wednesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms that developed over South Dakota early Thursday morning, moved across west-central, and central Minnesota during the morning. Most of the hail fell across the central third of Minnesota, with some hail stones measured at 2 inches in diameter. In addition, a few mesonet wind sensors and the St. Cloud regional airport all reported severe wind gusts. There was also wind damage near Cold Spring and in central Morrison County where power outages occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southward pushing cold air near the surface interacted with a post-frontal wave in southwest flow aloft to produce a prolonged period of light snowfall, which began as a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle across far southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and early evening of February 21. In addition to snowfall which accumulated from as much as 1 to 2 inches, strong northerly winds produced dangerous wind chills from 25 below to 35 below zero from later on February 21 through the afternoon of February 22.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While snowfall was not significant by amount with 3.0 inches at Minneota, 2.0 inches at Marshall, and 1.5 inches at Tracy, when combined with winds which gusted to 41 mph at Marshall and 35 mph at Tracy, visibility was one mile or less and frequently one-half mile or less between 0700-1800CST February 22. In addition, wind chill was as low as -25F to -30F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm was associated with a mid-level trough, along with a surface inverted trough centered across the eastern Dakotas. Most of the energy with this system was mid-level warm air advection across west central, southwest and south central Minnesota between Friday morning, through early Friday evening, January 14th. Light snow started Friday morning across western Minnesota and quickly moved southeast. Bands of moderate snowfall produced locally 1 inch per hour snowfall rates. The storm prediction center (SPC) highlighted west-central, southwest and south central Minnesota 3 times Friday with snowfall rates increasing. A broad area of light snow slowly expanded in areal coverage during the morning and spread as far east as east-central and southeast Minnesota. The heaviest snowfall occurred in a band from Madison, Minnesota, southeast toward Fairmont. Within this band, snowfall amounts ranged from 6-10 inches, with the heaviest along the Buffalo Ridge. The snowfall tapered off during the afternoon and evening from northwest to southeast as the storm moved out of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources across the county indicated that between 4 and 9 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of Arctic high pressure built across the Upper Midwest from Monday, January 24, through Tuesday, January 25th. The center of the high moved across the region Tuesday, with return flow developing Tuesday night and Wednesday the 26th. There were two periods where the wind chill values fell below -35F for several hours across portions of central, west central, southwest, and south central Minnesota. The first occurred with the initial ridge building across the region Monday, and Monday night. The second occurred with winds strengthen on the return flow Tuesday night, and into Wednesday. The worst and longest time frame of wind chill values between -35F to -42F were felt Tuesday night, and early Wednesday as return flow strengthen and temperatures were still near -20F. ||A few of the coldest wind chills during this time frame:||-42F at Morris. |-41F at Fairmont. |-40F at Benson. |-40F at Sauk Centre.|-40F at Mankato.|-40F at New Ulm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the Arctic airmass shifted to the east, return flow developed and dropped wind chill values below -35F for several hours Wednesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.

A blizzard developed in the afternoon hours on January 4th across eastern ND towards the Red River Valley, and adjacent counties in northwest through westcentral MN. Impacts where felt across the area, with blowing and drifting snow, strong gusting winds, and visibilities at or below 1/4 mile, often at whiteout conditions. This prompted the Travel not Advised across most of eastern ND and within the Red River Valley corridor of MN. Highest wind gusts were observed around 45-52mph across the RRV and towards the west. Snow totals ranged from 3-5 inches around the Highway 2 Corridor, and 1-3 further south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted at 35 to 52 mph, with visibilities at or below 1/4 mile due to falling and blowing snow for several hours. From 2 to 4 inches of new snow occurred along with existing blowable snowpack.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a blizzard producing storm system moved out of the area through the morning of January 5th, an Arctic front and associated Arctic airmass pushed in behind. The coldest air spread across portions of central and eastern ND for the first evening into the morning of January 6th, and then spread eastward across all of eastern ND and much pf northwest through westcentral MN on the evening of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures plummeted well below zero, ranging from minus 25 to minus 35 degrees Fahrenheit, with wind chills of from minus 35 to minus 50F, throughout the overnight hours of the 5th through the morning of the 6th, and again from the late afternoon of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of Thursday, May 12th, was a classic bow echo that surged north and east across western Minnesota. ||Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dew points, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area.||Winds were measured by wind sensors at over 100 mph across portions of South Dakota, with a measured wind gust at Madison, Minnesota of 94 mph. There were also numerous measured wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph across west central and southwest Minnesota with embedded surges that produced a few tornadoes. The most concentrated area of damage based on reports of farm equipment, trailers, semis, trees, power lines, and other debris, occurred near the upper Minnesota River Valley, northward to Alexandria and Long Prairie.||Over Stevens County alone, structural damage was considerable as the majority of the damage was to farm outbuildings, sheds and grain bins. There were a few locations in the county where the main inhabited house or home structure did suffer loss of roof and siding and windows but those were more isolated in nature. Many locations had debris tossed a mile northeast from its parent location. Nearly 100% of the damage witnessed in Stevens county was consistent with a straight line wind event, with just one tornado crossing the southeast corner of the county.  ||Ten tornadoes were confirmed this day, with two in Lac Qui Parle County, including a house in Bellingham that had much of its roof removed.  In northwestern Swift County, a tornado tipped over trailers and various farm equipment was heavily damaged.  Two tornadoes were in Todd County, including one that moved through Clarissa.  Two more were in Morrison County, and one was on the east side of Lake Alexander, where many trees came down on vehicles, sheds, and cabins. A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.

A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.

A line of severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of Thursday, May 12th, was a classic bow echo that surged north and east across western Minnesota. ||Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dew points, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area.||Winds were measured by wind sensors at over 100 mph across portions of South Dakota, with a measured wind gust at Madison, Minnesota of 94 mph. There were also numerous measured wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph across west central and southwest Minnesota with embedded surges that produced a few tornadoes. The most concentrated area of damage based on reports of farm equipment, trailers, semis, trees, power lines, and other debris, occurred near the upper Minnesota River Valley, northward to Alexandria and Long Prairie.||Over Stevens County alone, structural damage was considerable as the majority of the damage was to farm outbuildings, sheds and grain bins. There were a few locations in the county where the main inhabited house or home structure did suffer loss of roof and siding and windows but those were more isolated in nature. Many locations had debris tossed a mile northeast from its parent location. Nearly 100% of the damage witnessed in Stevens county was consistent with a straight line wind event, with just one tornado crossing the southeast corner of the county.  ||Ten tornadoes were confirmed this day, with two in Lac Qui Parle County, including a house in Bellingham that had much of its roof removed. Two more were in Morrison County, and one was on the east side of Lake Alexander, where many trees came down on vehicles, sheds, and cabins. A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of severe thunderstorms traversed the region starting on the evening of April 22nd. Initially, large hail was the primary concern, with an increasingly tornadic environment heading into April 23rd. Both hail (up to 1.5 inches) and tornadoes were reported with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported 1.5-inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass remained in place during the first two days of 2022. Temperatures managed to rise slowly, but gusty winds continue to produce wind chill values in the -35F degree range for several hours in the morning, and into the early afternoon for a few counties across central and west central Minnesota. Although wind speeds decreased during the afternoon, temperatures held in the upper single digits below zero, to around 15 degrees below zero. By early evening, as winds shifted to the southwest, temperatures dropped into the 20 to 25 below zero range and wind chill values once again fell to -35 to -40 degrees below zero for a few counties across west central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few observations across the county indicated temperatures fell to around 20 below zero by Saturday morning, and continued steady through most of the day. These temperatures combined with winds around 5-10 mph, held wind chill values between 35 and 40 degrees below zero through early afternoon before winds became light.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm was associated with a mid-level trough, along with a surface inverted trough centered across the eastern Dakotas. Most of the energy with this system was mid-level warm air advection across west central, southwest and south central Minnesota between Friday morning, through early Friday evening, January 14th. Light snow started Friday morning across western Minnesota and quickly moved southeast. Bands of moderate snowfall produced locally 1 inch per hour snowfall rates. The storm prediction center (SPC) highlighted west-central, southwest and south central Minnesota 3 times Friday with snowfall rates increasing. A broad area of light snow slowly expanded in areal coverage during the morning and spread as far east as east-central and southeast Minnesota. The heaviest snowfall occurred in a band from Madison, Minnesota, southeast toward Fairmont. Within this band, snowfall amounts ranged from 6-10 inches, with the heaviest along the Buffalo Ridge. The snowfall tapered off during the afternoon and evening from northwest to southeast as the storm moved out of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources across the county indicated that between 5 and 7 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of Arctic high pressure built across the Upper Midwest from Monday, January 24, through Tuesday, January 25th. The center of the high moved across the region Tuesday, with return flow developing Tuesday night and Wednesday the 26th. There were two periods where the wind chill values fell below -35F for several hours across portions of central, west central, southwest, and south central Minnesota. The first occurred with the initial ridge building across the region Monday, and Monday night. The second occurred with winds strengthen on the return flow Tuesday night, and into Wednesday. The worst and longest time frame of wind chill values between -35F to -42F were felt Tuesday night, and early Wednesday as return flow strengthen and temperatures were still near -20F. ||A few of the coldest wind chills during this time frame:||-42F at Morris. |-41F at Fairmont. |-40F at Benson. |-40F at Sauk Centre.|-40F at Mankato.|-40F at New Ulm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the Arctic airmass shifted to the east, return flow developed and dropped wind chill values below -35F for several hours Wednesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.

As a blizzard producing storm system moved out of the area through the morning of January 5th, an Arctic front and associated Arctic airmass pushed in behind. The coldest air spread across portions of central and eastern ND for the first evening into the morning of January 6th, and then spread eastward across all of eastern ND and much pf northwest through westcentral MN on the evening of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures plummeted well below zero, ranging from minus 25 to minus 35 degrees Fahrenheit, with wind chills of from minus 35 to minus 50F, throughout the overnight hours of the 5th through the morning of the 6th, and again from the late afternoon of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.

A fast moving cold frontal system dropped across eastern ND and northwest MN through the afternoon hours of January 18th. Very strong winds speed developed rapidly behind the front, and with abundant fresh and blowable snow, ground blizzard conditions became widespread across the largely exposed terrain of eastern ND and adjacent portions of northwest through westcentral MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 40 to 58 mph produced widespread ground blizzard condition over open country. Fresh and very blowable snow helped to sustain visibilities below 1/4 mile for several hours, with whiteout conditions at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: West-central and northwest Minnesota were impacted by a winter storm starting the afternoon of April 12th, and lasting until the evening of April 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall totals were reported across the area, with amounts ranging from 7 inches to 14 inches. Blowing snow reduced visibility at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass with temperatures in the teens below zero spread across southeast Minnesota toward the end of January. These temperatures combined with wind speeds of 10 to 20 mph produced dangerously cold wind chills of 35 to 40 below during the morning of January 25th. Another round of dangerously cold wind chills occurred during the  morning of January 26th. Wind chills during this period dropped into the 35 to 45 below range. The coldest reported wind chill was 48 below at mesonet station near Canton (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills around 35 below zero occurred across Dodge County during the morning of January 25th. The coldest wind chill of 37 below occurred at a mesonet station east of Kasson.

By early evening, as winds shifted to the southwest, temperatures dropped into the 20 to 25 below zero range and wind chill values once again fell to -35 to -40 degrees below zero for a few counties across west central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few observations across the county indicated temperatures fell to around 20 below zero by Saturday morning, and continued steady through most of the day. These temperatures combined with winds around 5-10 mph, held wind chill values between 35 and 40 degrees below zero through early afternoon before winds became light.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm was associated with a mid-level trough, along with a surface inverted trough centered across the eastern Dakotas. Most of the energy with this system was mid-level warm air advection across west central, southwest and south central Minnesota between Friday morning, through early Friday evening, January 14th. Light snow started Friday morning across western Minnesota and quickly moved southeast. Bands of moderate snowfall produced locally 1 inch per hour snowfall rates. The storm prediction center (SPC) highlighted west-central, southwest and south central Minnesota 3 times Friday with snowfall rates increasing. A broad area of light snow slowly expanded in areal coverage during the morning and spread as far east as east-central and southeast Minnesota. The heaviest snowfall occurred in a band from Madison, Minnesota, southeast toward Fairmont. Within this band, snowfall amounts ranged from 6-10 inches, with the heaviest along the Buffalo Ridge. The snowfall tapered off during the afternoon and evening from northwest to southeast as the storm moved out of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources across the county indicated that between 5 and 7 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of Arctic high pressure built across the Upper Midwest from Monday, January 24, through Tuesday, January 25th. The center of the high moved across the region Tuesday, with return flow developing Tuesday night and Wednesday the 26th. There were two periods where the wind chill values fell below -35F for several hours across portions of central, west central, southwest, and south central Minnesota. The first occurred with the initial ridge building across the region Monday, and Monday night. The second occurred with winds strengthen on the return flow Tuesday night, and into Wednesday. The worst and longest time frame of wind chill values between -35F to -42F were felt Tuesday night, and early Wednesday as return flow strengthen and temperatures were still near -20F. ||A few of the coldest wind chills during this time frame:||-42F at Morris. |-41F at Fairmont. |-40F at Benson. |-40F at Sauk Centre.|-40F at Mankato.|-40F at New Ulm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the Arctic airmass shifted to the east, return flow developed and dropped wind chill values below -35F for several hours Wednesday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving cold frontal system dropped across eastern ND and northwest MN through the afternoon hours of January 18th. Very strong winds speed developed rapidly behind the front, and with abundant fresh and blowable snow, ground blizzard conditions became widespread across the largely exposed terrain of eastern ND and adjacent portions of northwest through westcentral MN.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting from 40 to 58 mph produced widespread ground blizzard condition over open country. Fresh and very blowable snow helped to sustain visibilities below 1/4 mile for several hours, with whiteout conditions at times.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions prevailed through the late morning and into much of the afternoon, with visibility down to 1/4 mile and winds frequently gusting over 35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass with temperatures in the teens below zero spread across southeast Minnesota toward the end of January. These temperatures combined with wind speeds of 10 to 20 mph produced dangerously cold wind chills of 35 to 40 below during the morning of January 25th. Another round of dangerously cold wind chills occurred during the  morning of January 26th. Wind chills during this period dropped into the 35 to 45 below range. The coldest reported wind chill was 48 below at mesonet station near Canton (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills 35 to 40 below occurred across Olmsted County during the morning of January 25th. The coldest wind chill of 40 below was reported by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry-line/cold front was moving across Minnesota during the afternoon of Monday, May 9th. A few thunderstorms developed along this front across central Minnesota, and quickly spread south and eastward during the late afternoon and evening over eastern Minnesota, and adjacent west central Wisconsin. A brief funnel cloud was observed south of Gilman, and a tornado impacted Dakota County between Hastings and Miesville. Large hail and a few damaging wind gusts occurred along this line as it moved eastward. The largest hail stone was 2 inches in diameter and occurred near St. Paul downtown airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two separate groups of storm chasers photographed a funnel cloud. Neither photo showed any ground contact, and no damage was reported. There were no reliable spotter reports of it touching down, so this has been documented as a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms developed over south central Minnesota during the pre-dawn hours of Wednesday, May 11th. These storms quickly became severe near Elysian, and moved northward. This storm continued to move northward toward the Twin Cities but the severity diminished after New Prague and only small hail was reported in the southern suburbs of the Twin Cities. Another cluster of storms developed between Faribault and Owatonna and moved eastward before becoming severe over southern Goodhue County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was measured at 3.20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms produced large hail stones west of Granite Falls, near Olivia, and northeast toward the northwest suburbs of the Twin Cities. Most of these storms were elevated but had a steep lapse rate to generate large hail, up to 2 inches in diameter at times. ||These late morning, and early afternoon storms moved northeast out of Minnesota. A warm front lifted northward across southern Minnesota during the afternoon with a potent shortwave moving northeast out of Nebraska. This shortwave generated a large complex of storms and quickly moved northeast across southern Minnesota and produced numerous damaging wind gusts, tornadoes and hail. There were even some flooding concerns as three to five inches of rain fell across portions of west central and central Minnesota by the late evening as the storms diminished and moved off to the northeast.||Three tornadoes occurred within the large bow echo that surged from southwest Minnesota, northeast toward east central Minnesota. One occurred in Brown County, another in Sibley and Carver County, and a third in Anoka County. All of the tornadoes were rated EF0 and produced mainly tree and roof damage.||According to Xcel Energy, more than 75,000 customers were without power across southern Minnesota during the peak of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large branches were blown down, a few structures were damaged, including a building under construction that had its roof blown off. Power poles were also blown down near the same area, east of Madelia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across much of northeastern Minnesota during the month of January with overall little change observed. The worst areas were across parts of Koochiching and northwestern Itasca counties, along with portions of Lake and Cook counties where areas of D2 Severe Drought persisted. Precipitation overall was generally near to below normal, but slight improvement was observed over southern Lake County where deep snowpack was in place.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The far northwestern corner of northern Itasca County remained in D2 Severe Drought throughout the month with D1 Moderate Drought in place across the balance of northern Itasca County. Precipitation for the month was near to slightly below normal, but no change in the drought status was observed.

Behind a departing winter system, a strong arctic high settled into the region. Winds gusted to 45 to 60 mph as temperatures plummeted on the evening of January 4 into January 5. Northerly winds stayed steady and brisk into January 6th, as air temperatures dropped into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills colder than 20 below zero lasted for up to three days at many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -44F 1 mile west of Lake Benton at Road Weather Information System site MN027 and -41F 5 miles south of Hendricks at site MN029.

Northerly winds stayed steady and brisk into January 6th, as air temperatures dropped into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills colder than 20 below zero lasted for up to three days at many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -42F at site KMML in Marshall and -39F at KTKC in Tracy, as well as -35F at a personal weather station in Minneota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass remained in place during the first two days of 2022. Temperatures managed to rise slowly, but gusty winds continue to produce wind chill values in the -35F degree range for several hours in the morning, and into the early afternoon for a few counties across central and west central Minnesota. Although wind speeds decreased during the afternoon, temperatures held in the upper single digits below zero, to around 15 degrees below zero. By early evening, as winds shifted to the southwest, temperatures dropped into the 20 to 25 below zero range and wind chill values once again fell to -35 to -40 degrees below zero for a few counties across west central Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few observations across the county indicated temperatures fell to around 13 below zero by Saturday morning. These temperatures combined with winds around 10 mph, held wind chill values around 35 degrees below zero through mid morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of Arctic high pressure built across the Upper Midwest from Monday, January 24, through Tuesday, January 25th. The center of the high moved across the region Tuesday, with return flow developing Tuesday night and Wednesday the 26th. There were two periods where the wind chill values fell below -35F for several hours across portions of central, west central, southwest, and south central Minnesota. The first occurred with the initial ridge building across the region Monday, and Monday night. The second occurred with winds strengthen on the return flow Tuesday night, and into Wednesday. The worst and longest time frame of wind chill values between -35F to -42F were felt Tuesday night, and early Wednesday as return flow strengthen and temperatures were still near -20F. ||A few of the coldest wind chills during this time frame:||-42F at Morris. |-41F at Fairmont. |-40F at Benson. |-40F at Sauk Centre.|-40F at Mankato.|-40F at New Ulm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several sources of weather observations including airports, MnDOT, and private weather sensors indicated wind chill values dropped to -35F after midnight, and continued through mid morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a blizzard producing storm system moved out of the area through the morning of January 5th, an Arctic front and associated Arctic airmass pushed in behind. The coldest air spread across portions of central and eastern ND for the first evening into the morning of January 6th, and then spread eastward across all of eastern ND and much pf northwest through westcentral MN on the evening of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures plummeted well below zero, ranging from minus 25 to minus 35 degrees Fahrenheit, with wind chills of from minus 35 to minus 50F, throughout the overnight hours of the 5th through the morning of the 6th, and again from the late afternoon of the 6th through the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic airmass moved overhead across the Upper Midwest during the early morning of Friday, January 7th. The actual temperature dropped into the teens below to near -30F by morning. Although the surface ridge moved overhead, initially before winds became nearly calm, wind chill values dropped into the 35 to 45 below zero range. During the morning, winds shifted to the south as the surface ridge moved off to the east and allowed for wind chill values to once again drop below -35F before noon. ||Some of the lowest wind chill values during this time frame:||-46F at Glenwood. |-45F at Long Prairie. |-43F at Paynesville.|-43F at Morris.|-41F at Alexandria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surface observations, and in combination of airport ASOS/AWOS sites, indicated wind chill values dropped below -35F during the afternoon of Thursday, January 6th. Wind chill values didn't recover above -35F until noon the next day. However, there was a period during the overnight where winds dropped to nearly calm but temperatures fell to -30F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With cold air entrenched across the area, a mid-level wave dug southward through the Plains. A swath of snowfall and some light freezing rain developed in the leading warm advection zone northeast of the track of low pressure from South Dakota to Missouri. The highest snowfall of 5 to 11 inches was northeast of a line from De Smet, SD to Ida Grove, IA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was moderate to heavy at times between 0800-1400CST, and produced treacherous travel conditions. Snowfall totaled 10.0 inches at Ivanhoe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant hail event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Southeast-Central Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A multicellular convective mode advanced in two waves with the first wave occurring through the morning hours in Southern MN, while the second wave produced a long-track supercell over the Twin Cities metro and Central Wisconsin. Both waves produced significant hail and damaging wind gusts. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon produced countless hailstones across the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. Beyond the typical 1 inch diameter hailstones, there were several hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter that fell over Washington County. A few other large hailstones fell over Le Sueur, Dakota and Goodhue Counties of southern Minnesota.

Following a rapidly moving arctic cold front, an intensely cold arctic airmass settled into the area. As winds of from 10 to 20 mph persisted into the overnight hours of January 18th into the early morning of the 19th, and temperatures dropped from minus 15 to minus 25F, wind chills dropped from minus 35 to minus 50 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight low temperatures dropped from minus 15 to minus 25 F, with winds of 10 to 20 mph producing wind chills from minus 35 to minus 50 F.

During the morning of Monday, May 9th, a small complex of thunderstorms that developed across eastern South Dakota moved eastward and affected a large area of west-central, central and east-central Minnesota before moving into west-central Wisconsin late Monday morning. Although scattered thunderstorms developed south and north of this main supercell storm, this storm in particular was the main producing of large hail, up to baseball size, that fell from shortly after sunrise in far west-central Minnesota, to east-central Minnesota, north of the Twin Cities, by 10 AM. This storm continued to pulse up and down from tops as high as 60,000 feet. Damage was widespread where the large hailstone fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a social media picture of blown down power lines near Lindstrom. The emergency manager also reported several trees down in Lindstrom.

A dry-line/cold front was moving across Minnesota during the afternoon of Monday, May 9th. A few thunderstorms developed along this front across central Minnesota, and quickly spread south and eastward during the late afternoon and evening over eastern Minnesota, and adjacent west central Wisconsin. A brief funnel cloud was observed south of Gilman, and a tornado impacted Dakota County between Hastings and Miesville. Large hail and a few damaging wind gusts occurred along this line as it moved eastward. The largest hail stone was 2 inches in diameter and occurred near St. Paul downtown airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several dozen trees were uprooted or broken, with some landing on fences and houses. A damage survey indicated a classic microburst pattern. Time based on radar and a citizen time stamp on a photo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms developed over south central Minnesota during the pre-dawn hours of Wednesday, May 11th. These storms quickly became severe near Elysian, and moved northward. This storm continued to move northward toward the Twin Cities but the severity diminished after New Prague and only small hail was reported in the southern suburbs of the Twin Cities. Another cluster of storms developed between Faribault and Owatonna and moved eastward before becoming severe over southern Goodhue County.

A cluster of storms produced large hail stones west of Granite Falls, near Olivia, and northeast toward the northwest suburbs of the Twin Cities. Most of these storms were elevated but had a steep lapse rate to generate large hail, up to 2 inches in diameter at times. ||These late morning, and early afternoon storms moved northeast out of Minnesota. A warm front lifted northward across southern Minnesota during the afternoon with a potent shortwave moving northeast out of Nebraska. This shortwave generated a large complex of storms and quickly moved northeast across southern Minnesota and produced numerous damaging wind gusts, tornadoes and hail. There were even some flooding concerns as three to five inches of rain fell across portions of west central and central Minnesota by the late evening as the storms diminished and moved off to the northeast.||Three tornadoes occurred within the large bow echo that surged from southwest Minnesota, northeast toward east central Minnesota. One occurred in Brown County, another in Sibley and Carver County, and a third in Anoka County. All of the tornadoes were rated EF0 and produced mainly tree and roof damage.||According to Xcel Energy, more than 75,000 customers were without power across southern Minnesota during the peak of the storm.

A significant hail event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Southeast-Central Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A multicellular convective mode advanced in two waves with the first wave occurring through the morning hours in Southern MN, while the second wave produced a long-track supercell over the Twin Cities metro and Central Wisconsin. Both waves produced significant hail and damaging wind gusts. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon produced countless hailstones across the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. Beyond the typical 1 inch diameter hailstones, there were several hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter that fell over Washington County. A few other large hailstones fell over Le Sueur, Dakota and Goodhue Counties of southern Minnesota.

A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of Thursday, May 12th, was a classic bow echo that surged north and east across western Minnesota. ||Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dew points, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area.||Winds were measured by wind sensors at over 100 mph across portions of South Dakota, with a measured wind gust at Madison, Minnesota of 94 mph. There were also numerous measured wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph across west central and southwest Minnesota with embedded surges that produced a few tornadoes. The most concentrated area of damage based on reports of farm equipment, trailers, semis, trees, power lines, and other debris, occurred near the upper Minnesota River Valley, northward to Alexandria and Long Prairie.||Over Stevens County alone, structural damage was considerable as the majority of the damage was to farm outbuildings, sheds and grain bins. There were a few locations in the county where the main inhabited house or home structure did suffer loss of roof and siding and windows but those were more isolated in nature. Many locations had debris tossed a mile northeast from its parent location. Nearly 100% of the damage witnessed in Stevens county was consistent with a straight line wind event, with just one tornado crossing the southeast corner of the county.  ||Ten tornadoes were confirmed this day, with two in Lac Qui Parle County, including a house in Bellingham that had much of its roof removed.  In northwestern Swift County, a tornado tipped over trailers and various farm equipment was heavily damaged.  Two tornadoes were in Todd County, including one that moved through Clarissa.  Two more were in Morrison County, and one was on the east side of Lake Alexander, where many trees came down on vehicles, sheds, and cabins. A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of Monday, May 9th, a small complex of thunderstorms that developed across eastern South Dakota moved eastward and affected a large area of west-central, central and east-central Minnesota before moving into west-central Wisconsin late Monday morning. Although scattered thunderstorms developed south and north of this main supercell storm, this storm in particular was the main producing of large hail, up to baseball size, that fell from shortly after sunrise in far west-central Minnesota, to east-central Minnesota, north of the Twin Cities, by 10 AM. This storm continued to pulse up and down from tops as high as 60,000 feet. Damage was widespread where the large hailstone fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of trees and power lines were downed, with some trees and large branches landing on houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were downed, with one partially blocking a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of Thursday, May 12th, was a classic bow echo that surged north and east across western Minnesota. ||Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dew points, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area.||Winds were measured by wind sensors at over 100 mph across portions of South Dakota, with a measured wind gust at Madison, Minnesota of 94 mph. There were also numerous measured wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph across west central and southwest Minnesota with embedded surges that produced a few tornadoes. The most concentrated area of damage based on reports of farm equipment, trailers, semis, trees, power lines, and other debris, occurred near the upper Minnesota River Valley, northward to Alexandria and Long Prairie.||Over Stevens County alone, structural damage was considerable as the majority of the damage was to farm outbuildings, sheds and grain bins. There were a few locations in the county where the main inhabited house or home structure did suffer loss of roof and siding and windows but those were more isolated in nature. Many locations had debris tossed a mile northeast from its parent location. Nearly 100% of the damage witnessed in Stevens county was consistent with a straight line wind event, with just one tornado crossing the southeast corner of the county.  ||Ten tornadoes were confirmed this day, with two in Lac Qui Parle County, including a house in Bellingham that had much of its roof removed.  In northwestern Swift County, a tornado tipped over trailers and various farm equipment was heavily damaged.  Two tornadoes were in Todd County, including one that moved through Clarissa.  Two more were in Morrison County, and one was on the east side of Lake Alexander, where many trees came down on vehicles, sheds, and cabins. A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.

A dry-line/cold front was moving across Minnesota during the afternoon of Monday, May 9th. A few thunderstorms developed along this front across central Minnesota, and quickly spread south and eastward during the late afternoon and evening over eastern Minnesota, and adjacent west central Wisconsin. A brief funnel cloud was observed south of Gilman, and a tornado impacted Dakota County between Hastings and Miesville. Large hail and a few damaging wind gusts occurred along this line as it moved eastward. The largest hail stone was 2 inches in diameter and occurred near St. Paul downtown airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a social media picture of golf ball size hail.

A dry-line/cold front was moving across Minnesota during the afternoon of Monday, May 9th. A few thunderstorms developed along this front across central Minnesota, and quickly spread south and eastward during the late afternoon and evening over eastern Minnesota, and adjacent west central Wisconsin. A brief funnel cloud was observed south of Gilman, and a tornado impacted Dakota County between Hastings and Miesville. Large hail and a few damaging wind gusts occurred along this line as it moved eastward. The largest hail stone was 2 inches in diameter and occurred near St. Paul downtown airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a social media picture of 1.25 inch hail.

During the morning of Monday, May 9th, a small complex of thunderstorms that developed across eastern South Dakota moved eastward and affected a large area of west-central, central and east-central Minnesota before moving into west-central Wisconsin late Monday morning. Although scattered thunderstorms developed south and north of this main supercell storm, this storm in particular was the main producing of large hail, up to baseball size, that fell from shortly after sunrise in far west-central Minnesota, to east-central Minnesota, north of the Twin Cities, by 10 AM. This storm continued to pulse up and down from tops as high as 60,000 feet. Damage was widespread where the large hailstone fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a social media picture of 1.5 inch hail.

There were even some flooding concerns as three to five inches of rain fell across portions of west central and central Minnesota by the late evening as the storms diminished and moved off to the northeast.||Three tornadoes occurred within the large bow echo that surged from southwest Minnesota, northeast toward east central Minnesota. One occurred in Brown County, another in Sibley and Carver County, and a third in Anoka County. All of the tornadoes were rated EF0 and produced mainly tree and roof damage.||According to Xcel Energy, more than 75,000 customers were without power across southern Minnesota during the peak of the storm.

A significant hail event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Southeast-Central Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A multicellular convective mode advanced in two waves with the first wave occurring through the morning hours in Southern MN, while the second wave produced a long-track supercell over the Twin Cities metro and Central Wisconsin. Both waves produced significant hail and damaging wind gusts. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon produced countless hailstones across the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. Beyond the typical 1 inch diameter hailstones, there were several hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter that fell over Washington County. A few other large hailstones fell over Le Sueur, Dakota and Goodhue Counties of southern Minnesota.

A dry-line/cold front was moving across Minnesota during the afternoon of Monday, May 9th. A few thunderstorms developed along this front across central Minnesota, and quickly spread south and eastward during the late afternoon and evening over eastern Minnesota, and adjacent west central Wisconsin. A brief funnel cloud was observed south of Gilman, and a tornado impacted Dakota County between Hastings and Miesville. Large hail and a few damaging wind gusts occurred along this line as it moved eastward. The largest hail stone was 2 inches in diameter and occurred near St. Paul downtown airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A two foot diameter tree was blown down south of Hastings. This was additional damage from the severe thunderstorm that produced a tornado south of this location.

A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tops of pine trees were snapped off near Boyd.

A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms that developed across western Iowa during the pre-dawn hours, quickly moved north, and northeast across southern, and central Minnesota during the morning. Steep lapse rates in an elevated unstable atmosphere produced lots of small hail, but a few severe hail stones did occur in the strongest storms. Most of the hail was between one quarter and three quarters of an inch, but a few managed to increase to around 1.25 near Hutchinson. There was one wind damage report south of New Prague where a few large trees were blown down.

Scattered severe thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging wind gusts, and tornadoes during the afternoon of June 12th.

Two rounds of severe storms moved across southeast Minnesota on April 12th. The first round occurred in the afternoon as storms producing large hail moved across the area. Quarter sized hail fell from north of Racine (Mower County) east to near Winona (Winona County). A second round of severe storms occurred in the evening. An EF2 tornado moved through Taopi (Mower County), where homes were destroyed or severely damaged, grain bins were destroyed, cars were tossed or rolled, and trees and power lines were downed. Two people sustained injuries while sheltering from the storm when they became trapped under debris. An EF1 tornado occurred just southwest of Spring Valley (Fillmore County), damaging several farm outbuildings and trees. Two EF0 tornadoes occurred southwest of Lewiston (Winona County), where the damage was mainly to trees and one farm. The Mower County Board of Commissioners ratified an emergency declaration for Taopi and a state of emergency was declared for Fillmore County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell north of Racine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe storms moved across southeast Minnesota on April 12th. The first round occurred in the afternoon as storms producing large hail moved across the area. Quarter sized hail fell from north of Racine (Mower County) east to near Winona (Winona County). A second round of severe storms occurred in the evening. An EF2 tornado moved through Taopi (Mower County), where homes were destroyed or severely damaged, grain bins were destroyed, cars were tossed or rolled, and trees and power lines were downed. Two people sustained injuries while sheltering from the storm when they became trapped under debris. An EF1 tornado occurred just southwest of Spring Valley (Fillmore County), damaging several farm outbuildings and trees. Two EF0 tornadoes occurred southwest of Lewiston (Winona County), where the damage was mainly to trees and one farm. The Mower County Board of Commissioners ratified an emergency declaration for Taopi and a state of emergency was declared for Fillmore County.

A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms that developed across western Iowa during the pre-dawn hours, quickly moved north, and northeast across southern, and central Minnesota during the morning. Steep lapse rates in an elevated unstable atmosphere produced lots of small hail, but a few severe hail stones did occur in the strongest storms. Most of the hail was between one quarter and three quarters of an inch, but a few managed to increase to around 1.25 near Hutchinson. There was one wind damage report south of New Prague where a few large trees were blown down.

During the morning of Monday, May 9th, a small complex of thunderstorms that developed across eastern South Dakota moved eastward and affected a large area of west-central, central and east-central Minnesota before moving into west-central Wisconsin late Monday morning. Although scattered thunderstorms developed south and north of this main supercell storm, this storm in particular was the main producing of large hail, up to baseball size, that fell from shortly after sunrise in far west-central Minnesota, to east-central Minnesota, north of the Twin Cities, by 10 AM. This storm continued to pulse up and down from tops as high as 60,000 feet. Damage was widespread where the large hailstone fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A damaging wind event unfolded across west-central Minnesota during the evening of June 20 and into the overnight hours of June 21. Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dewpoints, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area. Numerous wind gusts of 60-70 kts were measured across west-central Minnesota, along with widespread tree damage. One fatality occurred when a tree fell on a camper in Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large trees were snapped off at the top west of Millerville.

A line of severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of Thursday, May 12th, was a classic bow echo that surged north and east across western Minnesota. ||Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dew points, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area.||Winds were measured by wind sensors at over 100 mph across portions of South Dakota, with a measured wind gust at Madison, Minnesota of 94 mph. There were also numerous measured wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph across west central and southwest Minnesota with embedded surges that produced a few tornadoes. The most concentrated area of damage based on reports of farm equipment, trailers, semis, trees, power lines, and other debris, occurred near the upper Minnesota River Valley, northward to Alexandria and Long Prairie.||Over Stevens County alone, structural damage was considerable as the majority of the damage was to farm outbuildings, sheds and grain bins. There were a few locations in the county where the main inhabited house or home structure did suffer loss of roof and siding and windows but those were more isolated in nature. Many locations had debris tossed a mile northeast from its parent location. Nearly 100% of the damage witnessed in Stevens county was consistent with a straight line wind event, with just one tornado crossing the southeast corner of the county.  ||Ten tornadoes were confirmed this day, with two in Lac Qui Parle County, including a house in Bellingham that had much of its roof removed.  In northwestern Swift County, a tornado tipped over trailers and various farm equipment was heavily damaged.  Two tornadoes were in Todd County, including one that moved through Clarissa.  Two more were in Morrison County, and one was on the east side of Lake Alexander, where many trees came down on vehicles, sheds, and cabins. A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video showed an old church was blown off its foundation and overturned. Time estimated by radar.

A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A street light was blown over on the SW corner of Hutchinson near School Rd SW and 8th Ave SW.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large tree limbs were blown down near St. Michael.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms that developed over South Dakota early Thursday morning, moved across west-central, and central Minnesota during the morning. Most of the hail fell across the central third of Minnesota, with some hail stones measured at 2 inches in diameter. In addition, a few mesonet wind sensors and the St. Cloud regional airport all reported severe wind gusts. There was also wind damage near Cold Spring and in central Morrison County where power outages occurred.

A line of thunderstorms developed late Tuesday morning across southwest Minnesota and quickly moved eastward over southern Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin by mid afternoon. One severe storm became severe near Waldorf and caused sporadic wind damage across rural Waseca County. ||Another strong storm became severe east of Mankato as strong wind shear and ample elevated instability produced large hail near Cannon Falls. There were numerous reports of small hail from northeast of Mankato, through Faribault and the Red Wing area. The largest hail stone fell near Northfield and was measured at 1.50 inches in diameter. ||There was also significant damage to areas southwest, south and southeast of the city of Faribault.  Damage included numerous trees down, power lines down, power line poles sheared off, trees on power lines, roofs partially off houses, some sheds, pole barns destroyed and pieces blown over long distances.  In addition to the wind damage, straight-line winds caused 9 semi trucks to be blown over on I-35, just south of Faribault.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was isolated wind damage reported 8 miles north of Kenyon, along Highway 56, where a few trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep area of low pressure that was centered across southern North Dakota late Wednesday night, slowly moved eastward across northern Minnesota on Thursday. On the south side of this area of low pressure, a strong pressure gradient created winds in excess of 40-45 mph sustained across a wide area of western and southern Minnesota. Several gusts in the 50 to 55 mph range also occurred with the strongest wind gust of 59 mph at the Redwood Falls Airport Thursday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind sensors across Swift County reported sustained winds in excess of 45 to 50 mph, with gusts near 55 mph at times.

On the south side of this area of low pressure, a strong pressure gradient created winds in excess of 40-45 mph sustained across a wide area of western and southern Minnesota. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind sensors across Redwood Falls County reported sustained winds in excess of 45 mph, with gusts near 55 mph at times. The Redwood Falls airport measured a wind gust of 59 mph during the height of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 90 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Cottonwood and Nobles Counties for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were much closer to normal in October, precipitation was generally less than 25 percent of normal, leading to the maintenance of long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite near normal temperatures, precipitation at Worthington was from 25 to 33 percent of normal during October.  Severe (D2) drought conditions developed mid-month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Pipestone, precipitation was around 33 percent of normal with temperatures of around 3 degrees above normal during September. Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to severe (D2) after mid-month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northward from the Central Plains to the Northern Plains on May 12th and lifted a warm front across the Northland bringing two rounds of storms to the region. The first round during the afternoon hours were prolific hail producing storms with the largest hail stones being ping pong ball sized. Another round of storms arrived during the early evening hours and contained strong winds that moved through the Brainerd Lakes area. A maximum gust of 61 mph was recorded at the Brainerd Airport leading to widespread tree damage in the area. As storms continued overnight, heavy rainfall along the North Shore led to Flash Flooding in Lake and Cook counties with numerous road washouts reported.

An area of low pressure moved across the Northland during the afternoon of the 18th. Storms developed ahead of an associated cold front and turned into a line of storms that worked across much of northeastern Minnesota and into northwest Wisconsin. The storms were mainly wind producers with numerous reports of trees down and minor structure damage as the moved through. Some hail was reported as well, but was mainly around an inch or less. An embedded EF-0 tornado was confirmed just north of the Duluth area and produced mainly tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo was received showing a healthy spruce tree that was downed by the storm along with additional deciduous tree branches on the ground around it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of Monday, May 9th, a small complex of thunderstorms that developed across eastern South Dakota moved eastward and affected a large area of west-central, central and east-central Minnesota before moving into west-central Wisconsin late Monday morning. Although scattered thunderstorms developed south and north of this main supercell storm, this storm in particular was the main producing of large hail, up to baseball size, that fell from shortly after sunrise in far west-central Minnesota, to east-central Minnesota, north of the Twin Cities, by 10 AM. This storm continued to pulse up and down from tops as high as 60,000 feet. Damage was widespread where the large hailstone fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a social media picture of 3 inch hail.
