EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of May 30th, a few discrete/semi-discrete supercells developed across central MN producing low-end severe hail. However, the main focus of the event was a QLCS moving northeast from FSD���s CWA into south-central to central Minnesota. After crossing into the MPX CWA in southwest Minnesota, this feature transitioned to more of a line/bow echo but no damaging wind reports were observed, likely due to the large amount of MLCIN in southern Minnesota. However, lesser MLCIN and slightly better low-level lapse rates across central MN allowed for the bow echo's  winds to start reaching the surface with an 84 mph wind gust measured at the Olivia AWOS. A grain bin was blown away near Sacred Heart as well as some trees and power lines downed near Belgrade.

A slow moving area of low pressure meandered across northwestern Minnesota on the 13th and 14th before moving out late on the 14th. Snowfall was generally light across the region, but the Kabetogama area in northwestern St. Louis County was able to record just over 6 inches on less than 12 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A localized area of just over 6 inches of snow fell in the northeast corner of the county. Totals elsewhere across the northern part of the county were around 4 inches or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms developed across Iowa during the afternoon of Saturday, April 23rd. These storms were moving quickly northward just ahead of a cold front that was moving eastward across Minnesota. As these storms moved into south central Minnesota, isolated damage occurred northeast of Janesville. Several surges along the reflectivity gradient line provided sporadic tree and farm outbuilding damage between Janesville and  Owatonna, northeastward to Red Wing. The strongest circulation was noted south of Cannon Falls, before moving northeastward over the Mississippi River near Red Wing.
High winds followed an area of showers in the early morning hours of May 8th. Winds as high as 71 mph were reported, with several reports of wind upwards of 63 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts in the nearby area ranged from 60 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved across southeast Minnesota on May 19th. The first occurred from late morning through the middle of the afternoon and produced primarily large hail across Mower and Olmsted Counties. The largest reported hail with this activity was ping pong ball sized near Lyle (Mower County), Adams (Mower County), south of Rochester (Olmsted County) and east of Rochester. The second round of storms also produced hail, although not as large as the earlier storms. The largest reported hail in the late afternoon was quarter sized north of Genoa (Olmsted County). A large tree, estimated to be four feet in diameter, was blown down near Whalan (Fillmore County). Flash flooding across eastern Winona County caused several mud slides, washouts and some cars were submerged in water. Winona County declared a state of emergency and the Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance to help with the recovery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported east of Rochester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving out of the Central Plains brought a warm front through northern Minnesota during the morning hours of the 9th. Supported by a strong low level jet and warm air pushing into the region, storms spread north through the morning hours. Storms produced hail as large as gold balls and damaging winds before losing strength during the late morning as they moved into the Arrowhead.

An area of low pressure moved across the Northland during the afternoon of the 18th. Storms developed ahead of an associated cold front and turned into a line of storms that worked across much of northeastern Minnesota and into northwest Wisconsin. The storms were mainly wind producers with numerous reports of trees down and minor structure damage as the moved through. Some hail was reported as well, but was mainly around an inch or less. An embedded EF-0 tornado was confirmed just north of the Duluth area and produced mainly tree damage.

A low pressure system moved northward from the Central Plains to the Northern Plains on May 12th and lifted a warm front across the Northland bringing two rounds of storms to the region. The first round during the afternoon hours were prolific hail producing storms with the largest hail stones being ping pong ball sized. Another round of storms arrived during the early evening hours and contained strong winds that moved through the Brainerd Lakes area. A maximum gust of 61 mph was recorded at the Brainerd Airport leading to widespread tree damage in the area. As storms continued overnight, heavy rainfall along the North Shore led to Flash Flooding in Lake and Cook counties with numerous road washouts reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of Monday, May 9th, a small complex of thunderstorms that developed across eastern South Dakota moved eastward and affected a large area of west-central, central and east-central Minnesota before moving into west-central Wisconsin late Monday morning. Although scattered thunderstorms developed south and north of this main supercell storm, this storm in particular was the main producing of large hail, up to baseball size, that fell from shortly after sunrise in far west-central Minnesota, to east-central Minnesota, north of the Twin Cities, by 10 AM. This storm continued to pulse up and down from tops as high as 60,000 feet. Damage was widespread where the large hailstone fell.

Nearly 100% of the damage witnessed in Stevens county was consistent with a straight line wind event, with just one tornado crossing the southeast corner of the county.  ||Ten tornadoes were confirmed this day, with two in Lac Qui Parle County, including a house in Bellingham that had much of its roof removed.  In northwestern Swift County, a tornado tipped over trailers and various farm equipment was heavily damaged.  Two tornadoes were in Todd County, including one that moved through Clarissa.  Two more were in Morrison County, and one was on the east side of Lake Alexander, where many trees came down on vehicles, sheds, and cabins. A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were uprooted northeast of Morris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of Thursday, May 12th, was a classic bow echo that surged north and east across western Minnesota. ||Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dew points, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area.||Winds were measured by wind sensors at over 100 mph across portions of South Dakota, with a measured wind gust at Madison, Minnesota of 94 mph. There were also numerous measured wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph across west central and southwest Minnesota with embedded surges that produced a few tornadoes. The most concentrated area of damage based on reports of farm equipment, trailers, semis, trees, power lines, and other debris, occurred near the upper Minnesota River Valley, northward to Alexandria and Long Prairie.||Over Stevens County alone, structural damage was considerable as the majority of the damage was to farm outbuildings, sheds and grain bins. There were a few locations in the county where the main inhabited house or home structure did suffer loss of roof and siding and windows but those were more isolated in nature. Many locations had debris tossed a mile northeast from its parent location. Nearly 100% of the damage witnessed in Stevens county was consistent with a straight line wind event, with just one tornado crossing the southeast corner of the county.  ||Ten tornadoes were confirmed this day, with two in Lac Qui Parle County, including a house in Bellingham that had much of its roof removed.  In northwestern Swift County, a tornado tipped over trailers and various farm equipment was heavily damaged.  Two tornadoes were in Todd County, including one that moved through Clarissa.  Two more were in Morrison County, and one was on the east side of Lake Alexander, where many trees came down on vehicles, sheds, and cabins. A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down around the city of Granite Falls.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado developed in Swift County, about 5 miles southwest of Danvers, and tracked north until an outflow boundary caught up with it, then the tornado tracked northeast to 7 miles northwest of Clontarf. It then moved through the southeast corner of Stevens County and into Pope County, where it dissipated. Several wooden poles with electrical transmission lines were snapped. Irrigation systems were overturned, trees were damaged, and some structural damage to homes and farm outbuildings also resulted. Maximum winds were estimated at 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of storms developed across Iowa during the afternoon of Saturday, April 23rd. These storms were moving quickly northward just ahead of a cold front that was moving eastward across Minnesota. As these storms moved into south central Minnesota, isolated damage occurred northeast of Janesville. Several surges along the reflectivity gradient line provided sporadic tree and farm outbuilding damage between Janesville and  Owatonna, northeastward to Red Wing. The strongest circulation was noted south of Cannon Falls, before moving northeastward over the Mississippi River near Red Wing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down. The tree landed on a parked vehicle and damaged it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low, and the associated negatively tilted trough, led to the development of severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening of May 8. These storms persisted into the morning of May 9, producing severe wind, hail and tornadoes.

Two rounds of storms moved across southeast Minnesota on May 19th. The first occurred from late morning through the middle of the afternoon and produced primarily large hail across Mower and Olmsted Counties. The largest reported hail with this activity was ping pong ball sized near Lyle (Mower County), Adams (Mower County), south of Rochester (Olmsted County) and east of Rochester. The second round of storms also produced hail, although not as large as the earlier storms. The largest reported hail in the late afternoon was quarter sized north of Genoa (Olmsted County). A large tree, estimated to be four feet in diameter, was blown down near Whalan (Fillmore County). Flash flooding across eastern Winona County caused several mud slides, washouts and some cars were submerged in water. Winona County declared a state of emergency and the Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance to help with the recovery.

An area of thunderstorms moved across portions of east-central Minnesota during the afternoon hours of the 11th. One of the storms did produce minor tree damage in Pine County, but otherwise the storms were not severe. A wake low developed on the backside of the storms and let to strong winds that moved across the Aitkin area. These winds produced extensive damage at the Aitkin County fairgrounds along with knocking down numerous trees and removing roofs from sheds. Additionally, storms persisted through the night leading to isolated reports of flash flooding along the North Shore the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong wind gust lofted a 70 pound garbage can into the air at least 90 feet passing over pine trees 2 miles WSW of Aitkin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the Northland during the afternoon of the 18th. Storms developed ahead of an associated cold front and turned into a line of storms that worked across much of northeastern Minnesota and into northwest Wisconsin. The storms were mainly wind producers with numerous reports of trees down and minor structure damage as the moved through. Some hail was reported as well, but was mainly around an inch or less. An embedded EF-0 tornado was confirmed just north of the Duluth area and produced mainly tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northward from the Central Plains to the Northern Plains on May 12th and lifted a warm front across the Northland bringing two rounds of storms to the region. The first round during the afternoon hours were prolific hail producing storms with the largest hail stones being ping pong ball sized. Another round of storms arrived during the early evening hours and contained strong winds that moved through the Brainerd Lakes area. A maximum gust of 61 mph was recorded at the Brainerd Airport leading to widespread tree damage in the area. As storms continued overnight, heavy rainfall along the North Shore led to Flash Flooding in Lake and Cook counties with numerous road washouts reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local TV meteorologist forwarded photos from a viewer showing a culvert that had washed out on Hill Road near Floodwood. There was a roughly 6 foot gap in the road that was at least 3 to 4 feet deep where the flow of the creek exceeded the capacity of the culvert and washed out the road. The viewers that sent the photos were unable to leave their property by vehicle and had to make a makeshift footbridge to get to town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of Monday, May 9th, a small complex of thunderstorms that developed across eastern South Dakota moved eastward and affected a large area of west-central, central and east-central Minnesota before moving into west-central Wisconsin late Monday morning. Although scattered thunderstorms developed south and north of this main supercell storm, this storm in particular was the main producing of large hail, up to baseball size, that fell from shortly after sunrise in far west-central Minnesota, to east-central Minnesota, north of the Twin Cities, by 10 AM. This storm continued to pulse up and down from tops as high as 60,000 feet. Damage was widespread where the large hailstone fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a social media picture of golf ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms produced large hail stones west of Granite Falls, near Olivia, and northeast toward the northwest suburbs of the Twin Cities. Most of these storms were elevated but had a steep lapse rate to generate large hail, up to 2 inches in diameter at times. ||These late morning, and early afternoon storms moved northeast out of Minnesota. A warm front lifted northward across southern Minnesota during the afternoon with a potent shortwave moving northeast out of Nebraska. This shortwave generated a large complex of storms and quickly moved northeast across southern Minnesota and produced numerous damaging wind gusts, tornadoes and hail. There were even some flooding concerns as three to five inches of rain fell across portions of west central and central Minnesota by the late evening as the storms diminished and moved off to the northeast.||Three tornadoes occurred within the large bow echo that surged from southwest Minnesota, northeast toward east central Minnesota. One occurred in Brown County, another in Sibley and Carver County, and a third in Anoka County. All of the tornadoes were rated EF0 and produced mainly tree and roof damage.||According to Xcel Energy, more than 75,000 customers were without power across southern Minnesota during the peak of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down from Falcon Heights to Roseville. Some of the trees were over 100 years old and several areas had power outages due to the falling debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were several lines of strong to occasionally severe thunderstorms that moved northward across southern Minnesota, and into portions of central Minnesota, and west central Wisconsin. The largest hail stones occurred in far southern Minnesota where deep layer shear and ample instability existed. Some of the largest hailstones occurred near Mankato, and south of Mankato toward the Iowa border, where there were a few reports of measured golf ball and half dollar size hail.  There were also numerous reports of smaller hail stones across southern Minnesota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northward from the Central Plains to the Northern Plains on May 12th and lifted a warm front across the Northland bringing two rounds of storms to the region. The first round during the afternoon hours were prolific hail producing storms with the largest hail stones being ping pong ball sized. Another round of storms arrived during the early evening hours and contained strong winds that moved through the Brainerd Lakes area. A maximum gust of 61 mph was recorded at the Brainerd Airport leading to widespread tree damage in the area. As storms continued overnight, heavy rainfall along the North Shore led to Flash Flooding in Lake and Cook counties with numerous road washouts reported.

A cluster of storms produced large hail stones west of Granite Falls, near Olivia, and northeast toward the northwest suburbs of the Twin Cities. Most of these storms were elevated but had a steep lapse rate to generate large hail, up to 2 inches in diameter at times. ||These late morning, and early afternoon storms moved northeast out of Minnesota. A warm front lifted northward across southern Minnesota during the afternoon with a potent shortwave moving northeast out of Nebraska. This shortwave generated a large complex of storms and quickly moved northeast across southern Minnesota and produced numerous damaging wind gusts, tornadoes and hail. There were even some flooding concerns as three to five inches of rain fell across portions of west central and central Minnesota by the late evening as the storms diminished and moved off to the northeast.||Three tornadoes occurred within the large bow echo that surged from southwest Minnesota, northeast toward east central Minnesota. One occurred in Brown County, another in Sibley and Carver County, and a third in Anoka County. All of the tornadoes were rated EF0 and produced mainly tree and roof damage.||According to Xcel Energy, more than 75,000 customers were without power across southern Minnesota during the peak of the storm.

During the early morning hours of May 30th, a few discrete/semi-discrete supercells developed across central MN producing low-end severe hail. However, the main focus of the event was a QLCS moving northeast from FSD���s CWA into south-central to central Minnesota. After crossing into the MPX CWA in southwest Minnesota, this feature transitioned to more of a line/bow echo but no damaging wind reports were observed, likely due to the large amount of MLCIN in southern Minnesota. However, lesser MLCIN and slightly better low-level lapse rates across central MN allowed for the bow echo's  winds to start reaching the surface with an 84 mph wind gust measured at the Olivia AWOS. A grain bin was blown away near Sacred Heart as well as some trees and power lines downed near Belgrade.

An area of low pressure moving out of the Northern Plains on the 17th brought heavy snow to portions of the North Shore. Favorable southeasterly winds streaming across Lake Superior into the higher elevations along the shore led to increased snowfall during the overnight hours of the 17th into the 18th. Snowfall upwards of an inch per hour were briefly observed with snow totals of just over 7 inches being recorded northeast of Grand Marias.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports of 4 to 6 inches were received across the area with a high report of 6.5 inches at Wolf Ridge near Finland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted into the Northland on the 22nd leading to the development of elevated thunderstorms during the afternoon hours. Most of these storms remained sub-severe, but one storm was able to produce quarter-sized hail over northern St. Louis County.

A line of thunderstorms developed late Tuesday morning across southwest Minnesota and quickly moved eastward over southern Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin by mid afternoon. One severe storm became severe near Waldorf and caused sporadic wind damage across rural Waseca County. ||Another strong storm became severe east of Mankato as strong wind shear and ample elevated instability produced large hail near Cannon Falls. There were numerous reports of small hail from northeast of Mankato, through Faribault and the Red Wing area. The largest hail stone fell near Northfield and was measured at 1.50 inches in diameter. ||There was also significant damage to areas southwest, south and southeast of the city of Faribault.  Damage included numerous trees down, power lines down, power line poles sheared off, trees on power lines, roofs partially off houses, some sheds, pole barns destroyed and pieces blown over long distances.  In addition to the wind damage, straight-line winds caused 9 semi trucks to be blown over on I-35, just south of Faribault.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half of the shingles were blown off a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep area of low pressure that was centered across southern North Dakota late Wednesday night, slowly moved eastward across northern Minnesota on Thursday. On the south side of this area of low pressure, a strong pressure gradient created winds in excess of 40-45 mph sustained across a wide area of western and southern Minnesota. Several gusts in the 50 to 55 mph range also occurred with the strongest wind gust of 59 mph at the Redwood Falls Airport Thursday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind sensors across Lac Qui Parle County reported sustained winds in excess of 45 to 50 mph, with gusts near 55 mph at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were much closer to normal in October, precipitation was generally less than 25 percent of normal, leading to the maintenance of long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite near normal temperatures, precipitation near Lakefield was less than 10 percent of normal during October.  Severe (D2) drought conditions developed mid-month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistence of above normal temperatures and minimal precipitation maintained or worsened long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Lake Wilson, precipitation was less than 25 percent of normal with temperatures of 3 to 4 degrees above normal during September. Moderate (D1) drought conditions worsened to severe (D2) after mid-month. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed toward midnight on leading elevated warm advection across south central South Dakota and progressed rapidly northeast through areas north of Interstate 90.  Large amounts of DCAPE in the air mass resulted in spotty damaging wind.  A second cluster of storms developed on the tail end of the initial storms on the nose of an increasing low-level jet, using up to 2000 J/kg of elevated instability to rapidly become severe.  Very large hail resulted as storms first developed supercellular characteristics, and then a mix of hail and damaging wind as short bowing segments evolved moving into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved across southeast Minnesota on May 19th. The first occurred from late morning through the middle of the afternoon and produced primarily large hail across Mower and Olmsted Counties. The largest reported hail with this activity was ping pong ball sized near Lyle (Mower County), Adams (Mower County), south of Rochester (Olmsted County) and east of Rochester. The second round of storms also produced hail, although not as large as the earlier storms. The largest reported hail in the late afternoon was quarter sized north of Genoa (Olmsted County). A large tree, estimated to be four feet in diameter, was blown down near Whalan (Fillmore County). Flash flooding across eastern Winona County caused several mud slides, washouts and some cars were submerged in water. Winona County declared a state of emergency and the Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance to help with the recovery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Stockton, 3.54 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the Northland during the afternoon of the 18th. Storms developed ahead of an associated cold front and turned into a line of storms that worked across much of northeastern Minnesota and into northwest Wisconsin. The storms were mainly wind producers with numerous reports of trees down and minor structure damage as the moved through. Some hail was reported as well, but was mainly around an inch or less. An embedded EF-0 tornado was confirmed just north of the Duluth area and produced mainly tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of trees were found to be bent or uprooted in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A damaging wind event unfolded across west-central Minnesota during the evening of June 20 and into the overnight hours of June 21. Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dewpoints, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area. Numerous wind gusts of 60-70 kts were measured across west-central Minnesota, along with widespread tree damage. One fatality occurred when a tree fell on a camper in Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree branches were blown down.

During the morning of Monday, May 9th, a small complex of thunderstorms that developed across eastern South Dakota moved eastward and affected a large area of west-central, central and east-central Minnesota before moving into west-central Wisconsin late Monday morning. Although scattered thunderstorms developed south and north of this main supercell storm, this storm in particular was the main producing of large hail, up to baseball size, that fell from shortly after sunrise in far west-central Minnesota, to east-central Minnesota, north of the Twin Cities, by 10 AM. This storm continued to pulse up and down from tops as high as 60,000 feet. Damage was widespread where the large hailstone fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were parts of a grain bin that blew across railroad tracks near Hanley Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were several lines of strong to occasionally severe thunderstorms that moved northward across southern Minnesota, and into portions of central Minnesota, and west central Wisconsin. The largest hail stones occurred in far southern Minnesota where deep layer shear and ample instability existed. Some of the largest hailstones occurred near Mankato, and south of Mankato toward the Iowa border, where there were a few reports of measured golf ball and half dollar size hail.  There were also numerous reports of smaller hail stones across southern Minnesota.

A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops,  powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes. ||In addition, a separate cluster of thunderstorms ahead of the derecho produced very large hail in portions of southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail accompanied strong thunderstorm wind gusts. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 90 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Cottonwood and Nobles Counties for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving out of the Central Plains brought a warm front through northern Minnesota during the morning hours of the 9th. Supported by a strong low level jet and warm air pushing into the region, storms spread north through the morning hours. Storms produced hail as large as gold balls and damaging winds before losing strength during the late morning as they moved into the Arrowhead.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several pine trees approximately 12 to 14 inches in diameter were snapped near the trunk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved across southeast Minnesota on May 19th. The first occurred from late morning through the middle of the afternoon and produced primarily large hail across Mower and Olmsted Counties. The largest reported hail with this activity was ping pong ball sized near Lyle (Mower County), Adams (Mower County), south of Rochester (Olmsted County) and east of Rochester. The second round of storms also produced hail, although not as large as the earlier storms. The largest reported hail in the late afternoon was quarter sized north of Genoa (Olmsted County). A large tree, estimated to be four feet in diameter, was blown down near Whalan (Fillmore County). Flash flooding across eastern Winona County caused several mud slides, washouts and some cars were submerged in water. Winona County declared a state of emergency and the Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance to help with the recovery.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport measured a 63 mph gust.

A state of emergency was declared and the Governor of Minnesota authorized emergency assistance to help with the recovery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northward from the Central Plains to the Northern Plains on May 12th and lifted a warm front across the Northland bringing two rounds of storms to the region. The first round during the afternoon hours were prolific hail producing storms with the largest hail stones being ping pong ball sized. Another round of storms arrived during the early evening hours and contained strong winds that moved through the Brainerd Lakes area. A maximum gust of 61 mph was recorded at the Brainerd Airport leading to widespread tree damage in the area. As storms continued overnight, heavy rainfall along the North Shore led to Flash Flooding in Lake and Cook counties with numerous road washouts reported.

An area of low pressure moved across the Northland during the afternoon of the 18th. Storms developed ahead of an associated cold front and turned into a line of storms that worked across much of northeastern Minnesota and into northwest Wisconsin. The storms were mainly wind producers with numerous reports of trees down and minor structure damage as the moved through. Some hail was reported as well, but was mainly around an inch or less. An embedded EF-0 tornado was confirmed just north of the Duluth area and produced mainly tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado tracked approximately west to east from north of the intersection of Lismore Road and Arnold Road, across Eagle Lake Road, and then across Jean Duluth Road before ending near Lady Nicole Lane east of Jean Duluth Road. This tornado caused numerous tree damage along its path, with a consistent tree fall pattern indicative of a tornado, but was unlikely above EF-0 in strength. While a few trees were snapped near the base, the vast majority of tree damage was large branches and trees that have bent over, with a few uprooted trees. The estimated maximum wind speed of this tornado was 85 mph. There was no known property damage outside of the tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the morning of Monday, May 9th, a small complex of thunderstorms that developed across eastern South Dakota moved eastward and affected a large area of west-central, central and east-central Minnesota before moving into west-central Wisconsin late Monday morning. Although scattered thunderstorms developed south and north of this main supercell storm, this storm in particular was the main producing of large hail, up to baseball size, that fell from shortly after sunrise in far west-central Minnesota, to east-central Minnesota, north of the Twin Cities, by 10 AM. This storm continued to pulse up and down from tops as high as 60,000 feet. Damage was widespread where the large hailstone fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a social media picture of 3 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There were several lines of strong to occasionally severe thunderstorms that moved northward across southern Minnesota, and into portions of central Minnesota, and west central Wisconsin. The largest hail stones occurred in far southern Minnesota where deep layer shear and ample instability existed. Some of the largest hailstones occurred near Mankato, and south of Mankato toward the Iowa border, where there were a few reports of measured golf ball and half dollar size hail.  There were also numerous reports of smaller hail stones across southern Minnesota.

Scattered storms moved across portions of southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of April 23rd. These storms dropped hail up to quarter size in Kellogg (Wabasha County).

Heavy rain fell across portions of southeast Minnesota from April 12th into the 13th. Runoff from this rain caused some minor river flooding to occur on the Whitewater River in Whitewater State Park (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff after a heavy rain event pushed the Whitewater River out of its banks in the Whitewater State Park. The river crested almost a foot above flood stage at 14.3 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient winds developed throughout the late forenoon and afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong sustained winds of 40 to 45 mph, with gusts from 55 to 65 mph were reported by Airport, NDAWN, and DOT RWIS stations in Northwest MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed late Tuesday morning across southwest Minnesota and quickly moved eastward over southern Minnesota, and into west central Wisconsin by mid afternoon. One severe storm became severe near Waldorf and caused sporadic wind damage across rural Waseca County. ||Another strong storm became severe east of Mankato as strong wind shear and ample elevated instability produced large hail near Cannon Falls. There were numerous reports of small hail from northeast of Mankato, through Faribault and the Red Wing area. The largest hail stone fell near Northfield and was measured at 1.50 inches in diameter. ||There was also significant damage to areas southwest, south and southeast of the city of Faribault.  Damage included numerous trees down, power lines down, power line poles sheared off, trees on power lines, roofs partially off houses, some sheds, pole barns destroyed and pieces blown over long distances.  In addition to the wind damage, straight-line winds caused 9 semi trucks to be blown over on I-35, just south of Faribault.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi truck was blown off the road north of Randolph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passing to the north of northeastern Minnesota created conditions favorable for snow squalls. Gusty northwest winds and convective snow showers developed during the afternoon hours and affected portions of the Arrowhead of Minnesota, primarily in Cook and Lake counties. Visibility from webcams in the area was less than 1/4 mile at times in the heaviest snow squalls. Satellite and radar imagery revealed the convective nature of the snow showers and increased confidence that the squalls would persist for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. Wind gusts of 30 to 35 mph and heavy snowfall led to visibilities around 1/2 mile or less in areas along the Gunflint Trail. Webcams showed visibility as low as 1/4 mile or less at times during the heaviest squalls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe storms moved across southeast Minnesota on April 12th. The first round occurred in the afternoon as storms producing large hail moved across the area. Quarter sized hail fell from north of Racine (Mower County) east to near Winona (Winona County). A second round of severe storms occurred in the evening. An EF2 tornado moved through Taopi (Mower County), where homes were destroyed or severely damaged, grain bins were destroyed, cars were tossed or rolled, and trees and power lines were downed. Two people sustained injuries while sheltering from the storm when they became trapped under debris. An EF1 tornado occurred just southwest of Spring Valley (Fillmore County), damaging several farm outbuildings and trees. Two EF0 tornadoes occurred southwest of Lewiston (Winona County), where the damage was mainly to trees and one farm. The Mower County Board of Commissioners ratified an emergency declaration for Taopi and a state of emergency was declared for Fillmore County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF2 tornado formed southwest of Taopi and quickly intensified and caused significant damage in Taopi. Most buildings were damaged and 10 were destroyed. Cars were tossed, grain bins destroyed, and numerous trees and power lines downed. Two people were injured seeking shelter from the storm when they became trapped under debris. Just outside of Taopi, part of the roof was removed from a farmhouse, several outbuildings were damaged, and several head of cattle were missing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-level jet gradually weakened early morning, but sustained a back-building cluster of thunderstorms across southwest Minnesota within an environment of 1500 J/kg MUCAPE and 40 knots of effective shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

In addition to the wind damage, straight-line winds caused 9 semi trucks to be blown over on I-35, just south of Faribault.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was significant wind damage from a downburst that expanded in width as it traveled northeast. By the time it stopped producing damage, it was 8 wide on either side of Nerstrand.  The damage began east-southeast of Morristown (near the Rice, Waseca, and Steele County line), moved across the south side of Faribault, and northeast to near the Nerstrand Big Woods State Park and then Nerstrand. The damage path became more isolated as it exited eastern Rice county.||Numerous trees, power lines, roofs, and pole barns were damaged along this path. In addition, nine semi trucks were blown over on I-35 south of Faribault due to straight-line winds.  Just over $1 Million dollars in damages were to public infrastructure, with remaining damages to houses, farms, and vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utilities reported a loss of $30,000 due to downed power poles.

A significant hail event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Southeast-Central Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A multicellular convective mode advanced in two waves with the first wave occurring through the morning hours in Southern MN, while the second wave produced a long-track supercell over the Twin Cities metro and Central Wisconsin. Both waves produced significant hail and damaging wind gusts. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon produced countless hailstones across the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. Beyond the typical 1 inch diameter hailstones, there were several hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter that fell over Washington County. A few other large hailstones fell over Le Sueur, Dakota and Goodhue Counties of southern Minnesota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of Thursday, May 12th, was a classic bow echo that surged north and east across western Minnesota. ||Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dew points, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area.||Winds were measured by wind sensors at over 100 mph across portions of South Dakota, with a measured wind gust at Madison, Minnesota of 94 mph. There were also numerous measured wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph across west central and southwest Minnesota with embedded surges that produced a few tornadoes. The most concentrated area of damage based on reports of farm equipment, trailers, semis, trees, power lines, and other debris, occurred near the upper Minnesota River Valley, northward to Alexandria and Long Prairie.||Over Stevens County alone, structural damage was considerable as the majority of the damage was to farm outbuildings, sheds and grain bins. There were a few locations in the county where the main inhabited house or home structure did suffer loss of roof and siding and windows but those were more isolated in nature. Many locations had debris tossed a mile northeast from its parent location. Nearly 100% of the damage witnessed in Stevens county was consistent with a straight line wind event, with just one tornado crossing the southeast corner of the county.  ||Ten tornadoes were confirmed this day, with two in Lac Qui Parle County, including a house in Bellingham that had much of its roof removed.  In northwestern Swift County, a tornado tipped over trailers and various farm equipment was heavily damaged.  Two tornadoes were in Todd County, including one that moved through Clarissa.  Two more were in Morrison County, and one was on the east side of Lake Alexander, where many trees came down on vehicles, sheds, and cabins. A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fast moving tornado (storms were moving northeast at 70 mph) touched down near Donna Drive and West Meadow Lane on the northwest edge of Alexandria, then tracked across Lake Darling before hitting land again along County Road 11. Numerous houses were hit on the south side of Lake Darling. After crossing Lake Darling, the tornado damage was mostly to trees, with less damage directly to houses, as the tornado was becoming weaker in this area. It dissipated on land shortly after moving across a small part of the southwest portion of Lake Carlos.  Maximum winds were estimated at 110 mph.

A cold front plowed into an environment with roughly 1500 J/kg MLCAPE but weak shear, which resulted in numerous thunderstorms with brief intense updrafts. The strongest storms produced isolated damaging wind gusts and hail up to golf ball size.  By evening, a stronger low-level flow ahead of the front resulted in a slightly stronger storm with hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter across northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in south central South Dakota the last half of the night, and moved northeast along the nose of a strong low-level jet toward east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota after sunrise. Elevated instability of 1000-2000J/kg and deep-layer elevated shear of 30-40 knots led to organization of storms into multicell structures capable of spotty large hail and damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the afternoon and early evening on May 30th across southwest Minnesota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

A warm front lifted northward across southern Minnesota during the afternoon with a potent shortwave moving northeast out of Nebraska. This shortwave generated a large complex of storms and quickly moved northeast across southern Minnesota and produced numerous damaging wind gusts, tornadoes and hail. There were even some flooding concerns as three to five inches of rain fell across portions of west central and central Minnesota by the late evening as the storms diminished and moved off to the northeast.||Three tornadoes occurred within the large bow echo that surged from southwest Minnesota, northeast toward east central Minnesota. One occurred in Brown County, another in Sibley and Carver County, and a third in Anoka County. All of the tornadoes were rated EF0 and produced mainly tree and roof damage.||According to Xcel Energy, more than 75,000 customers were without power across southern Minnesota during the peak of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant hail event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Southeast-Central Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A multicellular convective mode advanced in two waves with the first wave occurring through the morning hours in Southern MN, while the second wave produced a long-track supercell over the Twin Cities metro and Central Wisconsin. Both waves produced significant hail and damaging wind gusts. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon produced countless hailstones across the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. Beyond the typical 1 inch diameter hailstones, there were several hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter that fell over Washington County. A few other large hailstones fell over Le Sueur, Dakota and Goodhue Counties of southern Minnesota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant hail event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Southeast-Central Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A multicellular convective mode advanced in two waves with the first wave occurring through the morning hours in Southern MN, while the second wave produced a long-track supercell over the Twin Cities metro and Central Wisconsin. Both waves produced significant hail and damaging wind gusts. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon produced countless hailstones across the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. Beyond the typical 1 inch diameter hailstones, there were several hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter that fell over Washington County. A few other large hailstones fell over Le Sueur, Dakota and Goodhue Counties of southern Minnesota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of D2 Severe Drought persisted across parts of northeastern Minnesota to begin the month of March. These areas received near normal precipitation over the course of the month and, when combined with snow melt across the region, all areas of D2 Severe Drought were removed by the end of the month, leaving widespread D0 Abnormally Dry conditions and and small areas of D1 Moderate Drought, mainly where D2 Severe Drought had been.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much of the northern part of the county began the month with D1 Moderate Drought in place with a small area of D2 Severe Drought in place across the northwestern part of the county. Precipitation was generally near to slightly below normal over the course of the month, but, when combined with winter snow melt, this was enough to recharge the soil and lead to the removal of D2 Severe Drought across the county with the update on the 29th.  This left mainly D0 Abnormally Dry conditions in place across the county with an area of D1 Moderate Dry in the northwestern corner.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Much of northern Cool County began the month with D2 Severe Drought with the northeastern corner of Lake County in D2 Severe Drought. D1 Moderate Drought was in place elsewhere across northern Lake and Cook Counties. Precipitation was generally near to slightly above  normal over the course of the month, and, when combined with winter snow melt, this was enough to recharge the soil and lead to the removal of D2 Severe Drought across the area with the update on the 29th following a slight reduction in area with the update on the 8th.  This left mainly D1 Moderate Drought in place across the county with D0 Abnormally Dry conditions in far southern areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low moving area of low pressure tracked from eastern South Dakota on the morning of March 21 to northeastern Iowa the morning of the 22nd and then southeastern Iowa by the 23rd. This led to a period of snowfall and freezing rain across much of northeastern Minnesota on the 22nd into the 23rd. Winds off Lake Superior led to some lake and terrain enhancement along the North Shore with totals in excess of a foot near Grand Marais. Some areas also saw a period of freezing rain in addition to the snow with the Ely area receiving 0.20 inches of ice in addition to around 6 inches of snowfall. Temperatures to the south across the Brainerd Lakes area into northwestern Wisconsin were warm enough so that more in the way of liquid rainfall fell as opposed to snow and mix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: International Falls recorded 13.1 inches of snow by the time the storm ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving low pressure moved from the Dakotas early on the 29th to central Iowa the morning of the 30th. This low track spread a mix of rain, snow and wintry mix across much of the Northland. While most of northeastern Minnesota received sub-warnings conditions during the storm, some heavier snow was received across northern Itasca County where 7.5 was recorded. Additionally, about a quarter of an inch of freezing rain was received along part of the North Shore with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of freezing rain along the North Shore was recorded during the early morning hours of the 30th. A quarter of an inch of ice was reported near Castle Danger in about a 6 to 8 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low-pressure system impacted the region during the afternoon and evening hours of May 30th. A larger scale Derecho tracked from far southeastern South Dakota, through much of western through northcentral Minnesota. Widespread damage was reported from damaging winds, and tornadoes. Hail up to 1.5 inches was reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind measured at the Park Rapids Airport, KPKD. Numerous pine and poplar trees were snapped or uprooted across Todd and Arago Townships, from Fish Hook Lake northward through Potato, Eagle, Island, and Cedar Lakes. Time ranged between 529 and 539 pm CDT based on radar, nearby wind sensors, and public reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging wind gusts, and tornadoes during the afternoon of June 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado tracked for less than a minute through an open field. The ground based dust swirl was filmed or photographed by at least three spotters. A persistent and low wall cloud structure was tracked into the area and was above the swirl, with only a brief condensation cone apparent. First reported via social media and through broadcast media sources. Time estimated based on radar, along with photo and video records. Peak winds estimated to 70 mph, with a maximum width to 75 yards.

A larger scale Derecho tracked from far southeastern South Dakota, through much of western through northcentral Minnesota. Widespread damage was reported from damaging winds, and tornadoes. Hail up to 1.5 inches was reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large pine, ash, and poplar trees were snapped or uprooted across Clover and Lake Alice Townships. One or more snapped trees were evident along Highway 71 every 100 yards or less through these two townships. Power was lost to the MnDOT RWIS station along Hwy 71, between Douglas Lodge and Lake George. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed during the afternoon, moving across the southern and central portions of the Red River Valley, then into west-central and northwest Minnesota. Wind gusts to 85 mph were reported, along with 2 tornadoes, and numerous other reports of hail and high wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several boat docks were flipped, trees were snapped or uprooted, and a camper trailer was blown over on the southeastern shore of Otter Tail Lake. Time estimated based on radar and nearby wind sensor reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant hail event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Southeast-Central Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A multicellular convective mode advanced in two waves with the first wave occurring through the morning hours in Southern MN, while the second wave produced a long-track supercell over the Twin Cities metro and Central Wisconsin. Both waves produced significant hail and damaging wind gusts. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon produced countless hailstones across the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. Beyond the typical 1 inch diameter hailstones, there were several hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter that fell over Washington County. A few other large hailstones fell over Le Sueur, Dakota and Goodhue Counties of southern Minnesota.
A severe thunderstorm moved along Highway 2 producing damaging winds and large hail on August 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a public report of a few large 3 to 5 inch branches broken down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved along Highway 2 producing damaging winds and large hail on August 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lots of dime to nickel size hail with a few as large as quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved along Highway 2 producing damaging winds and large hail on August 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail across Northern Cormant Township, up to quarter size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm moved along Highway 2 producing damaging winds and large hail on August 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak winds at the fosston airport. Wind gusts continued through 417 pm.

A cluster of severe thunderstorms that developed over South Dakota early Thursday morning, moved across west-central, and central Minnesota during the morning. Most of the hail fell across the central third of Minnesota, with some hail stones measured at 2 inches in diameter. In addition, a few mesonet wind sensors and the St. Cloud regional airport all reported severe wind gusts. There was also wind damage near Cold Spring and in central Morrison County where power outages occurred.

A small cluster of thunderstorms developed over south central Minnesota during the pre-dawn hours of Wednesday, May 11th. These storms quickly became severe near Elysian, and moved northward. This storm continued to move northward toward the Twin Cities but the severity diminished after New Prague and only small hail was reported in the southern suburbs of the Twin Cities. Another cluster of storms developed between Faribault and Owatonna and moved eastward before becoming severe over southern Goodhue County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall was measured at 1.7 inches. Some minor street flooding occurred in Owatonna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure across the Northern Plains lifted a warm front across the Northland on Memorial Day morning. Showers and storms moved through during the morning hours with the atmosphere recovering during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms were severe during the morning and produced small hail and tree damage. An arc of thunderstorms developed across southwestern Minnesota and adjacent areas of Nebraska, South Dakota and Iowa by the mid-afternoon hours and moved to the northeast and across the Northland for the late afternoon and evening hours. These storms were much stronger and contained very strong winds that produced widespread damage across the region. Additionally, there were a few embedded tornadoes with the storms with the strongest tornadoes producing EF-1 damage in the Deer River and Hinckley areas.

A line of severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of Thursday, May 12th, was a classic bow echo that surged north and east across western Minnesota. ||Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dew points, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area.||Winds were measured by wind sensors at over 100 mph across portions of South Dakota, with a measured wind gust at Madison, Minnesota of 94 mph. There were also numerous measured wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph across west central and southwest Minnesota with embedded surges that produced a few tornadoes. The most concentrated area of damage based on reports of farm equipment, trailers, semis, trees, power lines, and other debris, occurred near the upper Minnesota River Valley, northward to Alexandria and Long Prairie.||Over Stevens County alone, structural damage was considerable as the majority of the damage was to farm outbuildings, sheds and grain bins. There were a few locations in the county where the main inhabited house or home structure did suffer loss of roof and siding and windows but those were more isolated in nature. Many locations had debris tossed a mile northeast from its parent location. Nearly 100% of the damage witnessed in Stevens county was consistent with a straight line wind event, with just one tornado crossing the southeast corner of the county.  ||Ten tornadoes were confirmed this day, with two in Lac Qui Parle County, including a house in Bellingham that had much of its roof removed.  In northwestern Swift County, a tornado tipped over trailers and various farm equipment was heavily damaged.  Two tornadoes were in Todd County, including one that moved through Clarissa.  Two more were in Morrison County, and one was on the east side of Lake Alexander, where many trees came down on vehicles, sheds, and cabins. A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down and a steel building was damaged near the intersection of 40th Street and Highway 29 south of Benson. Dozens of downed trees were scattered across Benson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry-line/cold front was moving across Minnesota during the afternoon of Monday, May 9th. A few thunderstorms developed along this front across central Minnesota, and quickly spread south and eastward during the late afternoon and evening over eastern Minnesota, and adjacent west central Wisconsin. A brief funnel cloud was observed south of Gilman, and a tornado impacted Dakota County between Hastings and Miesville. Large hail and a few damaging wind gusts occurred along this line as it moved eastward. The largest hail stone was 2 inches in diameter and occurred near St. Paul downtown airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a social media picture of 1.5 inch hail.

Shingles and metal roofing panels were torn off of houses and farm buildings and were spread downwind. Peak wind estimated to 90 mph. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring snowmelt, record April precipitation and frequent May rain events led to strong hydrologic responses within the Rainy Lake basin. Namakan Lake, Kabetogama Lake and Rainy Lake rose above the flood of record during the months of May and June before subsiding in July and returning to levels closer to normal. These high lake levels led to significant damage to homes and businesses along the shoreline along with docks and other related structures. Additionally, some roads were covered in water with one being temporarily raised to maintain access to a few resorts. In addition to the costs of the damage itself, many resorts in the area suffered from lost revenue by having to close during the beginning of peak tourist season in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal snowfall for the 2021-2022 season was observed across northern portions of Koochiching and St. Louis counties and much of that remained on the ground into April. Heavy rainfall then fell on top of that above normal snowpack at the end of April and sent abnormally high amounts of runoff into the Rainy River Basin leading to rises on Rainy Lake. International Falls experienced their wettest April on record, helping to set the stage for the flooding. By the end of May, the level of Rainy Lake reached 1112.4 feet which was 12 inches above the 2014 peak level and 7 inches below the all-time record set in 1950. The level of Namakan Lake and Kabatogama Lake reached 1122.2 feet which was 20.5 inches above the 2014 peak level and 7 inches below the all-time record set in 1916. The high lake levels led to extensive damage to properties adjacent to the water along with damage to docks and other infrastructure. Lake levels would continue to rise into June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.

By the end of May, the level of Rainy Lake reached 1112.4 feet which was 12 inches above the 2014 peak level and 7 inches below the all-time record set in 1950. The high lake levels led to extensive damage to properties adjacent to the water along with damage to docks and other infrastructure. Lake levels would continue to rise into June.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was wrapped in damaging downburst winds and rain as it rapidly tracked to the northeast from about 2 NW of the Staples Airport to about 2 NNE of the Staples Airport. It appears to have developed roughly half a mile east of an earlier tornado, based on divergent tree damage paths and supported by radar depictions. It snapped several poplar, ash, and spruce trees along its path. Peak winds to 90 mph. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado developed about 3 miles north northwest of Milan. It uprooted several trees and then moved into Swift County where it significantly damaged farm outbuildings. Maximum winds for the Chippewa County portion were estimated at 70 mph.

A significant hail event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Southeast-Central Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A multicellular convective mode advanced in two waves with the first wave occurring through the morning hours in Southern MN, while the second wave produced a long-track supercell over the Twin Cities metro and Central Wisconsin. Both waves produced significant hail and damaging wind gusts. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon produced countless hailstones across the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. Beyond the typical 1 inch diameter hailstones, there were several hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter that fell over Washington County. A few other large hailstones fell over Le Sueur, Dakota and Goodhue Counties of southern Minnesota.

A strong cold front and upper low traversed the area during the afternoon and evening of May 12th. During the afternoon, a larger scale Derecho moved from northeastern Nebraska, through eastern South Dakota and into portions of far southeastern North Dakota and west-central Minnesota. Widespread strong to severe thunderstorms were observed, with large hail, tornadoes, and damaging wind gusts all being reported during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail was reported, as well as numerous 4 to 6 inch diameter tree branches and limbs were broken by a damaging wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the Northland during the afternoon of the 18th. Storms developed ahead of an associated cold front and turned into a line of storms that worked across much of northeastern Minnesota and into northwest Wisconsin. The storms were mainly wind producers with numerous reports of trees down and minor structure damage as the moved through. Some hail was reported as well, but was mainly around an inch or less. An embedded EF-0 tornado was confirmed just north of the Duluth area and produced mainly tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were uprooted with a few falling on power lines leading to power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was likely wrapped in downburst winds and heavy rain as is tracked east northeastward across North Twin Lake and the community of Naytahwaush, ending around 1 ene of town by 923 pm CDT. The tornado snapped or uprooted numerous pine, poplar, and oak trees. It tore roughly 40 percent of the roof decking off of one house and shingles and roofing materials off of other buildings along its path. Peak winds were estimated to 105 mph, with a maximum width of 400 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This heat event was more pronounced than the previous one in June. Dew points pooled ahead of a frontal boundary Tuesday afternoon, August 2nd. These dew points that rose into the 70s combined with actual temperatures in the mid to upper 90s to support heat indices in the 100 to 115 degree range for several hours during the afternoon and evening of Tuesday, August 2nd. At one point Tuesday afternoon, heat indices rose to 115 degrees at Hutchinson, 114 degrees at Granite Falls, and 112 degrees at both Redwood Falls and New Ulm. Just west of the Twin Cities, heat indices were highest near Buffalo which rose to 111 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several temperature gauges measured heat indices between 105 and 114 degrees during the afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening of Tuesday, August 2nd, a complex of thunderstorms that developed over eastern South Dakota moved into southwest Minnesota. These storms quickly bowed out over Minnesota and moved east-northeast from Granite Falls, to Olivia, Hutchinson, and the western Twin Cities metro area. There were numerous measured wind gusts from Marshall, to the western Twin Cities metro area of 60 to 70 mph. There was one wind gust of 81 MPH west of Hector, Minnesota. This was measured by the Minnesota Department of Transportation wind sensor. In addition to the measured wind gusts, several large trees were blown down from Redwood Falls, Olivia, Hutchinson, and parts of the Twin Cities metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down in the Norwood, Young America area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of storms moved across southern Minnesota during the morning and afternoon of Saturday, August 27th. During the late afternoon and early evening, a stronger storm developed over south central Minnesota and quickly moved northeast and produced a bow-echo segment. This bow-echo segment took on the characteristic of a QLCS and produced several circulations across the south and southeastern Twin Cities metro area. Some of these circulations produced EF0 tornado damage from Apple Valley, northeast to Eagan, West St. Paul, and the east side of St. Paul and Maplewood. Most of the damage was associated with large trees blown down or uprooted. A few of the larger trees landed on buildings and a trailer park west of the Minnesota Zoo and caused damage to those structures.
