EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of storms that developed across western Minnesota during the late afternoon, and early evening of Sunday, August 28th. It had initially produced a brief tornado southwest of Prinsburg, Minnesota. However, as the line moved eastward, storms become more numerous, but didn't produce any more tornadoes or severe weather. Most of the storms were strong but only produced gusty non-severe winds and possibly small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chaser video showed a tornado that touched down in a field in Chippewa County, where it hit a tree and took down large branches. It continued moving across a bean field, then moved across a road and tracked into Kandiyohi County, where it entered a corn field and eventually dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that originally developed across the Arrowhead of Minnesota early Wednesday morning, August 17th, created a boundary farther southward across east-central Minnesota. This boundary, with daytime heating leading to more instability, caused additional thunderstorms to develop. Due to weak winds aloft, these storms drifted southward but continued to redevelop on the west side. This led to storms training over the same areas from Mora, southward to the eastern portion of the Twin Cities metro area. One area near Cambridge had over 4 inches of rain in less than 2 hours that led to flash flooding in the town. A local business had water damage due to the excessive rainfall. Other areas in the Twin Cities also had excessive rainfall, but only minor flooding occurred, and mainly in poor drainage areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry-line/cold front was moving across Minnesota during the afternoon of Monday, May 9th. A few thunderstorms developed along this front across central Minnesota, and quickly spread south and eastward during the late afternoon and evening over eastern Minnesota, and adjacent west central Wisconsin. A brief funnel cloud was observed south of Gilman, and a tornado impacted Dakota County between Hastings and Miesville. Large hail and a few damaging wind gusts occurred along this line as it moved eastward. The largest hail stone was 2 inches in diameter and occurred near St. Paul downtown airport.

A mesoscale convective system moved into eastern North Dakota during the morning hours of June 14th, containing isolated severe thunderstorms. Hail and damaging wind gusts were reported from the Devils Lake Basin, eastward to Grand Forks, then into west-central Minnesota. Damage was reported in Polk and Roseau Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind measured at a personal weather station; time based on radar and additional nearby wind sensors.

At one point Tuesday afternoon, heat indices rose to 115 degrees at Hutchinson, 114 degrees at Granite Falls, and 112 degrees at both Redwood Falls and New Ulm. Just west of the Twin Cities, heat indices were highest near Buffalo which rose to 111 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several temperature gauges measured heat indices between 105 and 115 degrees during the afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado formed in McLeod County (see corresponding entry) before entering Carver County. It then uprooted or broke dozens of trees. The tornado also caused significant damage to a machine shed. The tornado then moved into Wright County (see corresponding entry).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant hail event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Southeast-Central Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A multicellular convective mode advanced in two waves with the first wave occurring through the morning hours in Southern MN, while the second wave produced a long-track supercell over the Twin Cities metro and Central Wisconsin. Both waves produced significant hail and damaging wind gusts. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon produced countless hailstones across the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. Beyond the typical 1 inch diameter hailstones, there were several hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter that fell over Washington County. A few other large hailstones fell over Le Sueur, Dakota and Goodhue Counties of southern Minnesota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low-pressure system impacted the region during the afternoon and evening hours of May 30th. A larger scale Derecho tracked from far southeastern South Dakota, through much of western through northcentral Minnesota. Widespread damage was reported from damaging winds, and tornadoes. Hail up to 1.5 inches was reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Metal roofing and side panels were blown off a pole-shed with metal sheets strewn about and wrapped around trees near the intersection of State Highway 87 and CR43. Numerous poplar trees were snapped or uprooted across Spruce Grove and Runeberg Townships. Time estimated based on radar and public reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms formed during the afternoon, moving across the southern and central portions of the Red River Valley, then into west-central and northwest Minnesota. Wind gusts to 85 mph were reported, along with 2 tornadoes, and numerous other reports of hail and high wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large trees were snapped or uprooted from swan lake, down through Dalton to Ten Mile Lake in Lane Prairie and Tumuli Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved into eastern North Dakota during the morning hours of June 14th, containing isolated severe thunderstorms. Hail and damaging wind gusts were reported from the Devils Lake Basin, eastward to Grand Forks, then into west-central Minnesota. Damage was reported in Polk and Roseau Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photo and report of 1 inch hail was received via social media. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong synoptic cyclone swinging into the Northern Plains set the stage for a late Spring severe weather outbreak on May 30th. Discrete and semi-discrete supercells developed during the afternoon ahead of an arcing cold front and a nearby surface low. These storms quickly traveled northeast while gradually growing upscale into bowing line segments as storms merged together and new cells formed. During this time, hail and tornadoes occurred; the strongest being an EF2 through Forada, MN. The bowing line segments became dominant and a wind event unfolded.||Strong and broad synoptic lift, little capping, and questionable mid-level shear likely allowed for upscale growth. This caused storm interactions and competing updrafts which may have limited mesocyclone longevity and strength. While property was damaged, no lives were lost on the last day of a holiday weekend when many people were outdoors camping or doing other recreational activities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado tore through the eastern side of Eagle Bend. It knocked down or snapped many trees, partially removed roofs from several houses, peeled tin off the roof of multiple sheds and buildings, and took the top off three massive and filled grain bins in Eagle Bend. Multiple farmsteads were hit, destroying barns, machine sheds, and other outbuildings. It ended about 5 miles west-southwest of Staples where the scattered tree damage pattern could no longer be determined to be of tornadic origin. Determination of the specific path was assisted by Todd County Emergency Management, which provided drone imagery to the NWS. Maximum winds were estimated to be 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of severe thunderstorms produced large hail and gusty winds across the northern Red River Valley and into northwest Minnesota. Hail up to 2.75 inches was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was likely wrapped in downburst winds for  portions of its nearly 31 mile path through Little Toad Lake, Toad Lake, and Wolf Lake, ending around 2 se of Menagha  by 1035 pm cdt. Numerous pine, oak, and poplar trees were violently snapped or uprooted along with several wooden power poles. Farm outbuilding roofs were ripped off. A modern hog barn, an older style barn, and several smaller outbuildings were blown down at various farmsteads. Max winds to 115 mph, with a max width of 600 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant hail event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Southeast-Central Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A multicellular convective mode advanced in two waves with the first wave occurring through the morning hours in Southern MN, while the second wave produced a long-track supercell over the Twin Cities metro and Central Wisconsin. Both waves produced significant hail and damaging wind gusts. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon produced countless hailstones across the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. Beyond the typical 1 inch diameter hailstones, there were several hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter that fell over Washington County. A few other large hailstones fell over Le Sueur, Dakota and Goodhue Counties of southern Minnesota.

A dry-line/cold front was moving across Minnesota during the afternoon of Monday, May 9th. A few thunderstorms developed along this front across central Minnesota, and quickly spread south and eastward during the late afternoon and evening over eastern Minnesota, and adjacent west central Wisconsin. A brief funnel cloud was observed south of Gilman, and a tornado impacted Dakota County between Hastings and Miesville. Large hail and a few damaging wind gusts occurred along this line as it moved eastward. The largest hail stone was 2 inches in diameter and occurred near St. Paul downtown airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a social media picture of golf ball size hail.

A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 90 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Cottonwood and Nobles Counties for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds lofted two corn cribs onto adjacent bins, as well as snapped a large pine tree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power outage was  reported at Camp Wilderness on Mantrap Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant hail event occurred during the daytime hours of May 19th, 2022 across Southeast-Central Minnesota and Western Wisconsin. A multicellular convective mode advanced in two waves with the first wave occurring through the morning hours in Southern MN, while the second wave produced a long-track supercell over the Twin Cities metro and Central Wisconsin. Both waves produced significant hail and damaging wind gusts. The synoptic setup included weak ridging and zonal flow in the Upper Midwest giving way to a deepening trough in the Pacific Northwest. A cold front was advancing eastward through the Dakotas. Over Iowa, a northward advancing warm front coupled with steep lapse rates in the mid levels (7.5-8��C/km) were able to drive development initially at the MN/IA border and eventually farther north into the Metro, then eastward. Upper-air soundings depicted a marginal environment for Supercell development with 50-60kt 0-6km shear and an elevated area of 1200 J/kg MUCAPE in the mid-levels. The SPC issued threat level for the day was slight to enhanced risk with most attention given to a significant hail potential. ||The storms that developed in the mid-late afternoon produced countless hailstones across the Twin Cities metro area, and into west central Wisconsin by the late afternoon. Beyond the typical 1 inch diameter hailstones, there were several hailstones up to 2 inches in diameter that fell over Washington County. A few other large hailstones fell over Le Sueur, Dakota and Goodhue Counties of southern Minnesota.
Scattered severe thunderstorms brought damaging winds and large hail to the area during the morning, with a second round of severe storms during the mid-afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of dimes fell across portions of Compton Township.

Scattered severe thunderstorms brought damaging winds and large hail to the area during the morning, with a second round of severe storms during the mid-afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging from the size of ping pong balls to golf balls was reported, with photos sent via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms brought damaging winds and large hail to the area during the morning, with a second round of severe storms during the mid-afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter-sized hail was reported via photo on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of Thursday, May 12th, was a classic bow echo that surged north and east across western Minnesota. ||Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dew points, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area.||Winds were measured by wind sensors at over 100 mph across portions of South Dakota, with a measured wind gust at Madison, Minnesota of 94 mph. There were also numerous measured wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph across west central and southwest Minnesota with embedded surges that produced a few tornadoes. The most concentrated area of damage based on reports of farm equipment, trailers, semis, trees, power lines, and other debris, occurred near the upper Minnesota River Valley, northward to Alexandria and Long Prairie.||Over Stevens County alone, structural damage was considerable as the majority of the damage was to farm outbuildings, sheds and grain bins. There were a few locations in the county where the main inhabited house or home structure did suffer loss of roof and siding and windows but those were more isolated in nature. Many locations had debris tossed a mile northeast from its parent location. Nearly 100% of the damage witnessed in Stevens county was consistent with a straight line wind event, with just one tornado crossing the southeast corner of the county.  ||Ten tornadoes were confirmed this day, with two in Lac Qui Parle County, including a house in Bellingham that had much of its roof removed.  In northwestern Swift County, a tornado tipped over trailers and various farm equipment was heavily damaged.  Two tornadoes were in Todd County, including one that moved through Clarissa.  Two more were in Morrison County, and one was on the east side of Lake Alexander, where many trees came down on vehicles, sheds, and cabins. A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.
Two rounds of severe thunderstorms produced large hail and gusty winds across the northern Red River Valley and into northwest Minnesota. Hail up to 2.75 inches was reported.

A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 90 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Cottonwood and Nobles Counties for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power poles were snapped by strong thunderstorm winds, which downed powerlines across Interstate 90. A car stopped on the roadway to avoid crossing downed powerlines, and was struck from behind by a tractor trailer, resulting in the (indirect) fatality of one vehicle passenger and injury of the other three passengers in the car as well as one from another car.

A strong area of low pressure across the Northern Plains lifted a warm front across the Northland on Memorial Day morning. Showers and storms moved through during the morning hours with the atmosphere recovering during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms were severe during the morning and produced small hail and tree damage. An arc of thunderstorms developed across southwestern Minnesota and adjacent areas of Nebraska, South Dakota and Iowa by the mid-afternoon hours and moved to the northeast and across the Northland for the late afternoon and evening hours. These storms were much stronger and contained very strong winds that produced widespread damage across the region. Additionally, there were a few embedded tornadoes with the storms with the strongest tornadoes producing EF-1 damage in the Deer River and Hinckley areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado crossed into Cass County from Wadena County along County Road 30 just south of 84th Street Southwest and tracked northeast for about half a mile before lifting just after crossing 84th Street Southwest. The tornado blew a center pivot irrigation system across 84th Street Southwest, snapping power poles in the process. No other damage was observed. The time of the tornado was estimated by radar and peak winds were around 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Colorado Low moved through the area, bringing multiple inches of snow and 30 - 50 MPH winds to the area. This created blowing snow or blizzard conditions in some areas. In addition, there was a period of freezing rain in the southern portions of our CWA at the beginning of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports 10 inches of snow in Salol. Storm total.

A potent low-pressure system impacted the region during the afternoon and evening hours of May 30th. A larger scale Derecho tracked from far southeastern South Dakota, through much of western through northcentral Minnesota. Widespread damage was reported from damaging winds, and tornadoes. Hail up to 1.5 inches was reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several poplar and ash trees were snapped across western Blueberry Township. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several waves of storms moved across southern Minnesota during the morning and afternoon of Saturday, August 27th. During the late afternoon and early evening, a stronger storm developed over south central Minnesota and quickly moved northeast and produced a bow-echo segment. This bow-echo segment took on the characteristic of a QLCS and produced several circulations across the south and southeastern Twin Cities metro area. Some of these circulations produced EF0 tornado damage from Apple Valley, northeast to Eagan, West St. Paul, and the east side of St. Paul and Maplewood. Most of the damage was associated with large trees blown down or uprooted. A few of the larger trees landed on buildings and a trailer park west of the Minnesota Zoo and caused damage to those structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey concluded that an EF0 tornado touched down near Germane Avenue and 128th Street, or around Hidden Ponds, and Cedar Valley Apartments and moved northeast across 127th Street West. This tornado continued to move northeast before lifting between 126th and 125th Street along Genesee Avenue. This tornado uprooted or blew down numerous large trees along its path. Structural damage (mainly roof damage) occurred to apartment complexes, mobile homes, and single family homes between 128th and 125th Street. Maximum wind speeds were 85 mph with a width of 100 yards.

Several large trees were uprooted or blown down in this area with a maximum width of 50 yards, and a maximum speed of 85 mph. Most of the trees landed in open areas with little roof or property damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that originally developed across the Arrowhead of Minnesota early Wednesday morning, August 17th, created a boundary farther southward across east-central Minnesota. This boundary, with daytime heating leading to more instability, caused additional thunderstorms to develop. Due to weak winds aloft, these storms drifted southward but continued to redevelop on the west side. This led to storms training over the same areas from Mora, southward to the eastern portion of the Twin Cities metro area. One area near Cambridge had over 4 inches of rain in less than 2 hours that led to flash flooding in the town. A local business had water damage due to the excessive rainfall. Other areas in the Twin Cities also had excessive rainfall, but only minor flooding occurred, and mainly in poor drainage areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observations from local media to law enforcement indicated flooding occurring in the Cambridge city limits. The local cooperative observer reported 4.38 inches of rainfall in less than 2 hours with pictures around the city indicating stalled cars in flooded roadways. In addition, a local business had water damage from the runoff.

The damage was isolated to this area, but a tornado did touch down approximately 1/4 of a mile northeast of this location where the damage was more widespread and concentrated along the tornado's path.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey concluded that a brief EF0 tornado formed in a wooded area between Timbershore Lane and Tananger Circle, south of Duckwood Drive in Eagan, Minnesota. Most of the damage occurred in this area, with large hardwood trees uprooted or blown down. Several of the large trees landed on roofs, sheds and fences in this residential neighborhood.  The damage path became more disorganized as the tornado lifted near Lexington, and Yankee Doodle Road where a few large pine trees were uprooted. Damage indicators showed that up to 85 mph winds occurred, with a path width of 150 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This heat event was more pronounced than the previous one in June. Dew points pooled ahead of a frontal boundary Tuesday afternoon, August 2nd. These dew points that rose into the 70s combined with actual temperatures in the mid to upper 90s to support heat indices in the 100 to 115 degree range for several hours during the afternoon and evening of Tuesday, August 2nd. At one point Tuesday afternoon, heat indices rose to 115 degrees at Hutchinson, 114 degrees at Granite Falls, and 112 degrees at both Redwood Falls and New Ulm. Just west of the Twin Cities, heat indices were highest near Buffalo which rose to 111 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several temperature gauges measured heat indices between 105 and 110 degrees during the afternoon and evening hours.

At one point Tuesday afternoon, heat indices rose to 115 degrees at Hutchinson, 114 degrees at Granite Falls, and 112 degrees at both Redwood Falls and New Ulm. Just west of the Twin Cities, heat indices were highest near Buffalo which rose to 111 degrees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several temperature gauges measured heat indices between 105 and 110 degrees during the afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-term drought status was largely unchanged with a continuation of well-below normal precipitation across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Marshall and Tracy was near normal during November. Extreme (D3) drought conditions persisted through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-term drought status was largely unchanged with a continuation of well-below normal precipitation across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Precipitation at Worthington was about 75 percent of normal during November. Severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in south central South Dakota the last half of the night, and moved northeast along the nose of a strong low-level jet toward east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota after sunrise. Elevated instability of 1000-2000J/kg and deep-layer elevated shear of 30-40 knots led to organization of storms into multicell structures capable of spotty large hail and damaging wind.

During the late evening of Tuesday, August 2nd, a complex of thunderstorms that developed over eastern South Dakota moved into southwest Minnesota. These storms quickly bowed out over Minnesota and moved east-northeast from Granite Falls, to Olivia, Hutchinson, and the western Twin Cities metro area. There were numerous measured wind gusts from Marshall, to the western Twin Cities metro area of 60 to 70 mph. There was one wind gust of 81 MPH west of Hector, Minnesota. This was measured by the Minnesota Department of Transportation wind sensor. In addition to the measured wind gusts, several large trees were blown down from Redwood Falls, Olivia, Hutchinson, and parts of the Twin Cities metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree branches were blown down around the Uptown area of Minneapolis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into western Minnesota. This thunderstorm complex generated 2 confirmed tornadoes across Big Stone and Traverse Counties, and produced a broad area of widespread and destructive straight-line winds in excess of 80 mph. Trees, buildings, power lines, grain bins, semi trucks, and other property was damaged or destroyed across west central Minnesota, and power outages lasted for multiple days in some cases.

A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into western Minnesota. This thunderstorm complex generated 2 confirmed tornadoes across Big Stone and Traverse Counties, and produced a broad area of widespread and destructive straight-line winds in excess of 80 mph. Trees, buildings, power lines, grain bins, semi trucks, and other property was damaged or destroyed across west central Minnesota, and power outages lasted for multiple days in some cases.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shingles were pealed off a roof, and at least two large trees were snapped by estimated 70 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A damaging wind event unfolded across west-central Minnesota during the evening of June 20 and into the overnight hours of June 21. Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dewpoints, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area. Numerous wind gusts of 60-70 kts were measured across west-central Minnesota, along with widespread tree damage. One fatality occurred when a tree fell on a camper in Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed toward midnight on leading elevated warm advection across south central South Dakota and progressed rapidly northeast through areas north of Interstate 90.  Large amounts of DCAPE in the air mass resulted in spotty damaging wind.  A second cluster of storms developed on the tail end of the initial storms on the nose of an increasing low-level jet, using up to 2000 J/kg of elevated instability to rapidly become severe.  Very large hail resulted as storms first developed supercellular characteristics, and then a mix of hail and damaging wind as short bowing segments evolved moving into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail varied between dime- and nickel-sized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of storms produced large hail stones west of Granite Falls, near Olivia, and northeast toward the northwest suburbs of the Twin Cities. Most of these storms were elevated but had a steep lapse rate to generate large hail, up to 2 inches in diameter at times. ||These late morning, and early afternoon storms moved northeast out of Minnesota. A warm front lifted northward across southern Minnesota during the afternoon with a potent shortwave moving northeast out of Nebraska. This shortwave generated a large complex of storms and quickly moved northeast across southern Minnesota and produced numerous damaging wind gusts, tornadoes and hail. There were even some flooding concerns as three to five inches of rain fell across portions of west central and central Minnesota by the late evening as the storms diminished and moved off to the northeast.||Three tornadoes occurred within the large bow echo that surged from southwest Minnesota, northeast toward east central Minnesota. One occurred in Brown County, another in Sibley and Carver County, and a third in Anoka County. All of the tornadoes were rated EF0 and produced mainly tree and roof damage.||According to Xcel Energy, more than 75,000 customers were without power across southern Minnesota during the peak of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shed and power lines were blown down on the west side of Sanborn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure across the Northern Plains lifted a warm front across the Northland on Memorial Day morning. Showers and storms moved through during the morning hours with the atmosphere recovering during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms were severe during the morning and produced small hail and tree damage. An arc of thunderstorms developed across southwestern Minnesota and adjacent areas of Nebraska, South Dakota and Iowa by the mid-afternoon hours and moved to the northeast and across the Northland for the late afternoon and evening hours. These storms were much stronger and contained very strong winds that produced widespread damage across the region. Additionally, there were a few embedded tornadoes with the storms with the strongest tornadoes producing EF-1 damage in the Deer River and Hinckley areas.

There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the afternoon and early evening on May 30th across southwest Minnesota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 70 mph was measured at mesonet site AT653, 3 miles west of Arco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall on May 11 from 1 to 3 inches in the Pipestone Creek and Redwood River Basins led to rapid runoff and a brief push into minor flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 1 to 2 inches of rainfall occurred in the basin north of Pipestone on May 11, and resultant runoff produced a quick rise into minor flood stage. Pipestone Creek at Pipestone (gage site PIPM5) crested 0.76 feet above flood stage early on May 12. Small tracts of agricultural land succumbed to minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall on May 11 from 1 to 3 inches in the Pipestone Creek and Redwood River Basins led to rapid runoff and a brief push into minor flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 1 to 2 inches of rainfall occurred in the basin north of Pipestone on May 11, and resultant runoff produced a quick rise into minor flood stage. Pipestone Creek below  Pipestone (gage site PSTM5) crested briefly 0.09 feet above flood stage early on May 12. Small tracts of agricultural land succumbed to minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall on May 11 from 1 to 3 inches in the Pipestone Creek and Redwood River Basins led to rapid runoff and a brief push into minor flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall occurred in the upper portions of the Redwood River Basin, and resultant runoff produced a quick rise into minor flood stage. The Redwood River at Russell (gage site RUSM5) reached minor flood stage late on May 12, and crested 0.19 feet above flood stage on May 13. Small tracts of agricultural land succumbed to minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th. With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward. Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a concrete silo, damaged numerous outbuildings, and snapped several trees.

A cluster of severe thunderstorms that developed over South Dakota early Thursday morning, moved across west-central, and central Minnesota during the morning. Most of the hail fell across the central third of Minnesota, with some hail stones measured at 2 inches in diameter. In addition, a few mesonet wind sensors and the St. Cloud regional airport all reported severe wind gusts. There was also wind damage near Cold Spring and in central Morrison County where power outages occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska.  With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds.  With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours.  These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 68 mph was measured by Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN027 1 mile west-northwest of Lake Benton.

A strong cold front and upper low traversed the area during the afternoon and evening of May 12th. During the afternoon, a larger scale Derecho moved from northeastern Nebraska, through eastern South Dakota and into portions of far southeastern North Dakota and west-central Minnesota. Widespread strong to severe thunderstorms were observed, with large hail, tornadoes, and damaging wind gusts all being reported during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado crossed from far northwest Todd County around 7:52 pm, about 2.5 miles southwest of Verndale, MN. It tracked to the north until 8:11 pm where it lifted 5.5 miles east southeast of Sebeka, MN. It caused extreme damage which included numerous snapped power poles and numerous trees which were snapped or uprooted. Extensive damage was noted to farm buildings which had steel roofing and sidewall panels torn off, and turnkey barns and other industrial buildings lost roofing material. Peak winds estimated to 115 mph, with a maximum width of 500 yards.

There were numerous measured wind gusts from Marshall, to the western Twin Cities metro area of 60 to 70 mph. There was one wind gust of 81 MPH west of Hector, Minnesota. This was measured by the Minnesota Department of Transportation wind sensor. In addition to the measured wind gusts, several large trees were blown down from Redwood Falls, Olivia, Hutchinson, and parts of the Twin Cities metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private weather station measured a wind gust of 58 knots.

A low pressure system moving out of the Central Plains brought a warm front through northern Minnesota during the morning hours of the 9th. Supported by a strong low level jet and warm air pushing into the region, storms spread north through the morning hours. Storms produced hail as large as gold balls and damaging winds before losing strength during the late morning as they moved into the Arrowhead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An eight inch diameter tree was downed onto a power line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska.  With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds.  With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours.  These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 63 mph was measured by the Minnesota Road Weather Information System site at Trosky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the afternoon and early evening on May 30th across southwest Minnesota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph was measured by Automated Weather Observing System site KMML near Marshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho developed in south central South Dakota and traveled northeast into western Minnesota. This thunderstorm complex generated 2 confirmed tornadoes across Big Stone and Traverse Counties, and produced a broad area of widespread and destructive straight-line winds in excess of 80 mph. Trees, buildings, power lines, grain bins, semi trucks, and other property was damaged or destroyed across west central Minnesota, and power outages lasted for multiple days in some cases.
Heavy rainfall on May 11 from 1 to 3 inches in the Pipestone Creek and Redwood River Basins led to rapid runoff and a brief push into minor flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 1 to 2 inches of rainfall occurred in the basin north of Pipestone on May 11, and resultant runoff produced a quick rise into minor flood stage. The North Branch of Pipestone Creek near Pipestone (gage site PSNM5) crested 0.49 feet above flood stage early on May 12.  Small tracts of agricultural land succumbed to minor flooding.

A damaging wind event unfolded across west-central Minnesota during the evening of June 20 and into the overnight hours of June 21. Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dewpoints, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area. Numerous wind gusts of 60-70 kts were measured across west-central Minnesota, along with widespread tree damage. One fatality occurred when a tree fell on a camper in Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down south of Orrock, and a few roads were blocked.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th. With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward. Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed by strong thunderstorm winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video showed a brief tornado spinning up in open farmland. The tornado caused no damage from which the NWS could assign an EF-scale rating.  The average path width was estimated at 20 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Tuesday, July 5th, a complex of thunderstorms moved from South Dakota, eastward along a stationary front that lied near the Iowa border. North of this front, several lines of thunderstorms developed and moved eastward across far southern Minnesota. Although a few damage reports were noted, the main hazard was the increasing threat of heavy rainfall and flash flooding. Two areas of thunderstorms from southern Minnesota, and northern Iowa merged into a large complex as it slowly progressed to the east-southeast. Rainfall totals in a 3 hour period ranged from two to locally five inches from near Blue Earth in Faribault County, east to Albert Lea. The heaviest occurred over Albert Lea, and between Wells and Kiester in eastern Faribault County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree branches were blown down in the city of Alden.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power poles were bent or snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and upper low traversed the area during the afternoon and evening of May 12th. During the afternoon, a larger scale Derecho moved from northeastern Nebraska, through eastern South Dakota and into portions of far southeastern North Dakota and west-central Minnesota. Widespread strong to severe thunderstorms were observed, with large hail, tornadoes, and damaging wind gusts all being reported during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began around 7:07 pm half a mile southeast of Charlesville, MN and moved north by northeast until it lifted at 7:10 pm, 13 miles west of Elbow Lake, near North Ottawa. Several large ash and cottonwood tree limbs were broken down in shelterbelts. At least 2 wooden power poles were cracked and 2 others were leaning as a result of this storm. Estimated peak winds were 100 mph, equating to an EF1 rating with a path length of 3 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front across South Dakota triggered a line of severe thunderstorms that moved into a favorable environment, with hot temperatures in the 90s and muggy conditions, across west central Minnesota during the late evening. Straight-line winds of generally 60 to 70 mph were produced, including a measured gust of 71 mph both near Odessa and Graceville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel hail was observed, with little wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and upper low traversed the area during the afternoon and evening of May 12th. During the afternoon, a larger scale Derecho moved from northeastern Nebraska, through eastern South Dakota and into portions of far southeastern North Dakota and west-central Minnesota. Widespread strong to severe thunderstorms were observed, with large hail, tornadoes, and damaging wind gusts all being reported during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began at approximately 716 pm about 1 mile southwest of Tenney, MN, then tracked to the north where it lifted 3 miles north of Campbell, MN. This tornado was best marked by the trail of at least 23 power poles which were cracked or completely snapped along it's, route. Widespread tree damage and large steel grain bins at the Campbell elevator complex were partially caved in. Peak winds were estimated at 115 mph, equating to an EF2 rating. The path length was 9 miles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began about 2 miles west southwest of Battle Lake, MN and tracked to the north until 7:34 pm where it lifted 2 miles north northeast of Battle Lake, MN. One single-wide trailer was flipped. Numerous spruce, poplar, and ash trees were snapped or uprooted. Metal roofing was blown off of storage near the airport. Peak winds were estimated at 100 mph, giving this tornado an EF1 rating, with a path length of 3.5 miles. A couple of power poles were also damaged along the path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure across the Northern Plains lifted a warm front across the Northland on Memorial Day morning. Showers and storms moved through during the morning hours with the atmosphere recovering during the afternoon hours. A few of the storms were severe during the morning and produced small hail and tree damage. An arc of thunderstorms developed across southwestern Minnesota and adjacent areas of Nebraska, South Dakota and Iowa by the mid-afternoon hours and moved to the northeast and across the Northland for the late afternoon and evening hours. These storms were much stronger and contained very strong winds that produced widespread damage across the region. Additionally, there were a few embedded tornadoes with the storms with the strongest tornadoes producing EF-1 damage in the Deer River and Hinckley areas.

A potent low-pressure system impacted the region during the afternoon and evening hours of May 30th. A larger scale Derecho tracked from far southeastern South Dakota, through much of western through northcentral Minnesota. Widespread damage was reported from damaging winds, and tornadoes. Hail up to 1.5 inches was reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi tractor-trailer combination traveling on westbound I-94 was blown over onto an SUV that had parked on the shoulder of the highway due to an approaching storm. Reports indicate that three individuals were transported to a local hospital with non-life threatening injuries. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska.  With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds.  With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours.  These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second floor balcony of a historic hotel building was removed by a large tree limb displaced by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late evening of Tuesday, August 2nd, a complex of thunderstorms that developed over eastern South Dakota moved into southwest Minnesota. These storms quickly bowed out over Minnesota and moved east-northeast from Granite Falls, to Olivia, Hutchinson, and the western Twin Cities metro area. There were numerous measured wind gusts from Marshall, to the western Twin Cities metro area of 60 to 70 mph. There was one wind gust of 81 MPH west of Hector, Minnesota. This was measured by the Minnesota Department of Transportation wind sensor. In addition to the measured wind gusts, several large trees were blown down from Redwood Falls, Olivia, Hutchinson, and parts of the Twin Cities metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree branch was blown down in Eden Prairie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring snowmelt, record April precipitation and frequent May rain events led to strong hydrologic responses within the Rainy Lake basin. Namakan Lake, Kabetogama Lake and Rainy Lake rose above the flood of record during the months of May and June before subsiding in July and returning to levels closer to normal. These high lake levels led to significant damage to homes and businesses along the shoreline along with docks and other related structures. Additionally, some roads were covered in water with one being temporarily raised to maintain access to a few resorts. In addition to the costs of the damage itself, many resorts in the area suffered from lost revenue by having to close during the beginning of peak tourist season in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal snowfall for the 2021-2022 season was observed across northern portions of Koochiching and St. Louis counties and much of that remained on the ground into April. Heavy rainfall then fell on top of that above normal snowpack at the end of April and sent abnormally high amounts of runoff into the Rainy River Basin leading to rises on Rainy Lake. International Falls experienced their wettest April on record, helping to set the stage for the flooding. By June 15th, the level of Rainy Lake reached 1113.2 feet which was 22 inches above the 2014 peak level and 2.5 inches above the all-time record set in 1950. Having crested and setting a new all-time high, the lake level fell over the remainder of the month to 1112.4 feet (13 inches above 2014 peak and slightly below the 1950 record) by the start of July having fallen 11 inches since peaking on June 15. As for Namakan Lake and Kabetogama Lake, they crested at 1122.4 feet (22 inches above the 2014 peak and 6 inches below the record 1916 peak) June 1st and then fell over the remainder of June reaching 1119.6 feet (below the 2014 peak) by July 1st, having fallen 37 inches since peaking on June 1st. The high lake levels led to extensive damage to properties adjacent to the water along with damage to docks and other infrastructure. Lake levels would continue to fall over the first two weeks of July with the Flood Warning being canceled on July 11th as Namakan and Kabetogama Lakes had returned to their normal regulated levels. Levels on Rainy Lake remained above normal at this time, but were receding and the threat for additional flooding had ended. State and Federal disasters were declared for the event with millions of dollars in infrastructure damage and property loss.

A larger scale Derecho tracked from far southeastern South Dakota, through much of western through northcentral Minnesota. Widespread damage was reported from damaging winds, and tornadoes. EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was wrapped in damaging downburst winds and rain as it rapidly tracked to the north northeast from the Todd County line, across far southeast Wadena County for about 9.5 miles, to the Cass County line. It snapped numerous single pole and double X-braced power poles. it tumbled at least three center pivot irrigation systems.  Numerous oak, ash, and pine trees were snapped. The Old Wadena and Bullard Campgrounds had swaths of pine trees laid down. One cabin had the roof and sidewalls torn out. Peak winds to 130 mph. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and upper low traversed the area during the afternoon and evening of May 12th. During the afternoon, a larger scale Derecho moved from northeastern Nebraska, through eastern South Dakota and into portions of far southeastern North Dakota and west-central Minnesota. Widespread strong to severe thunderstorms were observed, with large hail, tornadoes, and damaging wind gusts all being reported during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pole shed roofing and trusses were torn up. Ash and spruce trees were broken. Time estimated based on radar and nearby wind sensor reports. A couple of nearby power poles were cracked.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind of 60 mph was measured at a personal weather station. Reports from Otter Tail Power state that a couple of power poles were cracked or otherwise damaged in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of severe thunderstorms that developed over South Dakota early Thursday morning, moved across west-central, and central Minnesota during the morning. Most of the hail fell across the central third of Minnesota, with some hail stones measured at 2 inches in diameter. In addition, a few mesonet wind sensors and the St. Cloud regional airport all reported severe wind gusts. There was also wind damage near Cold Spring and in central Morrison County where power outages occurred.

A line of severe thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota and Nebraska during the afternoon of Thursday, May 12th, was a classic bow echo that surged north and east across western Minnesota. ||Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dew points, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area.||Winds were measured by wind sensors at over 100 mph across portions of South Dakota, with a measured wind gust at Madison, Minnesota of 94 mph. There were also numerous measured wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph across west central and southwest Minnesota with embedded surges that produced a few tornadoes. The most concentrated area of damage based on reports of farm equipment, trailers, semis, trees, power lines, and other debris, occurred near the upper Minnesota River Valley, northward to Alexandria and Long Prairie.||Over Stevens County alone, structural damage was considerable as the majority of the damage was to farm outbuildings, sheds and grain bins. There were a few locations in the county where the main inhabited house or home structure did suffer loss of roof and siding and windows but those were more isolated in nature. Many locations had debris tossed a mile northeast from its parent location. Nearly 100% of the damage witnessed in Stevens county was consistent with a straight line wind event, with just one tornado crossing the southeast corner of the county.  ||Ten tornadoes were confirmed this day, with two in Lac Qui Parle County, including a house in Bellingham that had much of its roof removed.  In northwestern Swift County, a tornado tipped over trailers and various farm equipment was heavily damaged.  Two tornadoes were in Todd County, including one that moved through Clarissa.  Two more were in Morrison County, and one was on the east side of Lake Alexander, where many trees came down on vehicles, sheds, and cabins. A tornado also occurred in Douglas County, on the northwest side of Alexandria, where EF2 damage was noted.  The ninth tornado touched down south of Lowry in Pope County and was caught on video, and the tenth was in northwestern Stearns County, affecting the north side of Sauk Centre, and it was also caught on video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local emergency manager for Chippewa County reported a swath of sporadic tree, shed and roof damage from farms around Granite Falls, north-northeast to the west side of Clara City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the afternoon and early evening on May 30th across southwest Minnesota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grain bins were damaged with the debris tossed across the road into an open field.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private airport wind sensor measured a wind gust of 53 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 90 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Cottonwood and Nobles Counties for damages to public infrastructure.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph was measured by Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN027 1 mile west-northwest of Lake Benton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell storms in northeast South Dakota moved into west central Minnesota during the mid-day hours, producing an EF1 tornado by Artichoke Lake, ping pong sized hail in rural Traverse county and winds of 60 mph near Graceville. Heavy rain was also associated with the system with COOP observers measuring 3.78 inches in Wheaton and 2.42 inches in Browns Valley. The heavy rain associated with this system produced flooding in rural areas of Big Stone and Traverse counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Tuesday, July 5th, a complex of thunderstorms moved from South Dakota, eastward along a stationary front that lied near the Iowa border. North of this front, several lines of thunderstorms developed and moved eastward across far southern Minnesota. Although a few damage reports were noted, the main hazard was the increasing threat of heavy rainfall and flash flooding. Two areas of thunderstorms from southern Minnesota, and northern Iowa merged into a large complex as it slowly progressed to the east-southeast. Rainfall totals in a 3 hour period ranged from two to locally five inches from near Blue Earth in Faribault County, east to Albert Lea. The heaviest occurred over Albert Lea, and between Wells and Kiester in eastern Faribault County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding across much of western, southern, and central Freeborn County, with significant damage to numerous roads, bridges, and ditches in multiple townships, affecting areas in and near Shell Rock Watershed. Flooding caused major damage to two homes in Alden and two in Albert Lea, with walls collapsed. One more home in Albert Lea sustained minor damage.  Local media also showed two vehicles fully submerged at the intersection of Franks Avenue and East 8th Street in Albert Lea. There were also cars submerged in a parking lot on Main Street and South Washington Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 90 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Cottonwood and Nobles Counties for damages to public infrastructure.

Scattered thunderstorms developed in the early morning hours of April 23, 2022 within an area of elevated instability mainly along and east of Interstate 29. A couple thunderstorms produced marginally severe hail and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed wrist-sized branches, with one 8-inch diameter tree trunk snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure continued to produce a strong gradient across the Northern Plains, which intensified with another pulse of cold advection on April 14. Numerous high-profile vehicles had difficulty navigating the extreme wind gusts, with a few overturned semi-trucks across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were frequently between 40 and 44 mph at a Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN029 5 miles south of Hendricks and MN027 1 mile west of Lake Benton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure across Manitoba lifted a warm front across northeastern Minnesota during the afternoon hours of the 28th. A few elevated thunderstorms developed in association with the warm front and produced hail as large as golf balls in Cass County.

Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening hours of April 28, 2022. Elevated instability combined with weak forcing aloft and 0-6 km bulk shear of 30 to 40 kts to produce hailstones around 1.0 inch in diameter.
Heavy rainfall on May 11 from 1 to 3 inches in the Pipestone Creek and Redwood River Basins led to rapid runoff and a brief push into minor flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 2 to 3 inches of rainfall occurred in the upper portions of the Redwood River Basin, and resultant runoff produced a quick rise into minor flood stage. The Redwood River at Russell (gage site RUSM5) reached minor flood stage late on May 12, and crested 0.19 feet above flood stage on May 13. Small tracts of agricultural land succumbed to minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beneath a broad stratiform area of precipitation which expanded from earlier and stronger convection across south central South Dakota and central Nebraska, a wake low developed across southeast South Dakota by late evening. In a narrow zone from near Mitchell to north of Pipestone, winds suddenly increased to 40 to 50 mph, with a brief period of gusts from 58 to 65 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Non-thunderstorm wake low winds caused a machine shed to collapse 10 miles southwest of Lake Benton. Property damage costs are a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and upper low traversed the area during the afternoon and evening of May 12th. During the afternoon, a larger scale Derecho moved from northeastern Nebraska, through eastern South Dakota and into portions of far southeastern North Dakota and west-central Minnesota. Widespread strong to severe thunderstorms were observed, with large hail, tornadoes, and damaging wind gusts all being reported during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two semi trucks were flipped over along Interstate 94. Time estimated based on nearby wind sensors.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 mph was measured at Redstone RAWS site at 1621CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of May 30th, a few discrete/semi-discrete supercells developed across central MN producing low-end severe hail. However, the main focus of the event was a QLCS moving northeast from FSD���s CWA into south-central to central Minnesota. After crossing into the MPX CWA in southwest Minnesota, this feature transitioned to more of a line/bow echo but no damaging wind reports were observed, likely due to the large amount of MLCIN in southern Minnesota. However, lesser MLCIN and slightly better low-level lapse rates across central MN allowed for the bow echo's  winds to start reaching the surface with an 84 mph wind gust measured at the Olivia AWOS. A grain bin was blown away near Sacred Heart as well as some trees and power lines downed near Belgrade.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure moved along the International Border on the 16th and led to the development of a few slow moving storms across northern Minnesota. These storms produced some marginally severe hail in addition to heavy rainfall in the Grand Rapids area that led to some minor flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along Highway 169/Pokegama Avenue in Grand Rapids. Vehicles are having trouble getting through the water.
