EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms moved across Minnesota throughout the day on August 24th and increased in strength over southeast Minnesota in the evening. One storm produced a funnel cloud that was sighted north of Canton (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted north of Canton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of strong to severe thunderstorms developed across northeastern South Dakota during the late evening in association with an area of low pressure and cold front in a hot and muggy environment, and then moved east into west central Minnesota. These storms produced a measured wind gust of 67 mph seven miles southwest of Wheaton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station measured the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and upper low traversed the area during the afternoon and evening of May 12th. During the afternoon, a larger scale Derecho moved from northeastern Nebraska, through eastern South Dakota and into portions of far southeastern North Dakota and west-central Minnesota. Widespread strong to severe thunderstorms were observed, with large hail, tornadoes, and damaging wind gusts all being reported during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind of 94 mph was measured at a MN RWIS station. Widespread downburst wind damage was noted across Wadena and Aldrich Townships, with numerous large tree branches and limbs torn down, roofing material torn up, and spruce trees uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved northward from the Central Plains to the Northern Plains on May 12th and lifted a warm front across the Northland bringing two rounds of storms to the region. The first round during the afternoon hours were prolific hail producing storms with the largest hail stones being ping pong ball sized. Another round of storms arrived during the early evening hours and contained strong winds that moved through the Brainerd Lakes area. A maximum gust of 61 mph was recorded at the Brainerd Airport leading to widespread tree damage in the area. As storms continued overnight, heavy rainfall along the North Shore led to Flash Flooding in Lake and Cook counties with numerous road washouts reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed by the winds near the North Memorial Ambulance Station in downtown Brainerd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the Northland during the afternoon of the 18th. Storms developed ahead of an associated cold front and turned into a line of storms that worked across much of northeastern Minnesota and into northwest Wisconsin. The storms were mainly wind producers with numerous reports of trees down and minor structure damage as the moved through. Some hail was reported as well, but was mainly around an inch or less. An embedded EF-0 tornado was confirmed just north of the Duluth area and produced mainly tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees around 20 inches in diameter were snapped and down across a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half-dollar sized hail was reported by the public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A damaging wind event unfolded across west-central Minnesota during the evening of June 20 and into the overnight hours of June 21. Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dewpoints, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area. Numerous wind gusts of 60-70 kts were measured across west-central Minnesota, along with widespread tree damage. One fatality occurred when a tree fell on a camper in Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous power poles and lines were destroyed by the strong winds. Most of the county was without power anywhere from 3 to 24 hours. Numerous trees were toppled, including some falling on homes, sheds, and vehicles. A few agricultural buildings were also damaged. Losses were reported by the emergency manager.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon, and early evening of Thursday, June 23rd, a small complex of storms developed in central Minnesota. These storms were developing along an 850mb confluent boundary with an above normal plume of PWATs of 1.25. Due to a high freezing level, a sounding profile suggesting efficient warm-rain processes dominated.  With back-building storms across central Minnesota, rainfall amounts were excessive. Some areas received up to 7 inches of rainfall before rates diminished during the pre-dawn hours. The worst areas were across Todd, Morrison, Stearns and Benton Counties where a band of 3 to 6 inches fell before 4 am. In addition to the heavy rainfall, and flash flooding, the initial hazard was isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly 30 homes were evacuated in the town of Randall due to flood waters spilling over the banks of the Little Elk River. Numerous roads and bridges were also damaged. The heaviest rainfall occurred before 4 am, but flood waters developed during the morning. Damage totals were provided by Morrison County Emergency Management.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds were frequently between 40 and 48 mph at the Automated Weather Observing System site KOTG in Worthington, with a peak wind gust of 60 mph measured at 0902CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of elevated instability and warm air advection resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms early in the morning on April 22, 2022 across northwest Iowa into southwest Minnesota. One storm took on supercell characteristics and produced severe hail up to the size of half dollars across Jackson County.

Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening hours of April 28, 2022. Elevated instability combined with weak forcing aloft and 0-6 km bulk shear of 30 to 40 kts to produce hailstones around 1.0 inch in diameter.

A weak area of low pressure moved along the International Border on the 16th and led to the development of a few slow moving storms across northern Minnesota. These storms produced some marginally severe hail in addition to heavy rainfall in the Grand Rapids area that led to some minor flash flooding.

A weak area of low pressure moved along the International Border on the 16th and led to the development of a few slow moving storms across northern Minnesota. These storms produced some marginally severe hail in addition to heavy rainfall in the Grand Rapids area that led to some minor flash flooding.

Despite high pressure in place across much of the region on the 17th, a few slow moving storms developed across northeastern Minnesota during the afternoon hours. Heavy rainfall was the primary concern with these storms due to their slow movement, but no reports of flash flooding were received. One storm was able to produce hail to the size of golf balls as it moved across Aitkin and Carlton counties, but otherwise the storms remained below severe limits.

A warm front moved into Minnesota during the afternoon hours of the 2nd leading to the development of thunderstorms across the state. These storms were elevated in nature due to the warm front with large hail and heavy downpours being the primary threats. Some of the storms during the afternoon hours produced hail up to 2 inches in diameter across St. Louis and Itasca counties.

Supercell storms in northeast South Dakota moved into west central Minnesota during the mid-day hours, producing an EF1 tornado by Artichoke Lake, ping pong sized hail in rural Traverse county and winds of 60 mph near Graceville. Heavy rain was also associated with the system with COOP observers measuring 3.78 inches in Wheaton and 2.42 inches in Browns Valley. The heavy rain associated with this system produced flooding in rural areas of Big Stone and Traverse counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser documented the brief tornado touchdown. Two farmsteads were damaged. The first farm was located near 410th St and 590th Ave. This farm exhibited a clear track through trees on the property, the house had shingle damage and damage occurred to small outbuildings. Roughly three quarters of a mile to the north, near 400th St, the second property was damaged. This property had a grain bin swept off the base, complete destruction of machine shed, shingle damage to the home and a clear path of mangled trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of elevated instability and warm air advection resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms early in the morning on April 22, 2022 across northwest Iowa into southwest Minnesota. One storm took on supercell characteristics and produced severe hail up to the size of half dollars across Jackson County.

Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 45 mph at times during the late morning and early afternoon at Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN070 4 miles east-northeast of Jeffers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the afternoon and early evening on May 30th across southwest Minnesota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.

A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 90 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Cottonwood and Nobles Counties for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 20 foot by 60 foot cattle barn, which was open to the south, was lofted to the north and destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front and upper low traversed the area during the afternoon and evening of May 12th. During the afternoon, a larger scale Derecho moved from northeastern Nebraska, through eastern South Dakota and into portions of far southeastern North Dakota and west-central Minnesota. Widespread strong to severe thunderstorms were observed, with large hail, tornadoes, and damaging wind gusts all being reported during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A personal weather station measured a peak gust to 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low-pressure system impacted the region during the afternoon and evening hours of May 30th. A larger scale Derecho tracked from far southeastern South Dakota, through much of western through northcentral Minnesota. Widespread damage was reported from damaging winds, and tornadoes. Hail up to 1.5 inches was reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was wrapped in damaging downburst winds and rain as it rapidly tracked northeast for roughly 6 miles to around 3 SE of Elbow Lake by 419 pm CDT. Numerous wooden single and double X-Braced power poles were snapped, including a nearly 2-mile stretch of double pole structures along 240th street, as a broader downburst wind pattern developed. Numerous trees of all kinds were snapped or uprooted. Several farm buildings and grain bins were destroyed. Max winds to 120 mph, Max width to 350 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low-pressure system impacted the region during the afternoon and evening hours of May 30th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was wrapped in damaging downburst winds and rain as it rapidly tracked to the northeast for about 11 miles, ending around 4.3 miles NNE of Elbow Lake by 424 pm CDT. Numerous ash, spruce, cottonwood, and willow trees were snapped or uprooted. Numerous wooden power poles were snapped Numerous farm outbuilding and steel grain bins were destroyed, with roofing and wall panels carried downwind for a mile or more. Max winds were 115 mph, with a max width to 400 yards.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large ash and spruce trees snapped or uprooted around town. One of 4 large 100k bushel steel grain bins was blown down at the Elbow Lake cooperative grain elevator. Wind gusts likely ranged from 70 to 90 mph for several seconds. Time estimated based on radar. The Elbow Lake wind sensor went offline due to widespread power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front across South Dakota triggered a line of severe thunderstorms that moved into a favorable environment, with hot temperatures in the 90s and muggy conditions, across west central Minnesota during the late evening. Straight-line winds of generally 60 to 70 mph were produced, including a measured gust of 71 mph both near Odessa and Graceville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The time of the peak wind gust was estimated based on radar.

A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska.  With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds.  With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours.  These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 68 mph was measured by Automated Weather Observing System site KLYV 3 miles south of Luverne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th. With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward. Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds damaged the grain elevator and several roofs in Cottonwood. Trees and powerlines also down.

Thunderstorms developed toward midnight on leading elevated warm advection across south central South Dakota and progressed rapidly northeast through areas north of Interstate 90.  Large amounts of DCAPE in the air mass resulted in spotty damaging wind.  A second cluster of storms developed on the tail end of the initial storms on the nose of an increasing low-level jet, using up to 2000 J/kg of elevated instability to rapidly become severe.  Very large hail resulted as storms first developed supercellular characteristics, and then a mix of hail and damaging wind as short bowing segments evolved moving into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail varied between quarter and golf ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving out of the Central Plains brought a warm front through northern Minnesota during the morning hours of the 9th. Supported by a strong low level jet and warm air pushing into the region, storms spread north through the morning hours. Storms produced hail as large as gold balls and damaging winds before losing strength during the late morning as they moved into the Arrowhead.

A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: To the northeast of Adrian, strong winds caused a powerline leading into a barn to arc, and sparks ignited material stored in the hayloft.  The fire totally consumed the barn and two other outbuildings, and killed 17 sheep housed in the barn.  The fire then raced to the northeast a quarter mile into an adjoining property and burned grassland and numerous hay bales.  A second fire started in a burn pit near the Lismore dump when embers from a previous days burn reignited, but was quickly contained.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 44 mph at times during the late morning and early afternoon at Automated Weather Observing System site KMML near Marshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system resulted in a northward push of warmth and moisture into the mid-upper Missouri River Basin during the late afternoon and evening of April 12th. Ample 0 to 6 km shear of 60 to 70 kts, combined with MLCAPE values of 1000-2500 J/kg resulted in a prime environment for severe thunderstorms. Severe hail averaging in size from quarters to ping-pong balls fell across portions of southeast South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, and northwest Iowa.

Strong low pressure developed across central South Dakota on Saturday, which moved slowly toward northern Minnesota by Sunday morning. Southerly winds gusted as high as 50 to 70 mph at times in the late morning and afternoon, which caused sporadic tree and building damage across the area. A considerable amount of blowing dust also occurred with the strongest winds, with visibility briefly a mile or less. The strong winds combined with very low humidity and dormant vegetation resulted in numerous wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds reached 40 to 50 mph at times during the late morning and afternoon at Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN006 1 mile northeast of Rushmore, with a peak wind gust of 66 mph at 1330CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th. With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward. Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof panels were torn off a hog barn by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska.  With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds.  With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours.  These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 3 inch diameter branches were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Brief funnel clouds were observed near Beardsley, associated with a thundershower that moved to the southwest across west central Minnesota.
Brief funnel clouds were observed near Beardsley, associated with a thundershower that moved to the southwest across west central Minnesota.

A dome of high pressure built across the Upper Midwest during the late weekend of June 19th. Heat indices surpassed 100 degrees in several areas of the Twin Cities, and across west central Minnesota where the index approached 105 degrees for several hours on Sunday. The highest heat index value was 105 degrees at Granite Falls Sunday afternoon, with 104 degrees at the Crystal airport. Heat indices were actually higher on Monday due to limited cooling the night before and lasted longer in the metro area due to the heat island effect. The highest heat index values outside the metro area was 108 degrees at Redwood Falls. In the Twin Cities, Crystal airport once again was the highest with 108 degrees by late afternoon. There were other higher heat indices across the area, but these were uncontrolled private weather stations that aren't certified as official.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local airport observations indicated that heat indices rose above 100 degrees by mid afternoon, and kept around 100 degrees for several hours before dropping into the 90s by 7 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A damaging wind event unfolded across west-central Minnesota during the evening of June 20 and into the overnight hours of June 21. Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dewpoints, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area. Numerous wind gusts of 60-70 kts were measured across west-central Minnesota, along with widespread tree damage. One fatality occurred when a tree fell on a camper in Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down around Sauk Centre. The Sauk Centre airport measured a wind gust of 53 MPH at 1755 CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying area of thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota Tuesday evening, June 29th, moved eastward over west central Minnesota. Although a couple of lightning strikes were noted near Redwood Falls, the severe wind gusts near Madison were not associated with thunderstorms. Scattered showers were noted in the area, but very dry air aloft and stronger winds in the boundary layer, allowed for the mixing of the stronger winds to reach the surface. A few wind sensors in Lac Qui Parle, and Yellow Medicine Counties had wind gusts above 57 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Minnesota Department of Transportation wind sensor near Hanley Falls measured a non-thunderstorm wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon, and early evening of Thursday, June 23rd, a small complex of storms developed in central Minnesota. These storms were developing along an 850mb confluent boundary with an above normal plume of PWATs of 1.25. Due to a high freezing level, a sounding profile suggesting efficient warm-rain processes dominated.  With back-building storms across central Minnesota, rainfall amounts were excessive. Some areas received up to 7 inches of rainfall before rates diminished during the pre-dawn hours. The worst areas were across Todd, Morrison, Stearns and Benton Counties where a band of 3 to 6 inches fell before 4 am. In addition to the heavy rainfall, and flash flooding, the initial hazard was isolated large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops,  powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes. ||In addition, a separate cluster of thunderstorms ahead of the derecho produced very large hail in portions of southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down, including one that is two feet in diameter across 3rd Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 90 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Cottonwood and Nobles Counties for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused collapse of a cattle barn.

The tornado lifted before reaching County Road 5. The estimated maximum wind speed was 95 mph and the average path width was 50 yards.

A complex of storms that moved along the Minnesota/Iowa border surged northeast and began to train across south central Minnesota. Initially, large hail occurred south of Mankato, and around Owatonna. After the initial hail, training of the storms allowed for very heavy rainfall to occur between Waseca and Owatonna. Locally, 2.80 inches of rain fell around Waseca in less than three hours, and a few roads in the city of Owatonna were covered in water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a measured rainfall report of 2.80 inches of rain in less than three hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops,  powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes. ||In addition, a separate cluster of thunderstorms ahead of the derecho produced very large hail in portions of southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 7 was flooded by over a foot of flowing water one mile north of County Road 11.

Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front. The airmass featured at least 1000 J/kg DCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear parallel to the frontal boundary, which supported upscale growth and transition toward damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed several trees, including one on a powerline and one on a truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A damaging wind event unfolded across west-central Minnesota during the evening of June 20 and into the overnight hours of June 21. Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dewpoints, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area. Numerous wind gusts of 60-70 kts were measured across west-central Minnesota, along with widespread tree damage. One fatality occurred when a tree fell on a camper in Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a camper, and unfortunately one of the two individuals inside the camper was killed. The other individual was injured and hospitalized.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An amplified and vigorous upper level pattern pushed several weak disturbances northeast across the region during the overnight and early morning of May 30th. With ample elevated instability on the order of 2000-2500 J/kg available and effective shear of 35 to 45 knots, storms became severe across south central South Dakota and north central Nebraska and organized into a large scale QLCS as it moved east and northeast into Minnesota and Iowa late night. Several QLCS tornadoes developed from the Sioux Falls area eastward. Widespread winds of 60 to 90 mph caused scattered tree damage, with isolated pockets of more significant structural damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 70 mph was measured by the Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN005 1 mile west-southwest of Beaver Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the afternoon and early evening on May 30th across southwest Minnesota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 63 mph was measured at Automated Weather Observing System site KMML near Marshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A decaying area of thunderstorms that developed across South Dakota Tuesday evening, June 29th, moved eastward over west central Minnesota. Although a couple of lightning strikes were noted near Redwood Falls, the severe wind gusts near Madison were not associated with thunderstorms. Scattered showers were noted in the area, but very dry air aloft and stronger winds in the boundary layer, allowed for the mixing of the stronger winds to reach the surface. A few wind sensors in Lac Qui Parle, and Yellow Medicine Counties had wind gusts above 57 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Madison airport in Lac Qui Parle County measured a non-thunderstorm wind gust of 75 mph. Another wind gust of 63 mph occurred along the South Dakota border.

Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Lyon, Rock, and Nobles Counties in Minnesota for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust to 82 mph was measured by a personal weather station northwest of Cottonwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dome of high pressure built across the Upper Midwest during the late weekend of June 19th. Heat indices surpassed 100 degrees in several areas of the Twin Cities, and across west central Minnesota where the index approached 105 degrees for several hours on Sunday. The highest heat index value was 105 degrees at Granite Falls Sunday afternoon, with 104 degrees at the Crystal airport. Heat indices were actually higher on Monday due to limited cooling the night before and lasted longer in the metro area due to the heat island effect. The highest heat index values outside the metro area was 108 degrees at Redwood Falls. In the Twin Cities, Crystal airport once again was the highest with 108 degrees by late afternoon. There were other higher heat indices across the area, but these were uncontrolled private weather stations that aren't certified as official.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local airport observations indicated that heat indices rose above 100 degrees by mid afternoon, and kept around 100 degrees for several hours before dropping into the 90s by 7 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon, and early evening of Thursday, June 23rd, a small complex of storms developed in central Minnesota. These storms were developing along an 850mb confluent boundary with an above normal plume of PWATs of 1.25. Due to a high freezing level, a sounding profile suggesting efficient warm-rain processes dominated.  With back-building storms across central Minnesota, rainfall amounts were excessive. Some areas received up to 7 inches of rainfall before rates diminished during the pre-dawn hours. The worst areas were across Todd, Morrison, Stearns and Benton Counties where a band of 3 to 6 inches fell before 4 am. In addition to the heavy rainfall, and flash flooding, the initial hazard was isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total from overnight thunderstorms 4.20 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the afternoon and early evening on May 30th across southwest Minnesota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 90 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Cottonwood and Nobles Counties for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gustnado along the leading edge of damaging wind gusts estimated at 75 mph caused damage to a garage, garden shed, and shingles on a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A damaging wind event unfolded across west-central Minnesota during the evening of June 20 and into the overnight hours of June 21. Temperatures in the mid to upper 90s along with dew points in the mid to upper 60s created ample instability for thunderstorms to develop ahead of a cold front, once the cap weakened during the evening. Despite fairly muggy dewpoints, the hot surface temperatures created inverted-V soundings that were primed for damaging wind potential. Thunderstorms came in three waves during the evening, as marginal shear values prohibited any long-lived or well-organized thunderstorms across the area. Numerous wind gusts of 60-70 kts were measured across west-central Minnesota, along with widespread tree damage. One fatality occurred when a tree fell on a camper in Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree branches were blown down around Buffalo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms that developed early Saturday morning across northern South Dakota, moved eastward over west-central Minnesota. Several clusters of storms developed as the main system continued to propagate to the east-southeast during the morning across southern Minnesota. Most of the storms were pulse type and only produced isolated large hail or severe wind gusts. However, a couple of the storms managed to organize across Renville and Sibley Counties of southern Minnesota, and produced up to 3 inch diameter hail near Gibbon. In addition to the hail, severe wind gusts of 70 mph were reported at the Olivia airport, along with several large trees blown down. Another cluster of storms managed to organize near Mankato, to Kenyon by the late morning. The worst damage occurred in Kenyon where uprooted trees caused power outages in the town. Severe storms developed later in the evening and produced a 66 mph wind gust at the Waseca airport, and destroyed a barn northeast of Bixby in Steele County.

Most of the storms were pulse type and only produced isolated large hail or severe wind gusts. However, a couple of the storms managed to organize across Renville and Sibley Counties of southern Minnesota, and produced up to 3 inch diameter hail near Gibbon. In addition to the hail, severe wind gusts of 70 mph were reported at the Olivia airport, along with several large trees blown down. Another cluster of storms managed to organize near Mankato, to Kenyon by the late morning. The worst damage occurred in Kenyon where uprooted trees caused power outages in the town. Severe storms developed later in the evening and produced a 66 mph wind gust at the Waseca airport, and destroyed a barn northeast of Bixby in Steele County.

During the afternoon and evening of Tuesday, July 26th, a cold front moved southward across central and southern Minnesota. A line of storms developed and moved southeast across Minnesota and produced a wind gust of 44 mph at the Hutchinson airport. Also, property was damaged at two farms east of Hutchinson, and trees were also damaged farther east.  Both instability and effective wind shear along the front was ample enough to generate organized strong storms, but not enough for severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several spotters and fire department members reported a funnel cloud southwest of Winsted, near Highway 7 and County Road 15 (Falcon Ave). The funnel was photographed by at least two people, and caught on video (with time stamp) by one spotter. There was a second hand report of it briefly touching down, with two power poles leaning, but upon further investigation, none of the spotters or fire department members could confirm ground contact, nor was the power pole damage able to be confirmed. Satellite imagery was checked but that did not show any apparent tracks. Therefore this was listed as a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the day on the 11th on the backside of an area of low pressure over Lake Superior. While most of the storms were neither strong nor severe, one storm over rural northeastern Pine County interacted with a lake breeze boundary off Lake Superior and developed a weak, brief tornado over a portion of the Nemadji State Forest. The tornado was rated EF-1 based on tree damage visible on satellite analysis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was confirmed via both a radar tornadic debris signature and a comparison of satellite imagery before and after the storm. The rating was estimated by satellite observed damage to trees along the path. The maximum winds were estimated to be 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of storms developed along a frontal boundary to the north of the International Border during the early morning hours of the 18th. As these storms moved into northeastern Minnesota from Ontario, they were able to tap into some lingering instability and produce damaging wind gusts as they dropped to the south. Tree damage was reported along the path of this line of storms with most of the reports coming in later in the morning, well after the storms had passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and powerlines were downed by the winds.

Clusters of thunderstorms combined into a line of storms during the late evening in eastern North Dakota, then moved southeastward through the region over the next several hours, crossing into Minnesota after midnight. Significant damaging winds were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind was measured at the Crookston Airport /KCKN/. Several trees were reported blown down in the communities of fisher and Crookston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms combined into a line of storms during the late evening in eastern North Dakota, then moved southeastward through the region over the next several hours, crossing into Minnesota after midnight. Significant damaging winds were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind was measured at a MNDOT RWIS station. Multiple wind gusts measured at 60 mph or greater from 120 through 125 am CDT. Numerous trees snapped or uprooted in shelter belts across Huntsville and Fisher Townships.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A boat dock rolled over in the water and broke into pieces. Boat and boat lift were blown into a neighboring area, landing upside down just offshore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved across central Minnesota the afternoon and evening of July 31, with a trailing cold front moving across southern Minnesota.  Several storms developed, including one low topped mini supercell that produced a brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low topped mini supercell produced a brief tornado. It broke several large branches from a few trees and partially ripped off some soffits. A storm chaser caught video of the tornado breaking tree branches and dropping them to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms worked across the Northland on the 10th with the first round moving out of Canada during the early morning hours. These storms produced localized damaging winds shortly after sunrise with a few downed trees and powerlines reported. Another round moved out of the Northern Plains across north-central Minnesota during the mid-morning hours and produced some isolated damaging winds and hail to around the size of half dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was down blocking a road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three birch trees snapped in half during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved across southeast Minnesota on July 23rd. The first occurred in the afternoon and produced two tornadoes, wind damage and large hail. A brief EF0 tornado occurred south of Fremont (Winona County) that produced mainly tree damage. Another EF0 tornado touched down just north of Houston (Houston County) and damaged a barn and trees along its path. The storms also produced high winds that damaged structures in Claremont (Dodge County), blew down trees in numerous locations, and produced 66 mph gusts in downtown Rochester (Olmsted County) and near Brownsdale (Mower County) and Sheldon (Houston County). The largest reported hail was quarter sized near Oronoco (Olmsted County). The second round of storms occurred in the late evening, producing dime sized hail near Spring Valley (Fillmore County) before moving into northeast Iowa. Houston County declared a state of emergency following the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail fell west of Oronoco.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a 1.5 mile wide north to south swath of wind damage that extended from Nobles Avenue and 177th Street to the 16500 block of O'Day Avenue. Much of the roof of a large machine shed was blown off and a large door was collapsed inward. Dozens of trees were broken or large branches were downed, and some corn was flattened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure moved into the region starting on the 14th. The track of the low led to a long period of easterly winds across western Lake Superior. These winds combined with the relatively warm waters of Lake Superior led to a long duration lake-effect snow event along the North Shore of northeastern Minnesota. Enhanced bands were embedded within the wider expanse and led to narrow areas of higher snowfall. The highest report was from the Hovland area where over 30 inches was received by the time winds shifted and the lake-effect ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration lake-effect snow event led to 6 to 10 inches of snow along the higher terrain of the southern portions of Lake and Cook Counties. A persistent band of lake-effect snow led to nearly 17 inches of snow along the Gunflint Trail north of Grand Marais.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of Tuesday, July 12th, a weak cold front was moving southward across central Minnesota. A cluster of storms developed ahead of this front, and with 30-40 knots of effective wind shear, a few severe wind gusts occurred across central and east central Minnesota. Although instability was weak, there was enough DCAPE with the strongest storms to produce these damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a two vehicle accident southeast of Canton (Fillmore County) on November 5th. Roads were snow covered and slippery at the time of the accident as light snow had been falling for several hours before the accident. The snow persisted into the early morning hours of the 6th as an area of low pressure moved slowly across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a two vehicle accident that occurred on U.S. Highway 52 southeast of Canton on November 5th. Roads were snow covered and slippery at the time of the accident. Light snow had been falling for several hours before the accident and continued into the early morning of the 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front. The airmass featured at least 1000 J/kg DCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear parallel to the frontal boundary, which supported upscale growth and transition toward damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust to 58 mph was measured at 2001CST at a Minnesota Road Weather Information System site near Taunton with a peak gust of 68 mph at 2006CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across eastern Montana and the western Dakotas during the morning hours. These storms organized and turned southeastward, feeding on rich inflow of post-frontal air with up to 3000 J/kg of MLCAPE and over 1000 J/kg of DCAPE. Along with strong storm-relative shear, parameters favored development of a strong cold pool and balanced mesocyclone, which spread southeast with the cluster and developed a strong and long-duration severe wind event which traversed South Dakota, southwest Minnesota, extreme northeast Nebraska, and northwest Iowa during the afternoon and early evening. The storm was classified as a derecho, and featured numerous wind gusts from 70 to 100 mph and considerable damage to trees, crops,  powerlines, and structures along its path. In some cases, severe wind gusts persisted for more than 30 minutes. ||In addition, a separate cluster of thunderstorms ahead of the derecho produced very large hail in portions of southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of thunderstorm wind gusts of 58 mph or greater was measured by Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN006 1 mile west-southwest of Beaver Creek. A peak wind gust of 83 mph occurred at 1600CST. In addition, multiple large tree branches were downed in the city of Beaver Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving area of low pressure moved into the region starting on the 14th. The track of the low led to a long period of easterly winds across western Lake Superior. These winds combined with the relatively warm waters of Lake Superior led to a long duration lake-effect snow event along the North Shore of northeastern Minnesota. Enhanced bands were embedded within the wider expanse and led to narrow areas of higher snowfall. The highest report was from the Hovland area where over 30 inches was received by the time winds shifted and the lake-effect ended.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration lake-effect snow event led to snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches along the higher terrain up from Lake Superior. A persistent band of lake-effect snow led to a total of just over 27 inches near the Wolf Ridge Environmental Learning Center near Finland by the end of the event.

Several clusters of storms developed as the main system continued to propagate to the east-southeast during the morning across southern Minnesota. Most of the storms were pulse type and only produced isolated large hail or severe wind gusts. However, a couple of the storms managed to organize across Renville and Sibley Counties of southern Minnesota, and produced up to 3 inch diameter hail near Gibbon. In addition to the hail, severe wind gusts of 70 mph were reported at the Olivia airport, along with several large trees blown down. Another cluster of storms managed to organize near Mankato, to Kenyon by the late morning. The worst damage occurred in Kenyon where uprooted trees caused power outages in the town. Severe storms developed later in the evening and produced a 66 mph wind gust at the Waseca airport, and destroyed a barn northeast of Bixby in Steele County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down between Renville and Olivia. Some trees fell on cars, houses, and sheds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dome of high pressure built across the Upper Midwest during the late weekend of June 19th. Heat indices surpassed 100 degrees in several areas of the Twin Cities, and across west central Minnesota where the index approached 105 degrees for several hours on Sunday. The highest heat index value was 105 degrees at Granite Falls Sunday afternoon, with 104 degrees at the Crystal airport. Heat indices were actually higher on Monday due to limited cooling the night before and lasted longer in the metro area due to the heat island effect. The highest heat index values outside the metro area was 108 degrees at Redwood Falls. In the Twin Cities, Crystal airport once again was the highest with 108 degrees by late afternoon. There were other higher heat indices across the area, but these were uncontrolled private weather stations that aren't certified as official.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local airports and other reporting stations measured heat indices above 100 degrees from the early afternoon, through the early evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms worked across the Northland on the 10th with the first round moving out of Canada during the early morning hours. These storms produced localized damaging winds shortly after sunrise with a few downed trees and powerlines reported. Another round moved out of the Northern Plains across north-central Minnesota during the mid-morning hours and produced some isolated damaging winds and hail to around the size of half dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of Tuesday, July 12th, a weak cold front was moving southward across central Minnesota. A cluster of storms developed ahead of this front, and with 30-40 knots of effective wind shear, a few severe wind gusts occurred across central and east central Minnesota. Although instability was weak, there was enough DCAPE with the strongest storms to produce these damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was snapped at its base northeast of Maple Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved across southeast Minnesota on July 23rd. The first occurred in the afternoon and produced two tornadoes, wind damage and large hail. A brief EF0 tornado occurred south of Fremont (Winona County) that produced mainly tree damage. Another EF0 tornado touched down just north of Houston (Houston County) and damaged a barn and trees along its path. The storms also produced high winds that damaged structures in Claremont (Dodge County), blew down trees in numerous locations, and produced 66 mph gusts in downtown Rochester (Olmsted County) and near Brownsdale (Mower County) and Sheldon (Houston County). The largest reported hail was quarter sized near Oronoco (Olmsted County). The second round of storms occurred in the late evening, producing dime sized hail near Spring Valley (Fillmore County) before moving into northeast Iowa. Houston County declared a state of emergency following the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Mound Prairie.

There was barely a break between the exit of the earlier morning severe convection, and development of the next round during the afternoon and early evening on May 30th across southwest Minnesota.  A series of impulses moved northeast during the day, with a leading strong wave moving out of Nebraska by early afternoon. Storms blossomed near a cold frontal boundary toward the James River Valley, despite the limited diurnal destabilization, due to steep mid-level lapse rates which brought MLCAPE up to 1500-2000J/kg in the warm sector. Strong mid-level winds led to long, linear hodographs. With a warm front also lingering north of Interstate 90, storms that interacted with enhanced low-level shear in the vicinity of various boundaries produced a few brief tornadoes, but strong deep-layer forcing eventually resulted in an evolution toward linear structures with mainly damaging wind.  Near the core of the upper level wave, a secondary band of thunderstorms developed across Nebraska during the late afternoon and raced northeastward through early evening, featuring mainly damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous small tree limbs were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring snowmelt, record April precipitation and frequent May rain events led to strong hydrologic responses within the Rainy Lake basin. Namakan Lake, Kabetogama Lake and Rainy Lake rose above the flood of record during the months of May and June before subsiding in July and returning to levels closer to normal. These high lake levels led to significant damage to homes and businesses along the shoreline along with docks and other related structures. Additionally, some roads were covered in water with one being temporarily raised to maintain access to a few resorts. In addition to the costs of the damage itself, many resorts in the area suffered from lost revenue by having to close during the beginning of peak tourist season in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal snowfall for the 2021-2022 season was observed across northern portions of Koochiching and St. Louis counties and much of that remained on the ground into April. Heavy rainfall then fell on top of that above normal snowpack at the end of April and sent abnormally high amounts of runoff into the Rainy River Basin leading to rises on Rainy Lake. International Falls experienced their wettest April on record, helping to set the stage for the flooding. By June 15th, the level of Rainy Lake reached 1113.2 feet which was 22 inches above the 2014 peak level and 2.5 inches above the all-time record set in 1950. Having crested and setting a new all-time high, the lake level fell over the remainder of the month to 1112.4 feet (13 inches above 2014 peak and slightly below the 1950 record) by the start of July. The high lake levels led to extensive damage to properties adjacent to the water along with damage to docks and other infrastructure. Lake levels would continue to fall into July and the Flood Warning was canceled on July 11th. Levels on Rainy Lake remained above normal at this time, but were receding and the threat for additional flooding had ended. State and Federal disasters were declared for the event with millions of dollars in infrastructure damage and property loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms that moved along the Minnesota/Iowa border surged northeast and began to train across south central Minnesota. Initially, large hail occurred south of Mankato, and around Owatonna. After the initial hail, training of the storms allowed for very heavy rainfall to occur between Waseca and Owatonna. Locally, 2.80 inches of rain fell around Waseca in less than three hours, and a few roads in the city of Owatonna were covered in water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rainfall occurred in a short period of time in the city of Owatonna. There were a few streets that had water covering the roadway and were impassable at one point. These roads included the intersection of Elm and Main Street, and Rose and Elm Street on the east side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across parts of northeastern Minnesota at the start of November. A small area of D2 Severe Drought was located over parts of Carlton and Pine counties at the beginning of the month, but a heavy rainfall event during the second week of the month led to rapid drought improvement by the middle of the month. All areas of D2 Severe Drought were removed with the update on the 15th with the area seeing above normal precipitation over the course of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: November began with D2 Severe Drought across the far southeastern corner of Carlton County. A heavy rain event was received the second week of the month and led to the removal of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 15th. Precipitation for the month ended up around an inch above normal with D1 Moderate Drought persisting for the remainder of the month.

The second round of storms occurred in the late evening, producing dime sized hail near Spring Valley (Fillmore County) before moving into northeast Iowa. Houston County declared a state of emergency following the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down across Rochester. Power poles were snapped, creating several power outages across the city that affected more than 3,500 customers. One tree fell on a church, causing roof damage that allowed water to leak into the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of storms developed along a frontal boundary to the north of the International Border during the early morning hours of the 18th. As these storms moved into northeastern Minnesota from Ontario, they were able to tap into some lingering instability and produce damaging wind gusts as they dropped to the south. Tree damage was reported along the path of this line of storms with most of the reports coming in later in the morning, well after the storms had passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and powerlines were down along Minnesota Highway 1 west of Tower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across parts of northeastern Minnesota at the start of November. A small area of D2 Severe Drought was located over parts of Carlton and Pine counties at the beginning of the month, but a heavy rainfall event during the second week of the month led to rapid drought improvement by the middle of the month. All areas of D2 Severe Drought were removed with the update on the 15th with the area seeing above normal precipitation over the course of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: November began with D2 Severe Drought across the extreme northeastern corner of the county. A heavy rain event was received the second week of the month and led to the removal of D2 Severe Drought with the update on the 15th. Precipitation for the month ended up around an inch above normal with D1 Moderate Drought persisting for the remainder of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon, and early evening of Thursday, June 23rd, a small complex of storms developed in central Minnesota. These storms were developing along an 850mb confluent boundary with an above normal plume of PWATs of 1.25. Due to a high freezing level, a sounding profile suggesting efficient warm-rain processes dominated.  With back-building storms across central Minnesota, rainfall amounts were excessive. Some areas received up to 7 inches of rainfall before rates diminished during the pre-dawn hours. The worst areas were across Todd, Morrison, Stearns and Benton Counties where a band of 3 to 6 inches fell before 4 am. In addition to the heavy rainfall, and flash flooding, the initial hazard was isolated large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total from overnight thunderstorms 4.44 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved across southeast Minnesota on July 23rd. The first occurred in the afternoon and produced two tornadoes, wind damage and large hail. A brief EF0 tornado occurred south of Fremont (Winona County) that produced mainly tree damage. Another EF0 tornado touched down just north of Houston (Houston County) and damaged a barn and trees along its path. The storms also produced high winds that damaged structures in Claremont (Dodge County), blew down trees in numerous locations, and produced 66 mph gusts in downtown Rochester (Olmsted County) and near Brownsdale (Mower County) and Sheldon (Houston County). The largest reported hail was quarter sized near Oronoco (Olmsted County). The second round of storms occurred in the late evening, producing dime sized hail near Spring Valley (Fillmore County) before moving into northeast Iowa. Houston County declared a state of emergency following the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado moved through rural portions of Houston County north of the city of Houston. The tornado destroyed one barn, pushed another off its foundation, and damaged trees and some crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of heavy snow fell from southwest Minnesota through the Twin Cities metro and up through northwest Wisconsin on Tuesday, November 29, 2022. This band was caused by strong frontogenesis within the 800 to 900 mb layer. ||Snowfall rates within this band were over an inch per hour, with the MSP airport reporting a 2 inch-per-hour snowfall rate between 18 and 19Z. Total snowfall amounts within this band ranged from 5 to 8 inches, with a few locations reporting around 8.5. ||The impacts were typical for a winter storm. Schools closed early, and there were many traffic accidents across the Twin Cities metro. At times, interstates were temporarily closed when semis jackknifed across the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across Le Sueur County late Monday evening into Tuesday afternoon, locally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the county. The highest amounts were recorded in the northern half of the county east of Le Sueur, MN and in the city of New Prague, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across south-central South Dakota in the late afternoon of August 2nd in response to an eastward advancing short-wave aloft. Weak deep-layer shear and skinny CAPE profiles limited the overall severe threat, but a very dry sub-cloud layer allowed for stronger wind gusts aloft to reach the surface across portions of southeast South Dakota and southwest Minnesota during the evening hours. Observed wind gusts ranged from 60 to 80 mph with occasional reports of trees down and isolated structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Medium to large diameter branches were downed by thunderstorm winds in Minneota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front plowed into an environment with roughly 1500 J/kg MLCAPE but weak shear, which resulted in numerous thunderstorms with brief intense updrafts. The strongest storms produced isolated damaging wind gusts and hail up to golf ball size.  By evening, a stronger low-level flow ahead of the front resulted in a slightly stronger storm with hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter across northwest Iowa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Colorado low brought anomalously high moisture to the region on the 10th. Most of the Northland received heavy rainfall that changed over to snow, but Cook County at the tip of the Minnesota Arrowhead received a prolonged period of freezing rain with cold air trapped at the surface as warm air off Lake Superior moved overhead. Freezing rain accumulations of 0.20 to 0.35 inches were received from areas near Lake Superior and up the Gunflint Trail with most falling during the afternoon and early evening hours before ending. In addition to the freezing rain, easterly winds gusting to 45 to 55 mph in the Duluth area led to many reports of trees and power lines brought down by the winds that were accompanied by heavy rainfall that led to saturated soil. The winds also generated damaging waves on Lake Superior in the Duluth area that disrupted ongoing restoration and mitigation work caused by past storms in addition to causing new damage as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy freezing rain was received across Cook County on the 10th. Reports of 0.20 to 0.30 inches of ice accumulation were reported with a high amount of 0.35 inches 14 miles east-southeast of Sea Gull Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Easterly winds gusting in excess of 50 mph off Lake Superior led to widespread reports of damage in the Duluth area and up the North Shore in southern St. Louis County. Numerous reports of trees down with some on power lines along with debris blocking roads were received. Additionally, damage to public infrastructure was reported as well. A State of Local Disaster was declared for St. Louis County as a result of the storm. A max gust for the event of 54 mph was recorded at the Duluth International Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Easterly winds gusting in excess of 50 mph generated waves of 8 to 13 feet along the North Shore of Lake Superior in southern St. Louis County. These waves led to extensive damage and erosion along the shoreline. The worst damage was seen in the Brighton Park area of Duluth. Due to other events like this over the past 5 years, mitigation and restoration was still ongoing at the time of this event. The Brighton Beach area in Duluth in particular was hit hard with the loss of material being used in the restoration and mitigation work in addition to new damage as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across Watonwan County late Monday evening into Tuesday afternoon. Locally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the county. The highest observation was recorded  in Lewisville, MN with 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of heavy snow fell from southwest Minnesota through the Twin Cities metro and up through northwest Wisconsin on Tuesday, November 29, 2022. This band was caused by strong frontogenesis within the 800 to 900 mb layer. ||Snowfall rates within this band were over an inch per hour, with the MSP airport reporting a 2 inch-per-hour snowfall rate between 18 and 19Z. Total snowfall amounts within this band ranged from 5 to 8 inches, with a few locations reporting around 8.5. ||The impacts were typical for a winter storm. Schools closed early, and there were many traffic accidents across the Twin Cities metro. At times, interstates were temporarily closed when semis jackknifed across the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across Eastern Brown County late Monday evening into Tuesday afternoon, where several sources reported 4-5 inches of snowfall.

One business near Pequot Lakes received roof damage from the winds of a decaying storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high school cross country meet was held near Stewartville (Olmsted County) on September 1st. Fourteen runners needed treatment for heat exhaustion with four of them transported to a hospital for additional aid. Observations from the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester (Olmsted County) airport indicated that conditions were unusually humid for early September with dew points in the lower 70s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex upper low developed over the central and southern Rocky Mountain Region in the days leading up to this event. It strengthened and brought heavy snow, freezing rain, and mixed precipitation to the Northern Plains. Travel was significantly impacted, with roads and highways closed, and multiple reports of slide-offs and collisions. There were 3 periods of impactful weather associated with this system, with heavy precipitation on the 12th and 13th, light snow and blowing snow on the 14th, then additional light to moderate snowfall on the 15th and 16th. Blizzard conditions were observed on the 16th across portions of central and eastern North Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports 5 inches of snow in Thief River Falls. Relayed via Facebook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls impacted portions of the North Shore through the I-35 corridor in northeast Minnesota on December 16, 2022. A slow moving low pressure system was in place across eastern Minnesota and northwest Wisconsin. Low level instability and moisture supported cellular snow squall development from the late morning through the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Visibilities quickly dropped to around 1/4 mile or less as the squall passed through the area.

It strengthened and brought heavy snow, freezing rain, and mixed precipitation to the Northern Plains. Travel was significantly impacted, with roads and highways closed, and multiple reports of slide-offs and collisions. There were 3 periods of impactful weather associated with this system, with heavy precipitation on the 12th and 13th, light snow and blowing snow on the 14th, then additional light to moderate snowfall on the 15th and 16th. Blizzard conditions were observed on the 16th across portions of central and eastern North Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reports 8.5 inches of snow 2 NE Lake Itasca. Relayed via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls impacted portions of the North Shore through the I-35 corridor in northeast Minnesota on December 16, 2022. A slow moving low pressure system was in place across eastern Minnesota and northwest Wisconsin. Low level instability and moisture supported cellular snow squall development from the late morning through the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Visibilities quickly dropped to around 1/4 mile or less as the squall passed through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and storms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon of July 17th. This activity produced a funnel cloud that was sighted west of Hayfield (Dodge County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted west of Hayfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clear skies through the day with southwesterly flow at the surface lead to rapid heating and moisture advection across the Northland. This lead to the development of thunderstorms during the afternoon hours that were prolific hail producers across the region with the largest hail bring the size of baseballs. Additionally, slow storm movement along with training storms near Pine Center produced some flash flooding.

A warm front lifted across the Northland during the early morning hours of the 20th bringing an unusually warm and moist airmass to the region creating very unstable conditions by the afternoon hours. During the late evening hours, a very strong low level jet ramped up and led to the development of storms across central Minnesota that moved to the northeast. Additional storms developed on the backside of the initial wave with the severe storm potential tapering off just before midnight. The storms primarily produced tree damage as they worked across the region, but there was some marginally severe hail as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four trees were blown over.

Additional storms developed on the backside of the initial wave with the severe storm potential tapering off just before midnight. The storms primarily produced tree damage as they worked across the region, but there was some marginally severe hail as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parts of a metal building were ripped off and strewn over semi trucks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system over the Northern Plains moved northeast across the Northland late on the 24th. Fueled by strong southerly flow, the thunderstorm complex began to bow as the cold front advanced to the east. This line of storms produced damaging winds which lead to many trees downed and power outages across northern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were snapped blocking a road. Downed power lines were also reported in the area.
