EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring snowmelt, record April precipitation and frequent May rain events led to strong hydrologic responses within the Rainy Lake basin. Namakan Lake, Kabetogama Lake and Rainy Lake rose above the flood of record during the months of May and June before subsiding in July and returning to levels closer to normal. These high lake levels led to significant damage to homes and businesses along the shoreline along with docks and other related structures. Additionally, some roads were covered in water with one being temporarily raised to maintain access to a few resorts. In addition to the costs of the damage itself, many resorts in the area suffered from lost revenue by having to close during the beginning of peak tourist season in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal snowfall for the 2021-2022 season was observed across northern portions of Koochiching and St. Louis counties and much of that remained on the ground into April. Heavy rainfall then fell on top of that above normal snowpack at the end of April and sent abnormally high amounts of runoff into the Rainy River Basin leading to rises on Rainy Lake. International Falls experienced their wettest April on record, helping to set the stage for the flooding. By June 15th, the level of Rainy Lake reached 1113.2 feet which was 22 inches above the 2014 peak level and 2.5 inches above the all-time record set in 1950. Having crested and setting a new all-time high, the lake level fell over the remainder of the month to 1112.4 feet (13 inches above 2014 peak and slightly below the 1950 record) by the start of July. The high lake levels led to extensive damage to properties adjacent to the water along with damage to docks and other infrastructure. Lake levels would continue to fall into July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring snowmelt, record April precipitation and frequent May rain events led to strong hydrologic responses within the Rainy Lake basin. Namakan Lake, Kabetogama Lake and Rainy Lake rose above the flood of record during the months of May and June before subsiding in July and returning to levels closer to normal. These high lake levels led to significant damage to homes and businesses along the shoreline along with docks and other related structures. Additionally, some roads were covered in water with one being temporarily raised to maintain access to a few resorts. In addition to the costs of the damage itself, many resorts in the area suffered from lost revenue by having to close during the beginning of peak tourist season in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Above normal snowfall for the 2021-2022 season was observed across northern portions of Koochiching and St. Louis counties and much of that remained on the ground into April. Heavy rainfall then fell on top of that above normal snowpack at the end of April and sent abnormally high amounts of runoff into the Rainy River Basin leading to rises on Rainy Lake. International Falls experienced their wettest April on record, helping to set the stage for the flooding. By June 15th, the level of Rainy Lake reached 1113.2 feet which was 22 inches above the 2014 peak level and 2.5 inches above the all-time record set in 1950. Having crested and setting a new all-time high, the lake level fell over the remainder of the month to 1112.4 feet (13 inches above 2014 peak and slightly below the 1950 record) by the start of July having fallen 11 inches since peaking on June 15. As for Namakan Lake and Kabetogama Lake, they crested at 1122.4 feet (22 inches above the 2014 peak and 6 inches below the record 1916 peak) June 1st and then fell over the remainder of June reaching 1119.6 feet (below the 2014 peak) by July 1st, having fallen 37 inches since peaking on June 1st. The high lake levels led to extensive damage to properties adjacent to the water along with damage to docks and other infrastructure. Lake levels would continue to fall into July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak clipper system brought light snowfall to the region during the morning hours of the 6th. While accumulations were generally under 3 for most locations, a localized area of enhanced snowfall occurred over southern Cass County and led to 6 of snowfall in a 6 hour period near Pine River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals were generally around 3 inches or less, but an area of heavy snow led to a 6 report near Pine River over a period of 6 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved through Beltrami and Hubbard counties, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager reports 1-inch to 1.25-inch hail within Lake Bemidji State Park. Additional damage included small branches broken off trees. Time estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved through Beltrami and Hubbard counties, producing large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Employee spoke with the public and noted 3-inch to 4-inch tree branches down just west of the city of Laporte, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A volatile environment featured a warm front from parts of east central South Dakota into western Minnesota, with a dryline extending to the southwest into Nebraska.  With a very strong wave moving northeast through Nebraska into a highly unstable and highly sheared environment, scattered storms initiated and quickly grew upscale to a broad linear structure with damaging winds.  With crop development well behind schedule and many fields yet to be planted, there was ample dirt available to be lofted into the leading edge of the storms, which took on the characteristics of a haboob reducing visibility to near zero at the onset of high winds.||A broad swath of winds from 70 to 100 mph devastated much of southeast South Dakota, and portions of southwest Minnesota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa, causing extensive tree and structural damage and scores of injuries. The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours.  These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 79 mph was measured by Automated Weather Observing System site KMML 1 mile west of Marshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of early morning storms developed on the nose of steeper mid-level lapse rates and marginally strong elevated wind shear across far southeast South Dakota. Multicell convection produced spotty large hail moving into southwest Minnesota. During the afternoon, convection re-ignited near an advancing mesoscale convective vortex and moved northeast from eastern Nebraska into southwest Minnesota.  With greater surface-based instability with a MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg and deep-layer shear of 40-50 knots, storms quickly organized into a QLCS structure and produced hail up to ping-pong ball size, wind to 90 mph, and a few tornadoes across southwest Minnesota. Presidential Disaster Declarations were granted to Cottonwood and Nobles Counties for damages to public infrastructure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado formed 5 miles east-northeast of Fulda in Murray County, and entered Cottonwood County around 6 miles south-southwest of Westbrook. The tornado tracked northeast over open fields for less than a minute before dissipating.  While in Murray County, it was rated EF1, but was rated EFU in Cottonwood County. Average path width in Cottonwood County was 50 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were much closer to normal in October, precipitation was generally less than 25 percent of normal, leading to the worsening of long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite just slightly above normal temperatures, precipitation at Lake Wilson was only around 25 percent of normal during October.  Extreme (D3) drought conditions developed early in the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While temperatures were much closer to normal in October, precipitation was generally less than 25 percent of normal, leading to the worsening of long-term drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Despite just slightly above normal temperatures, precipitation at Pipestone was around 30 percent of normal during October. Extreme (D3) drought conditions developed early in the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong deep-layer shear developed as a result of an increase in the low-level jet and turning mid-level winds ahead of the approaching strong upper-level trough. A strong push of mid-level instability created up to 1000 J/kg MUCAPE, despite an environment with a strong sub-cloud dry layer. Storms moved rapidly northeast, with a cluster of hail producing storms in east central South Dakota. A second round of wind-producing storms erupted on the nose of the low-level jet across far northeast Nebraska, and translated quickly through southeast South Dakota into northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 74 mph was measured by Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN147 near Magnolia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front. The airmass featured at least 1000 J/kg DCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear parallel to the frontal boundary, which supported upscale growth and transition toward damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was split in half by thunderstorm wind gusts near Lake Shetek.

The highest observed wind gust was 107 mph near Tripp, SD. In addition, vehicles and high profile vehicles were blown off several roads, shutting down traffic on Interstates 29 and 90. Two (direct) fatalities occurred in vehicles impacted by debris as the storms moved into the Sioux Falls area. Power was disrupted in a widespread area, with estimates of over 45,000 customers impacted at one time. Many schools were closed due to damage and power issues. The South Dakota Governor requested and was granted a Presidential Disaster Declaration for damage to public infrastructure at an estimated cost of 6.7 million dollars across 20 counties and two reservations. Private losses were a much higher toll, and just one of the impacted counties (McCook) estimated at least 150 to 175 million dollars in damage. Presidential Disaster Declarations were also granted in Dixon County, Nebraska and Lincoln County, Minnesota.||Following the afternoon line of storms, a secondary band of storms developed near the core of the upper level jet across eastern Nebraska in the early evening hours.  These storms also quickly become severe, with a mix of large hail, damaging wind, and even an isolated tornado near Sioux City. This cluster of storms moved through far southeast South Dakota, northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph was measured by Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN038 2 miles west of Dovray.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a compact jet streak ejected out of a deep western U.S. trough, a strong area of low pressure developed and tracked northeast from Kansas to Iowa. Initial thermal profiles were warm enough to result in a brief period of light freezing rain before a transition to snow, but with generally light ice accumulations. A few rumbles of thunder in southwest Minnesota were accompanied by small hail. A few hours of moderate to heavy snowfall,  especially in areas near and north of Highway 14, resulted in accumulations from 2 to 4 inches, with lesser amounts to the south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While snow accumulation was less than one-half inch, a period of light freezing rain in the afternoon resulted in less than one-eighth of an inch ice accumulation at Brewster.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: While snowfall totaled just 0.7 inch near Currie, the snow was preceded by a period of light freezing rain in the afternoon that resulted in less than one-tenth of an inch ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow band of snow developed as cold air surged in behind evening showers and thunderstorms, which produced spotty small hail. A very small zone of 2 to 5 inches of snowfall spread across southeast South Dakota into parts of southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to briefly moderate snowfall totaled 4.0 inches at Minneota and 2.0 inches at Marshall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air began moving across the Upper Midwest on Dec 21-22 following the snow. Winds increased from west to east across the forecast area, creating dangerously cold wind chills of -30 to -48��F.||The coldest wind chill temperatures favored Western Minnesota given the physical geography of the land and lack of friction of surrounding structures. Morris Municipal Airport in Stevens County and Benson Municipal Airport in Swift County recorded the lowest observed wind chills at -48��. Glenwood Airport in Pope County recorded -45�� as well as St. James Municipal Airport in Watonwan County. The third lowest wind chill (-44��) was observed at the Olivia Regional Airport in Renville County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many hours with wind chill ranging from -44�� to -35��. The strongest winds were recorded at 9:57 AM on 12/23 with a gust at 54 mph at Redwood Falls Municipal Airport in Redwood Falls, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by rapid intensification of a low-pressure system over the Great Lakes led to a multi-day winter storm for much of Minnesota. Part 1 of this system was widespread fluffy snow with high snow-to-liquid ratios from southwest through east central Minnesota. ||There was a break, and then part 2 of this system followed as strong arctic winds developed in wrap-around circulation of the low-pressure system. Wind gusts of 35-50 mph, with the highest amounts in western and southern Minnesota. These winds together with the arctic air and fresh snow led to dangerous wind-chills of -25 to -35��F and blizzard conditions across the open terrain of the Minnesota River Valley. Roads became impassable due to reports of snow drifts measuring 5 to 10 feet tall and nearly 40 feet long in some areas and zero visibility in multiple counties across Western MN. Whiteout conditions forced many roads to close. Highway 71 in Redwood County remained closed for approximately 36 hours after the storm had passed. The National Guard was called upon to perform rescue missions stranded motorists Kandiyohi and Renville Counties. One Kandiyohi County rescue case involved a stranded motorist who was diagnosed with critical hypothermia after being exposed in dangerous conditions for nearly nine hours. ||Rescued motorists were housed in various locations for their safety as the storm passed. In Renville County, approximately 50 people were housed at the Olivia Armory in Oliva, MN. Watonwan County opened emergency shelters in Medelia, MN and Saint James, MN. In Martin County, the Salvation Army provided hotel vouchers prior to the event for anyone in need to seek shelter during the storm. Redwood County opened a church in Sanborn to shelter 10-12 people. ||Farther north and east it was more wooded, so blizzard conditions didn't develop. Roads were still slick, along with dangerous wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: They housed about 50 people at the Olivia Armory who were stranded, and activated National Guard. All total, they had over 170 people and 3 pets sheltered for stranded people motorists. It was an all day event on Friday, and incidents didn���t taper off until the late evening. There were widespread whiteout conditions and over 4 foot drifts in many spots, impassable roads with vehicles stuck in snow drifts in the middle of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the second phase of a winter storm that affected the region in the days leading up to the Christmas holiday. As the area of low pressure began to move away from the region, cold air spilled into the region from Canada. A pressure gradient remained over the region as well that kept winds from diminishing overnight. This led to dangerously cold wind chills as low as -43F across parts of northeastern Minnesota the morning of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills bottomed out at -43F at the Hibbing airport during the early morning hours of the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cyclone with warm air aloft was forecast to bring a wintry mix / ice across western MN, and snow farther north. Areas south and east of the Winter Storm Warning saw less wintry precipitation due to warmer temperatures. Western MN did see some ice, but the main impacts were from the accumulating snow. Several counties had over 6 of snow once it transitioned from the wintry mix. ||Snowfall observations from this event ranged between 4-7 inches across the warned area.  A few of the higher snowfall totals included 6.5 to 7 inches just South of Mora (Kanabec), Long Prairie (Todd), and St. Cloud (Stearns). KAXN ASOS station in Douglas County also recorded a 41 mph wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow (4 to 6) were observed in the county, with 6 to 7 inches just to the west. Along the U.S. Hwy 10 corridor, there were a few observations of 5 inches. Prior to the snow, there was a wintry mix. The combination of a wintry mix followed by several inches of snow verifies the winter storm warning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band a very heavy snow driven by a TROWAL (TRough of Warm Air aLoft) tracked along I-90 and produced totals of 6 to 7 inches in southern Minnesota. Areas near Sioux Falls reported thunder snow. A Winter Weather Advisory was in effect for 4 to 6 inches of snow.||A majority of the snow that fell measured between 2-6 inches overall however the highest snowfall was 7 inches near Winnebago, MN in Faribault County (15 mi NW of Fairmont, MN).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS observations reported 2-6 inches across Waseca County. 2-4 inches fell across the northern portion of the county and 4-6 inches in the southern portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid air temperatures below zero combined with very strong northwesterly wind gusts from 40 to 60 mph to result in a prolonged period of life-threatening wind chills. The entire region exceeded Wind Chill Warning criteria for multiple days and most locations bottomed out between 45 below and 55 below zero. Numerous reports were received of frozen pipes and water-damaged or flooded structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -46F 5 miles south of Ivanhoe (personal weather station site AT653).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by rapid intensification of a low-pressure system over the Great Lakes led to a multi-day winter storm for much of Minnesota. Part 1 of this system was widespread fluffy snow with high snow-to-liquid ratios from southwest through east central Minnesota. There was a break, and then part 2 of this system followed as strong arctic winds developed in wrap-around circulation of the low-pressure system. Wind gusts of 35-50 mph, with the highest speeds in western and southern Minnesota. These winds together with the arctic air and fresh snow led to dangerous wind-chills near -25 to -35��F and blizzard conditions across the open terrain of the Minnesota River Valley. Roads became impassable, and the National Guard was called upon to help rescue folks. Farther north and east it was more wooded, so blizzard conditions didn't develop. Roads were still slick, along with dangerous wind chills.||The highest amounts of observed snowfall appeared to surround eastern MN and western WI. More specifically the Andover and Ham Lake (Andover) in 10.8 inches. Northern Dakota County 8-9 inches near Lakeville and Rosemont. Farther west, Scott County observed 7 to 9 inches in the southeastern part of the county between New Prague and Lakeville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of 8 to 10 inches across the county within a 12-hour period. Highest snowfall observed were near Coon Rapids, MN (9.7 inches) and Ham Lake, MN (10.8 inches). This verifies the winter storm warning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow moved across southeast Minnesota on December 8th and 9th. This was the first heavy snowfall of the season. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 10 inches in parts of Mower and Fillmore Counties. The highest reported totals were 10.3 inches in Dexter (Mower County) and 9.5 inches in Preston (Fillmore County). Several schools across Mower county canceled classes or had a late start on December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 4 to 10 inches of snow fell across Mower County. The highest reported total was 10.3 inches in Dexter.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 3 to 9 inches of snow fell across Fillmore County. The highest reported total was 9.5 inches in Preston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls impacted portions of the North Shore through the I-35 corridor in northeast Minnesota on December 16, 2022. A slow moving low pressure system was in place across eastern Minnesota and northwest Wisconsin. Low level instability and moisture supported cellular snow squall development from the late morning through the early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow Squall. Visibilities quickly dropped to around 1/4 mile or less as the squall passed through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the second phase of a winter storm that affected the region in the days leading up to the Christmas holiday. As the area of low pressure began to move away from the region, cold air spilled into the region from Canada. A pressure gradient remained over the region as well that kept winds from diminishing overnight. This led to dangerously cold wind chills as low as -43F across parts of northeastern Minnesota the morning of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds chills fell to as low as -43F at the Duluth airport during the early morning hours of the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The final phase of the storm during the days ahead of the Christmas holiday featured blizzard conditions along the North Shore. A second low developed over the Ohio Valley and moved into the region as it deepened. This led to strong northwesterly winds along the North Shore with gusts of 50 to 70 mph being common from the Twin Ports to Grand Portage. These winds lofted the snow from earlier in this system, along with some light new snow, and created blizzard conditions from Silver Bay northward along the shore. A maximum gust of 82 mph was recorded just offshore by a passing lake freighter near Grand Marais during the height of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were reported for a period of seven hours at the Cook County airport up the hill from Grand Marais.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cyclone with warm air aloft was forecast to bring a wintry mix / ice across western MN, and snow farther north. Areas south and east of the Winter Storm Warning saw less wintry precipitation due to warmer temperatures. Western MN did see some ice, but the main impacts were from the accumulating snow. Several counties had over 6 of snow once it transitioned from the wintry mix. ||Snowfall observations from this event ranged between 4-7 inches across the warned area.  A few of the higher snowfall totals included 6.5 to 7 inches just South of Mora (Kanabec), Long Prairie (Todd), and St. Cloud (Stearns). KAXN ASOS station in Douglas County also recorded a 41 mph wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow (4 to 5) were observed just to the east and just to the north of the county line. In addition, the ASOS at KAXN measured 41 mph wind gusts. The combination of a wintry mix followed by several inches of snow and strong winds verifies the winter storm warning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frigid air temperatures below zero combined with very strong northwesterly wind gusts from 40 to 60 mph to result in a prolonged period of life-threatening wind chills. The entire region exceeded Wind Chill Warning criteria for multiple days and most locations bottomed out between 45 below and 55 below zero. Numerous reports were received of frozen pipes and water-damaged or flooded structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills dropped as low as -50F at Florence (Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN160) and -47F at Marshall (AWOS site KMML) and Tracy (AWOS site KTKC).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first phase of a large and long-duration winter storm brought strong warming aloft atop a deep cold layer. With areas of freezing rain and drizzle, significant ice accumulations spread across portions of southwest Minnesota. Spotty minor power outages and broken tree limbs were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle and light freezing rain accumulated to around three-quarter inch on elevated and road surfaces at Trosky and one-quarter inch near Edgerton. Slick conditions resulted in several accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure brought strong winds and periods of snow and heavy snow over a 4 plus day period. The bulk of the snow across west central Minnesota fell from late on December 14th through the evening of December 16th, with some of the highest totals including 14.0 inches one mile east of Artichoke Lake, 9.3 inches at Browns Valley, 8.4 inches at Wheaton, and 6 inches 1 mile north of Ortonville.||This storm was severe, widespread and prolonged in nature, lasting from December 12th-16th across the region. Impacts included numerous No Travel Advised or road closures by the Minnesota Department of Transportation, numerous vehicle accidents, and widespread school cancellations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour total of 7.2 inches of snow fell 2 miles ESE of Artichoke from 6pm on the 14th to 6pm on the 15th. The grand total by the 17th was 14.0 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulated to around one-tenth of an inch on elevated and road surfaces at Jackson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by rapid intensification of a low-pressure system over the Great Lakes led to a multi-day winter storm for much of Minnesota. Part 1 of this system was widespread fluffy snow with high snow-to-liquid ratios from southwest through east central Minnesota. There was a break, and then part 2 of this system followed as strong arctic winds developed in wrap-around circulation of the low-pressure system. Wind gusts of 35-50 mph, with the highest speeds in western and southern Minnesota. These winds together with the arctic air and fresh snow led to dangerous wind-chills near -25 to -35��F and blizzard conditions across the open terrain of the Minnesota River Valley. Roads became impassable, and the National Guard was called upon to help rescue folks. Farther north and east it was more wooded, so blizzard conditions didn't develop. Roads were still slick, along with dangerous wind chills.||The highest amounts of observed snowfall appeared to surround eastern MN and western WI. More specifically the Andover and Ham Lake (Andover) in 10.8 inches. Northern Dakota County 8-9 inches near Lakeville and Rosemont. Farther west, Scott County observed 7 to 9 inches in the southeastern part of the county between New Prague and Lakeville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of 6 to 8 inches across the county within a 12 hour period. This verifies the winter storm warning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a wintry mix of precipitation on December 12 and a warmup to allow mainly rainfall on December 13, temperatures fell with a change to snow. Snow and stronger winds began earlier in the day December 14 west of the James River, with the transition to snow mainly later into the early morning hours of December 15 toward Interstate 29. With northwest winds gusting at times from 40 to 55 mph, several areas exhibited periods of blizzard conditions on the 15th and early on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall occurred from the James River Valley westward, where up to 10 to 17 inches was common. Amounts decreased toward the south and east, with less than 3 inches in far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed early on December 15, with strong and gusty winds developing shortly after this time. Consistent visibility of one-quarter mile occurred from late morning through the afternoon, with a peak wind gust reaching 41 mph at the Automated Weather Observing System site KLYV near Luverne. Snowfall totaled 2.0 inches at Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Arctic air masses crossed the region over a period of 7 days beginning on Sunday, December 19th. Temperatures failed to even reach 5 degrees above zero during this period through December 26th, with lows consistently dropping into the teens and 20s below zero. An unusually potent blast of cold air for December followed in behind a reinforcing Arctic front Tuesday night, December 20th, into Wednesday, December 21st, along with a trace to as much as 2 to 4 of new snowfall on top of the pre-existing snow pack. Wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph behind this front impacted the region from the 21st through the 23rd, resulting in an extended period of life-threatening wind chills in the -35 to -50 degree F range and near blizzard conditions. The coldest measured wind chills include -48 F at Graceville, -47 F at Wheaton, -45 F at Ortonville (all on the 22nd). ||The extreme cold made the threat to stranded motorists even more dangerous, as numerous roads were deemed No Travel Advised or closed by the Minnesota Department of Transportation. Numerous vehicle accidents occurred, and numerous schools closed throughout the event.

A powerful, long duration winter storm brought widespread snowfall amounts of 8 to 24 inches across parts of northeast Minnesota and northwest Wisconsin, with areas around Lake Superior receiving up to 30 inches of snow. A wintry mixture of sleet, freezing rain and snow began Tuesday (December 13th) afternoon and evening, spreading northeast overnight Tuesday night and into Wednesday (December 14th) morning. This first wave of precipitation brought a widespread 6 to 12 inches of snow.  We had a break in the snowfall during the day on Wednesday, but it started up again Wednesday night and continued into Thursday (December 15th), bringing another 8-20 inches of snow.  This second wave of precipitation was nearly all snow, enhanced by the main upper level low pressure system as it moved close to the area.  Lighter snow lingered into Friday and Saturday, December 16th and 17th.  A low pressure system developed over eastern Colorado early Tuesday, moved northeast to eastern Nebraska by Wednesday morning, then to east-central Minnesota by Thursday morning, lingering there through Friday before moving off to the northeast Friday night and Saturday. ||The storm resulted in a long period of poor road conditions, with strong winds causing blowing and drifting snow. There were also widespread power outages, with some people reporting no power for over two days!||Notable with this storm was how wet and heavy the snow was, with 3-day liquid equivalent values of 1 to 4 inches. The wet quality of this snow, unusual for winter storms in this area, heavily weighed down trees and power lines. The density of the snowpack also exacerbated the quality of snow to absorb the red end of the visible light spectrum, transmitting the blue end, which led to many folks observing a glacier blue color in holes and cracks in the snow as they cleared it. Because of this blue color, as well as the very wet quality of the snowfall, NWS Duluth has taken to referring to this snow as the Blue Blizzard of 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 8 to 10 inches were reported across Koochiching County with a high report of 10.5 inches near International Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air began moving across the Upper Midwest on Dec 21-22 following the snow. Winds increased from west to east across the forecast area, creating dangerously cold wind chills of -30 to -48��F.||The coldest wind chill temperatures favored Western Minnesota given the physical geography of the land and lack of friction of surrounding structures. Morris Municipal Airport in Stevens County and Benson Municipal Airport in Swift County recorded the lowest observed wind chills at -48��. Glenwood Airport in Pope County recorded -45�� as well as St. James Municipal Airport in Watonwan County. The third lowest wind chill (-44��) was observed at the Olivia Regional Airport in Renville County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many hours with wind chill ranging from -41�� to -35��. The strongest winds were recorded at 2:33 PM with a gust at 31 mph at the Degner Municipal Airport in Owatonna, MN. The ASOS site did not record any data from 12/23 to 12/29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by rapid intensification of a low-pressure system over the Great Lakes led to a multi-day winter storm for much of Minnesota. Part 1 of this system was widespread fluffy snow with high snow-to-liquid ratios from southwest through east central Minnesota. ||There was a break, and then part 2 of this system followed as strong arctic winds developed in wrap-around circulation of the low-pressure system. Wind gusts of 35-50 mph, with the highest amounts in western and southern Minnesota. These winds together with the arctic air and fresh snow led to dangerous wind-chills of -25 to -35��F and blizzard conditions across the open terrain of the Minnesota River Valley. Roads became impassable due to reports of snow drifts measuring 5 to 10 feet tall and nearly 40 feet long in some areas and zero visibility in multiple counties across Western MN. Whiteout conditions forced many roads to close. Highway 71 in Redwood County remained closed for approximately 36 hours after the storm had passed. The National Guard was called upon to perform rescue missions stranded motorists Kandiyohi and Renville Counties. One Kandiyohi County rescue case involved a stranded motorist who was diagnosed with critical hypothermia after being exposed in dangerous conditions for nearly nine hours. ||Rescued motorists were housed in various locations for their safety as the storm passed. In Renville County, approximately 50 people were housed at the Olivia Armory in Oliva, MN. Watonwan County opened emergency shelters in Medelia, MN and Saint James, MN. In Martin County, the Salvation Army provided hotel vouchers prior to the event for anyone in need to seek shelter during the storm. Redwood County opened a church in Sanborn to shelter 10-12 people. ||Farther north and east it was more wooded, so blizzard conditions didn't develop. Roads were still slick, along with dangerous wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many state and county highways were impassable, and vehicles became stranded and had to be rescued. Some people had to shelter in place if they had enough fuel. About 10 folks had to spend the night in the firehall. A lot of the people that got stuck were not from the county, but were taking part in the Dakota 38+2 commemorative ride.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band a very heavy snow driven by a TROWAL (TRough of Warm Air aLoft) tracked along I-90 and produced totals of 6 to 7 inches in southern Minnesota. Areas near Sioux Falls reported thunder snow. A Winter Weather Advisory was in effect for 4 to 6 inches of snow.||A majority of the snow that fell measured between 2-6 inches overall however the highest snowfall was 7 inches near Winnebago, MN in Faribault County (15 mi NW of Fairmont, MN).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extrapolation from observations in neighboring counties supports 4-7 of snow, with the highest amounts across the southern part of the county. CoCoRaHS report of 4 inches fell in central Martin County near Fairmont, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Long-term drought status was largely unchanged with frozen ground conditions, despite up to double to triple normal precipitation across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions persisted through December. Monthly precipitation at Worthington was around 250 percent of normal, much in the form of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought improved to severe (D2) by late December. Monthly precipitation at Lake Wilson was around 150 percent of normal, much in the form of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought improved to severe (D2) by late December. Monthly precipitation at Pipestone was around 200 percent of normal, much in the form of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by rapid intensification of a low-pressure system over the Great Lakes led to a multi-day winter storm for much of Minnesota. Part 1 of this system was widespread fluffy snow with high snow-to-liquid ratios from southwest through east central Minnesota. There was a break, and then part 2 of this system followed as strong arctic winds developed in wrap-around circulation of the low-pressure system. Wind gusts of 35-50 mph, with the highest speeds in western and southern Minnesota. These winds together with the arctic air and fresh snow led to dangerous wind-chills near -25 to -35��F and blizzard conditions across the open terrain of the Minnesota River Valley. Roads became impassable, and the National Guard was called upon to help rescue folks. Farther north and east it was more wooded, so blizzard conditions didn't develop. Roads were still slick, along with dangerous wind chills.||The highest amounts of observed snowfall appeared to surround eastern MN and western WI. More specifically the Andover and Ham Lake (Andover) in 10.8 inches. Northern Dakota County 8-9 inches near Lakeville and Rosemont. Farther west, Scott County observed 7 to 9 inches in the southeastern part of the county between New Prague and Lakeville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of 6 to 8 inches across the county within a 12 hour period. This verifies the winter storm warning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a wintry mix of precipitation on December 12 and a warmup to allow mainly rainfall on December 13, temperatures fell with a change to snow. Snow and stronger winds began earlier in the day December 14 west of the James River, with the transition to snow mainly later into the early morning hours of December 15 toward Interstate 29. With northwest winds gusting at times from 40 to 55 mph, several areas exhibited periods of blizzard conditions on the 15th and early on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall occurred from the James River Valley westward, where up to 10 to 17 inches was common. Amounts decreased toward the south and east, with less than 3 inches in far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall resulted in accumulations of 7.0 inches 8 miles southeast of Hendricks. With the addition of winds which gusted to 44 mph at Marshall (Automated Weather Observing System site KMML) and 31 mph at Florence (Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN030), visibility briefly dropped to one-quarter to one-half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful, long duration winter storm brought widespread snowfall amounts of 8 to 24 inches across parts of northeast Minnesota and northwest Wisconsin, with areas around Lake Superior receiving up to 30 inches of snow. A wintry mixture of sleet, freezing rain and snow began Tuesday (December 13th) afternoon and evening, spreading northeast overnight Tuesday night and into Wednesday (December 14th) morning. This first wave of precipitation brought a widespread 6 to 12 inches of snow.  We had a break in the snowfall during the day on Wednesday, but it started up again Wednesday night and continued into Thursday (December 15th), bringing another 8-20 inches of snow.  This second wave of precipitation was nearly all snow, enhanced by the main upper level low pressure system as it moved close to the area.  Lighter snow lingered into Friday and Saturday, December 16th and 17th.  A low pressure system developed over eastern Colorado early Tuesday, moved northeast to eastern Nebraska by Wednesday morning, then to east-central Minnesota by Thursday morning, lingering there through Friday before moving off to the northeast Friday night and Saturday. ||The storm resulted in a long period of poor road conditions, with strong winds causing blowing and drifting snow. There were also widespread power outages, with some people reporting no power for over two days!||Notable with this storm was how wet and heavy the snow was, with 3-day liquid equivalent values of 1 to 4 inches. The wet quality of this snow, unusual for winter storms in this area, heavily weighed down trees and power lines. The density of the snowpack also exacerbated the quality of snow to absorb the red end of the visible light spectrum, transmitting the blue end, which led to many folks observing a glacier blue color in holes and cracks in the snow as they cleared it. Because of this blue color, as well as the very wet quality of the snowfall, NWS Duluth has taken to referring to this snow as the Blue Blizzard of 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals of 10 to 15 inches were recorded across the southern part of Itasca County by the time the storm ended on the 15th. The highest report was 18 inches outside of Grand Rapids.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air began moving across the Upper Midwest on Dec 21-22 following the snow. Winds increased from west to east across the forecast area, creating dangerously cold wind chills of -30 to -48��F.||The coldest wind chill temperatures favored Western Minnesota given the physical geography of the land and lack of friction of surrounding structures. Morris Municipal Airport in Stevens County and Benson Municipal Airport in Swift County recorded the lowest observed wind chills at -48��. Glenwood Airport in Pope County recorded -45�� as well as St. James Municipal Airport in Watonwan County. The third lowest wind chill (-44��) was observed at the Olivia Regional Airport in Renville County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Airfield stations in neighboring counties recorded wind-chill temperatures below -42�� to -35�� for numerous hours. Strongest winds occurred in the late morning at 40-45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper moved across the region during the evening and overnight hours of the 19th and into the 20th. While most of the region received 2-5 inches of accumulation, lake enhancement and orographic lift along the North Shore led to widespread totals of aound 7 inches in southern Cook County. The highest total recorded was 7.8 inches near Grand Marais.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals around 7 inches were seen near Lake Superior as a clipper system with some lake enhancement moved across the area. A high report of 7.8 inches was recorded near Grand Marais.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by rapid intensification of a low-pressure system over the Great Lakes led to a multi-day winter storm for much of Minnesota. Part 1 of this system was widespread fluffy snow with high snow-to-liquid ratios from southwest through east central Minnesota. ||There was a break, and then part 2 of this system followed as strong arctic winds developed in wrap-around circulation of the low-pressure system. Wind gusts of 35-50 mph, with the highest amounts in western and southern Minnesota. These winds together with the arctic air and fresh snow led to dangerous wind-chills of -25 to -35��F and blizzard conditions across the open terrain of the Minnesota River Valley. Roads became impassable due to reports of snow drifts measuring 5 to 10 feet tall and nearly 40 feet long in some areas and zero visibility in multiple counties across Western MN. Whiteout conditions forced many roads to close. Highway 71 in Redwood County remained closed for approximately 36 hours after the storm had passed. The National Guard was called upon to perform rescue missions stranded motorists Kandiyohi and Renville Counties. One Kandiyohi County rescue case involved a stranded motorist who was diagnosed with critical hypothermia after being exposed in dangerous conditions for nearly nine hours. ||Rescued motorists were housed in various locations for their safety as the storm passed. In Renville County, approximately 50 people were housed at the Olivia Armory in Oliva, MN. Watonwan County opened emergency shelters in Medelia, MN and Saint James, MN. In Martin County, the Salvation Army provided hotel vouchers prior to the event for anyone in need to seek shelter during the storm. Redwood County opened a church in Sanborn to shelter 10-12 people. ||Farther north and east it was more wooded, so blizzard conditions didn't develop. Roads were still slick, along with dangerous wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were closed and impassable. Friday night was the worst, very windy and cold with whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band a very heavy snow driven by a TROWAL (TRough of Warm Air aLoft) tracked along I-90 and produced totals of 6 to 7 inches in southern Minnesota. Areas near Sioux Falls reported thunder snow. A Winter Weather Advisory was in effect for 4 to 6 inches of snow.||A majority of the snow that fell measured between 2-6 inches overall however the highest snowfall was 7 inches near Winnebago, MN in Faribault County (15 mi NW of Fairmont, MN).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: CoCoRaHS reports of 5-6 inches in Freeborn County. Albert Lea, MN observed 5 inches. Meanwhile just to the east of the county border, 6 to 7 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by rapid intensification of a low-pressure system over the Great Lakes led to a multi-day winter storm for much of Minnesota. Part 1 of this system was widespread fluffy snow with high snow-to-liquid ratios from southwest through east central Minnesota. ||There was a break, and then part 2 of this system followed as strong arctic winds developed in wrap-around circulation of the low-pressure system. Wind gusts of 35-50 mph, with the highest amounts in western and southern Minnesota. These winds together with the arctic air and fresh snow led to dangerous wind-chills of -25 to -35��F and blizzard conditions across the open terrain of the Minnesota River Valley. Roads became impassable due to reports of snow drifts measuring 5 to 10 feet tall and nearly 40 feet long in some areas and zero visibility in multiple counties across Western MN. Whiteout conditions forced many roads to close. Highway 71 in Redwood County remained closed for approximately 36 hours after the storm had passed. The National Guard was called upon to perform rescue missions stranded motorists Kandiyohi and Renville Counties. In Renville County, approximately 50 people were housed at the Olivia Armory in Oliva, MN. Watonwan County opened emergency shelters in Medelia, MN and Saint James, MN. In Martin County, the Salvation Army provided hotel vouchers prior to the event for anyone in need to seek shelter during the storm. Redwood County opened a church in Sanborn to shelter 10-12 people. ||Farther north and east it was more wooded, so blizzard conditions didn't develop. Roads were still slick, along with dangerous wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: They had large snow drifts about 5 feet tall. They sheltered 70 people overnight in the national guard armory. Performed 25 rescue missions (national guard 13). Most locations were in and out of whiteout conditions once the winds picked up on Friday. The worst conditions were Friday Night. They were busy all night. People would come on the interstates and try to take an alternate route and then they���d get stranded. One vehicle had 7 people in the car coming back from Florida, including an 11 month old baby, that became stuck in a drift without heat.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a wintry mix of precipitation on December 12 and a warmup to allow mainly rainfall on December 13, temperatures fell with a change to snow. Snow and stronger winds began earlier in the day December 14 west of the James River, with the transition to snow mainly later into the early morning hours of December 15 toward Interstate 29. With northwest winds gusting at times from 40 to 55 mph, several areas exhibited periods of blizzard conditions on the 15th and early on the 16th. The heaviest snowfall occurred from the James River Valley westward, where up to 10 to 17 inches was common. Amounts decreased toward the south and east, with less than 3 inches in far southeast South Dakota, northeast Nebraska and northwest Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall resulted in accumulations of 4.3 inches at Windom. With the addition of winds which gusted to 39 mph at Windom (Automated Weather Observing System site KMWM) and 44 mph 4 miles east-northeast of Jeffers (Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN071), visibility briefly dropped to one-quarter to one-half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought an extended period of hazardous winter weather to southeast Minnesota ahead of the Christmas holiday. Periods of snow fell from December 21st into the 23rd. Most accumulations were in the 3 to 5 inch range but combined with winds of 35 to 50 mph, significant blowing and drifting resulted. It even resulted in some whiteout conditions. The wind also combined with an influx of arctic air to create dangerously cold wind chills from the 22nd into Christmas morning. Values of 25 to 45 below zero were common during this period with the lowest calculated wind chill of -47 at a mesonet site near Eyota (Olmsted County). Numerous schools closed in advance of the storm. Deliveries of fuel were delayed causing some gas stations to run out of fuel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported around 3 inches of snow across Dodge County. The highest reported total was 3 inches in and around Kasson. Strong winds with gusts of 25 to 45 mph created blowing and drifting snow with some occasional whiteouts. The highest record gust was 47 mph at a mesonet site near Byron.

However, around an inch of liquid equivalent moisture fell during a large storm system from December 12-16th, which improved conditions back to Moderate/D1 by the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought an extended period of hazardous winter weather to southeast Minnesota ahead of the Christmas holiday. Periods of snow fell from December 21st into the 23rd. Most accumulations were in the 3 to 5 inch range but combined with winds of 35 to 50 mph, significant blowing and drifting resulted. It even resulted in some whiteout conditions. The wind also combined with an influx of arctic air to create dangerously cold wind chills from the 22nd into Christmas morning. Values of 25 to 45 below zero were common during this period with the lowest calculated wind chill of -47 at a mesonet site near Eyota (Olmsted County). Numerous schools closed in advance of the storm. Deliveries of fuel were delayed causing some gas stations to run out of fuel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 2 to 3 inches of snow across Fillmore County. The highest reported total was 2.8 inches in Mabel. Strong winds with gusts of 25 to 40 mph created blowing and drifting snow with some occasional whiteouts. The highest record gust was 39 mph at the Preston airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air began moving across the Upper Midwest on Dec 21-22 following the snow. Winds increased from west to east across the forecast area, creating dangerously cold wind chills of -30 to -48��F.||The coldest wind chill temperatures favored Western Minnesota given the physical geography of the land and lack of friction of surrounding structures. Morris Municipal Airport in Stevens County and Benson Municipal Airport in Swift County recorded the lowest observed wind chills at -48��. Glenwood Airport in Pope County recorded -45�� as well as St. James Municipal Airport in Watonwan County. The third lowest wind chill (-44��) was observed at the Olivia Regional Airport in Renville County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: AWOS station at the Morrison County Airport in Little Falls, MN recorded fluctuating wind-chill temperatures below -35�� for 3 hours. Strongest winds occurred in the early afternoon between 30-35 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a 2 to 5 inch fluffy snow accumulation across most of southwest Minnesota on December 21 and 22, a strong Arctic front surged through the area with strong and gusty northwest winds from 35 to 55 mph, creating widespread whiteout conditions. All state, county, and federal highways were either impassable or with travel not recommended.  Drifts as high as 5 to 10 feet were documented through social media. Numerous accidents and stranded vehicles complicated recovery efforts. School, county, and state operations were cancelled for several days when conditions were coupled with life-threatening wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted to 45 mph at Minnesota Road Weather Information site MN008 near Lakefield, resulting in frequent whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 6.0 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 0.2 miles northwest of Hills.

Part 1 of this system was widespread fluffy snow with high snow-to-liquid ratios from southwest through east central Minnesota. There was a break, and then part 2 of this system followed as strong arctic winds developed in wrap-around circulation of the low-pressure system. Wind gusts of 35-50 mph, with the highest speeds in western and southern Minnesota. These winds together with the arctic air and fresh snow led to dangerous wind-chills near -25 to -35��F and blizzard conditions across the open terrain of the Minnesota River Valley. Roads became impassable, and the National Guard was called upon to help rescue folks. Farther north and east it was more wooded, so blizzard conditions didn't develop. Roads were still slick, along with dangerous wind chills.||The highest amounts of observed snowfall appeared to surround eastern MN and western WI. More specifically the Andover and Ham Lake (Andover) in 10.8 inches. Northern Dakota County 8-9 inches near Lakeville and Rosemont. Farther west, Scott County observed 7 to 9 inches in the southeastern part of the county between New Prague and Lakeville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of 5 to 7 inches across the county within a 12 hour period. This verifies the winter storm warning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a 2 to 5 inch fluffy snow accumulation across most of southwest Minnesota on December 21 and 22, a strong Arctic front surged through the area with strong and gusty northwest winds from 35 to 55 mph, creating widespread whiteout conditions. All state, county, and federal highways were either impassable or with travel not recommended.  Drifts as high as 5 to 10 feet were documented through social media. Numerous accidents and stranded vehicles complicated recovery efforts. School, county, and state operations were cancelled for several days when conditions were coupled with life-threatening wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds gusted to 48 mph at Automated Weather Observing System site KTKC at Tracy, 47 mph at KMML, and 46 mph at Minnesota Road Weather Information System site MN156 near Taunton, resulting in frequent whiteout conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought an extended period of hazardous winter weather to southeast Minnesota ahead of the Christmas holiday. Periods of snow fell from December 21st into the 23rd. Most accumulations were in the 3 to 5 inch range but combined with winds of 35 to 50 mph, significant blowing and drifting resulted. It even resulted in some whiteout conditions. The wind also combined with an influx of arctic air to create dangerously cold wind chills from the 22nd into Christmas morning. Values of 25 to 45 below zero were common during this period with the lowest calculated wind chill of -47 at a mesonet site near Eyota (Olmsted County). Numerous schools closed in advance of the storm. Deliveries of fuel were delayed causing some gas stations to run out of fuel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 4 to 5 inches of snow across Wabasha County. The highest reported total was 5 inches in Lake City. Strong winds with gusts of 25 to 40 mph created blowing and drifting snow with some occasional whiteouts. The highest record gust was 42 mph at a mesonet site near Lake City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 6.8 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 1.8 miles northwest of Bigelow and 4.0 inches at the COOP site 2 miles north-northeast of Worthington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by rapid intensification of a low-pressure system over the Great Lakes led to a multi-day winter storm for much of Minnesota. Wind gusts of 35-50 mph, with the highest amounts in western and southern Minnesota. These winds together with the arctic air and fresh snow led to dangerous wind-chills of -25 to -35��F and blizzard conditions across the open terrain of the Minnesota River Valley. Roads became impassable due to reports of snow drifts measuring 5 to 10 feet tall and nearly 40 feet long in some areas and zero visibility in multiple counties across Western MN. Whiteout conditions forced many roads to close. Highway 71 in Redwood County remained closed for approximately 36 hours after the storm had passed. The National Guard was called upon to perform rescue missions stranded motorists Kandiyohi and Renville Counties. In Renville County, approximately 50 people were housed at the Olivia Armory in Oliva, MN. Watonwan County opened emergency shelters in Medelia, MN and Saint James, MN. In Martin County, the Salvation Army provided hotel vouchers prior to the event for anyone in need to seek shelter during the storm. Redwood County opened a church in Sanborn to shelter 10-12 people. ||Farther north and east it was more wooded, so blizzard conditions didn't develop. Roads were still slick, along with dangerous wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stevens County sustained blizzard conditions. No main county roads closed but secondary roads were closed with snow drifts around 6 to 8 feet deep reported by a storm spotter. Law enforcement reported a few rescues from people going into the ditch. Morris airport gusted to 47 mph and reported zero visibility.

The third lowest wind chill (-44��) was observed at the Olivia Regional Airport in Renville County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many hours with wind chill ranging from -43�� to -35�� observed at KAXN. The strongest winds were recorded at 11:00 AM with a gust at 46 mph.

Wind gusts of 35-50 mph, with the highest speeds in western and southern Minnesota. These winds together with the arctic air and fresh snow led to dangerous wind-chills near -25 to -35��F and blizzard conditions across the open terrain of the Minnesota River Valley. Roads became impassable, and the National Guard was called upon to help rescue folks. Farther north and east it was more wooded, so blizzard conditions didn't develop. Roads were still slick, along with dangerous wind chills.||The highest amounts of observed snowfall appeared to surround eastern MN and western WI. More specifically the Andover and Ham Lake (Andover) in 10.8 inches. Northern Dakota County 8-9 inches near Lakeville and Rosemont. Farther west, Scott County observed 7 to 9 inches in the southeastern part of the county between New Prague and Lakeville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of 5 to 7 inches across the county within a 12 hour period. This verifies the winter storm warning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm brought an extended period of hazardous winter weather to southeast Minnesota ahead of the Christmas holiday. Periods of snow fell from December 21st into the 23rd. Most accumulations were in the 3 to 5 inch range but combined with winds of 35 to 50 mph, significant blowing and drifting resulted. It even resulted in some whiteout conditions. The wind also combined with an influx of arctic air to create dangerously cold wind chills from the 22nd into Christmas morning. Values of 25 to 45 below zero were common during this period with the lowest calculated wind chill of -47 at a mesonet site near Eyota (Olmsted County). Numerous schools closed in advance of the storm. Deliveries of fuel were delayed causing some gas stations to run out of fuel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of arctic air and wind gusts of 25 to 45 mph created wind chills of 20 to 45 below zero across Olmsted County. The lowest calculated wind chill was -47 near Eyota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm resulted in warm-air advection precipitation lifting from south to north across northeast Nebraska, northwest Iowa, southeast South Dakota, and southwest Minnesota during the afternoon and evening of December 8th and continuing into the early morning hours of December 9th. Initially, the precipitation fell as light freezing drizzle or rain, but quickly transitioned to snow. While light to moderate snow persisted for much of the evening, heavy snow fell in a narrow convective-like band where rates exceeded 2 to 3 inches per hour. Thunder-snow was also observed with very large (plate-like) aggregates. Within this band, which stretched from near Mitchell to Sioux Falls to Spencer, snowfall amounts exceeded 6 inches with some isolated areas picking up 9 to 10 inches. Light winds persisted throughout the event, so reductions in visibility were related to the snowfall rate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain or drizzle resulted in a glaze of ice. Precipitation then quickly transitioned to snow, accumulating to 4.5 inches at the CoCoRaHS site 0.2 miles west-southwest of Edgerton and 3.0 inches at the COOP site in Pipestone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by rapid intensification of a low-pressure system over the Great Lakes led to a multi-day winter storm for much of Minnesota. Part 1 of this system was widespread fluffy snow with high snow-to-liquid ratios from southwest through east central Minnesota. ||There was a break, and then part 2 of this system followed as strong arctic winds developed in wrap-around circulation of the low-pressure system. Wind gusts of 35-50 mph, with the highest amounts in western and southern Minnesota. These winds together with the arctic air and fresh snow led to dangerous wind-chills of -25 to -35��F and blizzard conditions across the open terrain of the Minnesota River Valley. Roads became impassable due to reports of snow drifts measuring 5 to 10 feet tall and nearly 40 feet long in some areas and zero visibility in multiple counties across Western MN. Whiteout conditions forced many roads to close. Highway 71 in Redwood County remained closed for approximately 36 hours after the storm had passed. The National Guard was called upon to perform rescue missions stranded motorists Kandiyohi and Renville Counties. One Kandiyohi County rescue case involved a stranded motorist who was diagnosed with critical hypothermia after being exposed in dangerous conditions for nearly nine hours. ||Rescued motorists were housed in various locations for their safety as the storm passed. In Renville County, approximately 50 people were housed at the Olivia Armory in Oliva, MN. Watonwan County opened emergency shelters in Medelia, MN and Saint James, MN. In Martin County, the Salvation Army provided hotel vouchers prior to the event for anyone in need to seek shelter during the storm. Redwood County opened a church in Sanborn to shelter 10-12 people. ||Farther north and east it was more wooded, so blizzard conditions didn't develop. Roads were still slick, along with dangerous wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All county and state roads were closed due to blizzard conditions. I-90 was closed more for safety due to reduced visibility. Snow drifts were 2 to 3 feet deep, and one family was rescued and transported to a hotel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure moved across the Upper Midwest in the days leading up to the Christmas holiday, bringing a storm with three distinct phases. The first phase occurred on the 21-22nd with widespread snowfall across the region of 3 to 7 inches. Higher amounts to around a foot were recorded along the North Shore in southern Cook County due to lake enhancement and orographic effects. Later phases of the storm included dangerous wind chills and blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals varied greatly across the southern part of the county with reports ranging from 3 to 11 inches. A high report of 12 inches was reported near Tofte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air began moving across the Upper Midwest on Dec 21-22 following the snow. Winds increased from west to east across the forecast area, creating dangerously cold wind chills of -30 to -48��F.||The coldest wind chill temperatures favored Western Minnesota given the physical geography of the land and lack of friction of surrounding structures. Morris Municipal Airport in Stevens County and Benson Municipal Airport in Swift County recorded the lowest observed wind chills at -48��. Glenwood Airport in Pope County recorded -45�� as well as St. James Municipal Airport in Watonwan County. The third lowest wind chill (-44��) was observed at the Olivia Regional Airport in Renville County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many hours with wind chill ranging from -44�� to -35��. The strongest winds were recorded at 12:35 PM on 12/23 with a gust at 51 mph at Olivia Regional Airport in Olivia, MN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow followed by rapid intensification of a low-pressure system over the Great Lakes led to a multi-day winter storm for much of Minnesota. Part 1 of this system was widespread fluffy snow with high snow-to-liquid ratios from southwest through east central Minnesota. ||There was a break, and then part 2 of this system followed as strong arctic winds developed in wrap-around circulation of the low-pressure system. Wind gusts of 35-50 mph, with the highest amounts in western and southern Minnesota. These winds together with the arctic air and fresh snow led to dangerous wind-chills of -25 to -35��F and blizzard conditions across the open terrain of the Minnesota River Valley. Roads became impassable due to reports of snow drifts measuring 5 to 10 feet tall and nearly 40 feet long in some areas and zero visibility in multiple counties across Western MN. Whiteout conditions forced many roads to close. Highway 71 in Redwood County remained closed for approximately 36 hours after the storm had passed. The National Guard was called upon to perform rescue missions stranded motorists Kandiyohi and Renville Counties. One Kandiyohi County rescue case involved a stranded motorist who was diagnosed with critical hypothermia after being exposed in dangerous conditions for nearly nine hours. ||Rescued motorists were housed in various locations for their safety as the storm passed. In Renville County, approximately 50 people were housed at the Olivia Armory in Oliva, MN. Watonwan County opened emergency shelters in Medelia, MN and Saint James, MN. In Martin County, the Salvation Army provided hotel vouchers prior to the event for anyone in need to seek shelter during the storm. Redwood County opened a church in Sanborn to shelter 10-12 people. ||Farther north and east it was more wooded, so blizzard conditions didn't develop. Roads were still slick, along with dangerous wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All state highways were closed and county roads were impassable due to zero visibility and snow drifts 6 to 8 feet tall. Highway 71 was still closed 36 hours later. They opened a shelter at a church in Sanborn for about about 10 to 12 people total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of Arctic air masses crossed the region over a period of 7 days beginning on Sunday, December 19th. Temperatures failed to even reach 5 degrees above zero during this period through December 26th, with lows consistently dropping into the teens and 20s below zero. An unusually potent blast of cold air for December followed in behind a reinforcing Arctic front Tuesday night, December 20th, into Wednesday, December 21st, along with a trace to as much as 2 to 4 of new snowfall on top of the pre-existing snow pack. Wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph behind this front impacted the region from the 21st through the 23rd, resulting in an extended period of life-threatening wind chills in the -35 to -50 degree F range and near blizzard conditions. The coldest measured wind chills include -48 F at Graceville, -47 F at Wheaton, -45 F at Ortonville (all on the 22nd). ||The extreme cold made the threat to stranded motorists even more dangerous, as numerous roads were deemed No Travel Advised or closed by the Minnesota Department of Transportation. Numerous vehicle accidents occurred, and numerous schools closed throughout the event.
